
## 4.3 Block Architecture — Generator's Electrical Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEa[Generated EMF Eg inside Armature]:::emf
    nodeRa[Armature Resistance Ra]:::res
    nodeIa[Armature Current Ia]:::curr
    nodeRsh[Shunt Field Resistance Rsh]:::res
    nodeIsh[Shunt Field Current Ish]:::curr
    nodeVt[Terminal Voltage Vt]:::vt
    nodeIL[Load Current IL]:::curr
    nodeLB[Load Resistor RL]:::load

    nodeEa --- nodeRa --- nodeVt
    nodeVt --- nodeLB
    nodeVt --- nodeRsh
    nodeRsh --- nodeIa
    nodeVt --- nodeIL

    classDef emf fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef curr fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef vt fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Records:** Always reproduce the **circuit diagram** (not the block diagram) in your record using standard electrical symbols — circle with `+` and `-` for the generator, zig-zag line for resistance, circle with `A` for ammeter, and circle with `V` for voltmeter. The block diagrams above are for **conceptual understanding only** — they will **not** be accepted as a substitute for the circuit diagram in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO2]`
**Why is the external characteristic of a DC shunt generator a drooping curve?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The external characteristic of a DC shunt generator plots terminal voltage $V_t$ against load current $I_L$. As $I_L$ increases, the terminal voltage drops due to **two simultaneous effects**: (1) the **armature resistance drop** $I_a R_a$, which directly subtracts from the generated EMF, and (2) the **armature reaction**, which distorts and weakens the main field flux $\Phi$, thereby reducing the generated EMF $E_g = k\Phi N$. Since both effects increase with load, the $V_t$ vs $I_L$ curve is **monotonically drooping**, making the shunt generator suitable for applications requiring approximately constant voltage (1 mark for each cause + 1 mark for conclusion).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, CO3]`
**Define voltage regulation of a DC generator and state its typical value for a shunt generator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Voltage regulation is defined as the **change in terminal voltage when the load is removed from full load to no load, expressed as a percentage of the full-load voltage** (1 mark):

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

For a DC **shunt generator**, the regulation is **positive and typically between 5% and 15%** (1 mark). It is always positive because $V_{NL} > V_{FL}$ due to armature reaction and ohmic drop. This distinguishes the shunt generator from a series generator (negative regulation) and a compound generator (can be differentially compounded to give near-zero regulation) (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, CO2 + CO3]`**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct the load test on a DC shunt generator to obtain its external and internal characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt generator has $Z = 540$ conductors, $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, and $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$. At a certain load, the armature current is 60 A. Find the **generated EMF**, the **flux per pole** (if the speed is 1500 rpm), and the **voltage regulation** (assuming $V_{NL} = 235$ V at the same field current and speed). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

1. **Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the complete circuit diagram showing the prime mover, DC shunt generator with armature, shunt field, field rheostat, voltmeter across terminals, ammeter in load circuit, ammeter in field circuit, and variable resistive load bank.

2. **Pre-Test Preparations (2 Marks):**
   - Measure $R_a$ using the voltmeter-ammeter method with the armature disconnected from the field, after carefully locating the **interpoles** to avoid errors.
   - Measure $R_{sh}$ using a multimeter.
   - Check continuity and insulation resistance using a **megger** (insulation resistance should be > 1 MΩ).
   - Verify nameplate ratings: $V$, $I$, $N$, $P$, and winding type.

3. **Procedure (3 Marks):**
   - Start the prime mover and bring the generator to **rated speed** (verified by tachometer).
   - Adjust the **field rheostat** to obtain rated voltage at no-load. This is the **no-load voltage $V_{NL}$**. Record $V_t$, $I_{sh}$, and $N$.
   - Switch on the **load bank** in small, equal increments (typically 6 to 8 steps from no load to 125% of full load).
   - At each step, **simultaneously adjust the field rheostat** to keep speed and field current constant (or note any deviation), and record $V_t$, $I_L$, $I_{sh}$, and $N$.
   - Take readings at **constant speed** to obtain a true characteristic. If speed varies, correct $V_t$ by the relation $V_{corrected} = V_{observed} \times (N_{rated}/N_{actual})$.
   - After reaching the rated full load, gradually **unload** the machine and stop the prime mover.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute shunt field current at full load (assuming rated terminal voltage):**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2.0 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute generated EMF:**

$$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a = 220 + (60)(0.2) = 220 + 12 = 232 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute flux per pole:**

For wave winding: $A = 2$.

$$\Phi = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{N \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{232 \times 60 \times 2}{1500 \times 540 \times 4}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{27{,}840}{3{,}240{,}000} = 8.59 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Wb} = 8.59 \, \text{mWb}$$

**Step 4 — Compute voltage regulation:**

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{235 - 220}{220} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{15}{220} \times 100 = 6.82 \, \%$$

**Final Answer:** $E_g = 232$ V, $\Phi = 8.59$ mWb, $\text{VR} = 6.82\%$. (Distribution: 2 marks for $E_g$, 3 marks for $\Phi$ with full derivation, 2 marks for VR)

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, CO2 + CO3]`**

**(a)** What is **armature reaction**? Explain its effect on the terminal voltage of a DC shunt generator with the help of a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were obtained during the load test on a 220 V DC shunt generator with $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 220 \, \Omega$. Find the **voltage regulation**, plot the **external and internal characteristics**, and find the **flux per pole** at full load (machine is wave wound with $Z = 480$, $P = 4$, $N = 1200$ rpm). **(7 Marks)**

| $I_L$ (A) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_t$ (V) | 230 | 224 | 217 | 209 | 200 |

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Definition (2 Marks):** Armature reaction is the effect of the magnetic field produced by the armature current on the distribution of flux in the main field of a DC machine. When current flows in the armature conductors, it produces a **cross-magnetizing flux** perpendicular to the main field flux.

**Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the field flux distribution showing the main flux $\Phi_M$ (horizontal arrows from N to S pole) and the armature flux $\Phi_A$ (vertical arrows from top to bottom at the pole tips). Show the **distorted resultant flux** with concentration at the trailing pole tips and weakening at the leading pole tips.

**Effects on Terminal Voltage (3 Marks):**
- The cross-magnetizing effect **weakens the main flux** $\Phi_M$, especially at the leading pole tips.
- Reduced $\Phi$ reduces the generated EMF $E_g = k\Phi N$.
- In a **shunt generator**, this also reduces the shunt field current $I_{sh}$ (since $I_{sh} = V_t/R_{sh}$), creating a **cumulative de-excitation effect**.
- The combined effect causes the **terminal voltage to drop** more rapidly with load than would be caused by the armature resistance drop alone.
- Armature reaction is mitigated by using **interpoles** and **compensating windings** in larger machines.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute shunt field current at each load (using $I_{sh} = V_t/R_{sh}$):**

| $I_L$ (A) | $V_t$ (V) | $I_{sh} = V_t/R_{sh}$ (A) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (A) | $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 230 | 1.045 | 1.045 | 230.52 |
| 4 | 224 | 1.018 | 5.018 | 226.51 |
| 8 | 217 | 0.986 | 8.986 | 221.49 |
| 12 | 209 | 0.950 | 12.950 | 215.48 |
| 16 | 200 | 0.909 | 16.909 | 208.45 |

**Step 2 — Voltage Regulation:**

$$\text{VR (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{230 - 200}{200} \times 100 = 15.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Plotting the Characteristics:**

The external characteristic is a plot of $V_t$ vs $I_L$ (drooping from 230 V to 200 V). The internal characteristic is a plot of $E_g$ vs $I_a$ (lies above the external characteristic by an amount equal to $I_a R_a$, starting from $I_a = I_{sh}$ at no-load).

**Step 4 — Flux per Pole at Full Load:**

At full load: $E_g = 208.45$ V, $N = 1200$ rpm, $Z = 480$, $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave wound).

$$\Phi = \frac{E_g \cdot 60 \cdot A}{N \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{208.45 \times 60 \times 2}{1200 \times 480 \times 4}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{25{,}014}{2{,}304{,}000} = 1.0856 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{Wb} = 10.86 \, \text{mWb}$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{VR} = 15\%$, $\Phi_{FL} = 10.86$ mWb. (Distribution: 3 marks for VR + table, 2 marks for graph interpretation, 2 marks for $\Phi$ calculation)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports 2022–2024):**
> 1. **Confusing $I_a$ with $I_L$:** Students frequently plot $V_t$ vs $I_a$ instead of $V_t$ vs $I_L$ for the external characteristic. This is **WRONG**. The external characteristic is **always $V_t$ vs $I_L$**.
> 2. **Forgetting to add $I_{sh}$ to $I_L$ to get $I_a$:** This gives incorrect $E_g$ values and an erroneous internal characteristic. Marks lost: **up to 3 out of 7**.
> 3. **Wrong sign in voltage regulation formula:** Writing $\dfrac{V_{FL} - V_{NL}}{V_{NL}}$ instead of $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}}$. Marks lost: **1 full mark**.
> 4. **Using $A = P$ for a wave wound generator:** Always remember **$A = 2$ for wave, $A = P$ for lap**.
> 5. **Not drawing the graph with origin shifted:** If the curve does not start at $(0, 0)$, the origin must be shifted to the smallest x-value and y-value. Drawing axes through the origin wastes the graph area.
> 6. **Omitting units in the calculation table:** KTU strictly deducts **0.5 marks per missing unit**. Always write V, A, Ω, rpm, mWb, and % explicitly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Load Test on DC Shunt Generator**
> - [x] **Aim:** To obtain the external and internal characteristics and determine voltage regulation.
> - [x] **Circuit:** Generator armature + shunt field with rheostat in parallel, variable resistive load bank, voltmeter across terminals, two ammeters (one for load, one for shunt field).
> - [x] **Procedure:** Start prime mover → set rated speed → set $V_{NL}$ → load in steps → record $V_t$, $I_L$, $I_{sh}$, $N$ at each step.
> - [x] **External Char.:** $V_t$ vs $I_L$ — always drooping (positive VR).
> - [x] **Internal Char.:** $E_g$ vs $I_a$ — lies *above* external char. by $I_a R_a$.
> - [x] **Key Equations:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$, $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$, $\text{VR} = (V_{NL}-V_{FL})/V_{FL} \times 100$.
> - [x] **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \Phi N Z P / (60 A)$; for wave $A = 2$, for lap $A = P$.
> - [x] **Two Voltage Drop Causes:** $I_a R_a$ drop + Armature Reaction (demagnetizing).
> - [x] **Typical VR Range:** 5% – 15% for shunt generators.
> - [x] **Graph Plotting:** Use graph sheet, shift origin if needed, label axes with units, draw smooth curve through points (not zig-zag).
> - [x] **Viva Triggers:** "Why does the shunt field rheostat increase the voltage when resistance decreases?" — Answer: Lower $R_{sh}$ → higher $I_{sh}$ → higher $\Phi$ → higher $E_g$ → higher $V_t$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brake test on DC shunt motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Brake test on DC shunt motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Brake Test** (also called the **Prony Brake Test** or **Rope Brake Test**) on a DC Shunt Motor is a *direct-loading method* of testing in which a measured, controllable mechanical frictional load is applied to the rotating armature shaft via a rope-and-drum (or band-and-pulley) assembly. The retarding torque developed by friction is quantified using spring balances, and the true mechanical output power developed by the motor is computed from this torque and the measured shaft speed. The electrical input power is recorded simultaneously using voltmeter and ammeter readings, allowing the **overall efficiency** of the machine to be determined across its full load range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A direct method of testing a DC motor in which the mechanical power developed by the machine is absorbed by a friction brake and measured directly, while the electrical input is measured by standard instrument readings."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are cycling a bicycle on a stationary stand. The back wheel presses against a roller covered with a felt pad that you can tighten with a screw. As you pedal:
- The tighter the screw, the harder it is to pedal → this is **increased mechanical load**.
- The roller resists the wheel's motion through **friction**, and that friction can be measured by spring balances attached to the felt pad.
- The faster you pedal (more rotations per minute) and the harder the pad grips, the more "work" you do per second, which is your **mechanical power output**.

In a DC shunt motor brake test, the rotating armature is the "wheel," the rope wound around the pulley is the "friction pad," and the two spring balances measure the pull on either side of the rope, giving us the net retarding force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** A **brake dynamometer** works on exactly this principle — it absorbs and measures the mechanical power of an engine using friction instead of dissipating it electrically.

## 1.3 Why is the Brake Test Important?

| Aspect | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Direct measurement** | Unlike Swinburne's test (which is a no-load indirect test), brake test directly measures output at varying loads |
| **Real-load behaviour** | Captures actual motor characteristics: speed regulation, torque curve, commutation under load |
| **Cooling advantage** | The water-cooled drum continuously removes heat, allowing high loads without overheating the motor |
| **Educational value** | It is a **compulsory experiment** in PCEEL407 — KTU examiners frequently test the procedure and calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **Brake Test** (mechanical, on motors only) with the **Hopkinson Test** (electrical regenerative, on two identical machines). The brake test is **destructive to energy** — all output is wasted as heat in friction.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard drum radius range:** $R = 0.05$ to $0.10$ m (typical lab value: $R = 0.075$ m)
- **Rated speeds of DC shunt motors:** typically **1500 rpm** or **1000 rpm**
- **Standard unit conversions:** $1 \ \text{kgf} = 9.81 \ \text{N}$

## 1.5 Apparatus Required (KTU Practical Record Standard List)

1. DC Shunt Motor under test
2. Rope brake (hemp/cotton rope) wound on water-cooled drum
3. Two spring balances (0–10 kg range, calibrated)
4. Water supply for cooling the drum
5. DC voltmeter (0–300 V)
6. DC ammeter (0–10 A or 0–20 A depending on motor)
7. Tachometer (digital or analog, for measuring $N$)
8. Rheostat (for field regulation, if speed variation is also studied)
9. DPST switch, fuse unit, and 3-point starter
10. Connecting wires and crocodile clips
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics of Frictional Loading

When the rope is wound around the rotating drum, the friction between the rope and the drum surface drags the rope along. The two spring balances at the ends of the rope register unequal tensions:
- **$S_1$** = tension on the **tight side** (kgf) — always greater
- **$S_2$** = tension on the **slack side** (kgf) — always smaller

The **net effective frictional force** retarding the drum is:
$$F = (S_1 - S_2) \ \text{kgf}$$

This force acts at the drum surface, at a distance $R$ (radius) from the axis, producing a **retarding torque** (also called **shaft torque** or **brake torque**):
$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times 9.81 \ \text{N·m}$$

## 2.2 Why Two Spring Balances Instead of One?

If a fixed dead-weight $W$ is attached at one end of the rope and a single spring balance $S$ is at the other end, the net force is $(W - S)$. However, this method has issues:
- The rope may slip on the drum at high loads.
- A single balance cannot distinguish between useful frictional torque and rope stiffness.

Using **two spring balances** allows the net tangential force on the drum to be measured **directly** and **accurately**, which is the KTU-preferred method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Highlight:** In a true friction brake, $S_1$ and $S_2$ are related by the **belt friction equation**: $\frac{S_1}{S_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$, where $\mu$ is the rope-drum coefficient of friction and $\theta$ is the angle of wrap (in radians). For a single turn, $\theta = 2\pi$.

## 2.3 Power Calculations — Exhaustive Breakdown

### (a) Mechanical Output Power (Shaft Power)

The motor does useful mechanical work against the frictional torque per revolution. With $N$ revolutions per second, the energy per second is:

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = T_{sh} \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Substituting the torque expression in N·m:

$$P_{out} = 2 \pi N \times (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times \frac{9.81}{60} \ \text{watts}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N \cdot (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81}{60} \ \text{W}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplication order in calculator (KTU trick):** Compute as $\frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times N \times (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times 9.81}{60}$. Always enter $(S_1 - S_2)$ as a positive value — never as negative.

### (b) Electrical Input Power

For a DC shunt motor, the input from the DC mains is:

$$P_{in} = V \times I_L \ \text{watts}$$

where:
- $V$ = supply voltage (voltmeter reading)
- $I_L$ = total line current drawn from supply (ammeter reading)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pitfall:** Do NOT use armature current $I_a$ for input power. The line current $I_L$ includes both armature and shunt field currents.

### (c) Total Losses

$$\text{Total Loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = I_L^2 R_{sh} \cdot (\text{field}) + I_a^2 R_a + P_{brush} + P_{stray} + P_{f \ \& \ w}$$

Where the *constant losses* (field copper loss, friction, windage, and stray losses) remain roughly **constant** across loads, while the *variable losses* ($I_a^2 R_a$) vary with the square of armature current.

### (d) Overall Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

The efficiency is a maximum when **variable loss = constant loss**, a condition KTU examiners love to ask about.

## 2.4 Performance Characteristics (Graphs to be Plotted)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Record Requirement:** Every brake test report MUST include the following three characteristic curves, drawn on graph paper with proper axis labels and equal scaling:

| Graph No. | X-axis | Y-axis | Nature of Curve |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Power $P_{out}$ (W) | Efficiency $\eta$ (%) | Inverted U-shape (peaks at full load) |
| 2 | Output Power $P_{out}$ (W) | Line Current $I_L$ (A) | Nearly linear (slight curvature) |
| 3 | Output Power $P_{out}$ (W) | Speed $N$ (rpm) | Slightly drooping (DC shunt motor has good speed regulation) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net frictional force | $F = (S_1 - S_2)$ | kgf | $S_1 > S_2$ always |
| 2 | Net frictional force (SI) | $F = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81$ | N | Convert to Newtons |
| 3 | Shaft torque | $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81$ | N·m | Multiplied by drum radius $R$ |
| 4 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 5 | Output power | $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ | W | Fundamental relation |
| 6 | Output power (expanded) | $P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N \cdot (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81}{60}$ | W | Direct numerical formula |
| 7 | Input power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ | W | $I_L$ is line current |
| 8 | Field current | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | A | Constant for constant $V$ |
| 9 | Armature current | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | A | Must be positive |
| 10 | Armature copper loss | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$ | W | Variable loss |
| 11 | Field copper loss | $P_{cu,f} = V \cdot I_{sh} = I_{sh}^2 \cdot R_{sh}$ | W | Constant loss (DC shunt) |
| 12 | Overall efficiency | $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$ | % | Always < 100% |
| 13 | Constant losses | $P_{const} = P_{in,NL} - I_{a,NL}^2 R_a - V I_{sh}$ | W | From no-load data |
| 14 | Condition for max $\eta$ | $I_a^2 R_a = P_{const}$ | W | Variable loss = Constant loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion tip for KTU problems:** $1 \ \text{hp} = 746 \ \text{W}$. When output is given in horsepower, multiply by 746 to convert to watts before comparing with $P_{in}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The brake dynamometer is not just a lab curiosity — it is the industry standard for:
- **Performance testing of traction motors** (railways, EVs)
- **Type-testing of fractional-horsepower motors** at manufacturer facilities
- **Quality control** in motor manufacturing units
- **Research and development** of new motor designs
- **Calibration of torque transducers** — the brake test provides the reference torque

The water-cooled version you use in the lab is essentially a small-scale version of the **eddy-current dynamometer** and **hydraulic dynamometer** used in industry, with the friction-based rope brake being the simplest and cheapest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fundamental Mechanics to the Output Power Formula

### Step 1 — Definition of Work Done

Work is defined as the product of force and displacement in the direction of force. When the drum rotates through one complete revolution, the rope (held by the spring balances) effectively moves a distance equal to the **circumference** of the drum:

$$\text{Displacement per revolution} = 2\pi R \ \text{meters}$$

### Step 2 — Definition of Torque

The net tangential force on the drum is $(S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81$ Newtons, acting at radius $R$ meters. The torque is force times perpendicular distance:

$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \ \text{N·m}$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Angular Velocity

The motor shaft makes $N$ revolutions per minute, so per second it makes $N/60$ revolutions. Each revolution corresponds to $2\pi$ radians:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30} \ \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 4 — Definition of Power

Power is the rate of doing work, which equals torque times angular velocity:

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega$$

### Step 5 — Final Substitution

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$P_{out} = \left[ (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R \right] \times \left[ \frac{2\pi N}{60} \right]$$

Rearranging the multiplicative factors for clarity:

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot R \cdot (S_1 - S_2) \cdot 9.81}{60} \ \text{watts}}$$

This is the **single most important formula** in the entire experiment and is **almost guaranteed** to appear in KTU theory exams.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Standard)

### Problem Statement

A DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of **3 A** at **220 V**. The armature and shunt field resistances are **0.5 Ω** and **220 Ω** respectively. A brake test on the motor gives the following observations at a particular load:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Supply voltage $V$ | 220 V |
| Line current $I_L$ | 22 A |
| Spring balance 1 ($S_1$) | 6.0 kgf |
| Spring balance 2 ($S_2$) | 1.0 kgf |
| Drum radius $R$ | 0.07 m |
| Motor speed $N$ | 1450 rpm |

Calculate: **(a)** Shaft torque, **(b)** Output power, **(c)** Input power, **(d)** Overall efficiency, **(e)** All individual losses, **(f)** Shaft torque developed in N·m.

---

### Solution

**Given Data (extracted into symbolic form):**

$$V = 220 \ \text{V}, \quad I_L = 22 \ \text{A}, \quad S_1 = 6.0 \ \text{kgf}, \quad S_2 = 1.0 \ \text{kgf}$$

$$R = 0.07 \ \text{m}, \quad N = 1450 \ \text{rpm}, \quad R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega, \quad R_{sh} = 220 \ \Omega$$

---

### Part (a) — Shaft Torque $T_{sh}$

Net force on the rope:
$$F = S_1 - S_2 = 6.0 - 1.0 = 5.0 \ \text{kgf}$$

Converting to Newtons:
$$F = 5.0 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \ \text{N}$$

Shaft torque (force × radius):
$$T_{sh} = F \times R = 49.05 \times 0.07 = 3.4335 \ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 3.43 \ \text{N·m}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Output Power $P_{out}$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{9110.65}{60} = 151.844 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = 3.4335 \times 151.844 = 521.4 \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 521.4 \ \text{W}}$$

**Cross-verification using expanded formula:**

$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 0.07 \times 5.0 \times 9.81}{60}$$

Numerator step-by-step:
$$2 \times 3.1416 \times 1450 = 9110.65$$
$$9110.65 \times 0.07 = 637.745$$
$$637.745 \times 5.0 = 3188.73$$
$$3188.73 \times 9.81 = 31281.45$$
$$P_{out} = \frac{31281.45}{60} = 521.36 \ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods agree. ✓

---

### Part (c) — Input Power $P_{in}$

$$P_{in} = V \times I_L = 220 \times 22 = 4840 \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{in} = 4840 \ \text{W} = 4.84 \ \text{kW}}$$

---

### Part (d) — Overall Efficiency $\eta$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{521.4}{4840} \times 100 = 10.77\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 10.77\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Realistic interpretation:** Such a low efficiency indicates either a very small load or that this is an old/lab motor with significant losses. A healthy 5-hp DC shunt motor typically operates at 80–88% efficiency at full load.

---

### Part (e) — All Individual Losses

**Step 1 — Field current:**
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1.0 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Armature current:**
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 22 - 1 = 21 \ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Armature copper loss:**
$$P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (21)^2 \times 0.5 = 441 \times 0.5 = 220.5 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Field copper loss:**
$$P_{cu,f} = V \times I_{sh} = 220 \times 1.0 = 220 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Total losses (energy balance check):**
$$P_{total\ loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 4840 - 521.4 = 4318.6 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Stray + friction + windage + brush losses (combined):**
$$P_{stray+f+w} = P_{total\ loss} - P_{cu,a} - P_{cu,f} = 4318.6 - 220.5 - 220 = 3878.1 \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{stray+f+w} \approx 3878.1 \ \text{W}}$$

This huge stray loss is the reason for the low efficiency — the motor is operating far below its design load.

---

### Part (f) — Developed (Internal) Torque $T_d$

The torque developed electromagnetically inside the armature is related to the gross developed power, which equals the output plus the friction & windage losses. But a more direct way is using the armature EMF and current:

The back-EMF is:
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (21 \times 0.5) = 220 - 10.5 = 209.5 \ \text{V}$$

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_d = E_b \times I_a = 209.5 \times 21 = 4399.5 \ \text{W}$$

Developed torque:
$$T_d = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{4399.5}{151.844} = 28.97 \ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 28.97 \ \text{N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $T_d > T_{sh}$?** The developed torque $T_d$ is at the armature surface, and it must overcome the **shaft torque** $T_{sh}$ (delivered to the load) **plus** the torque lost to friction and windage of the rotating parts. The difference between $T_d$ and $T_{sh}$ is the **rotational loss torque**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on Performance

| Parameter | Symbol | If Increased (Load) | Effect on $T_{sh}$ | Effect on $N$ | Effect on $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drum friction | (rope tightening) | ↑ | ↑ (linear) | ↓ (slight) | Depends |
| Line current $I_L$ | $I_L$ | ↑ | ↑ (proportional) | ↓ (slight) | ↑ then ↓ |
| Field excitation | $I_{sh}$ | ↑ (weaker field) | ↑ (for same $I_a$) | ↑ | ↓ |
| Drum radius $R$ | $R$ | ↑ | ↑ (linear) | No direct effect | Indirect |
| Supply voltage $V$ | $V$ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (slight) | ↑ |

## 3.4 Common Numerical Traps in KTU Exams

1. **Using $I_a$ instead of $I_L$ for input power** → overestimates efficiency.
2. **Forgetting to multiply by 9.81** when $S_1 - S_2$ is in kgf → torque becomes wrong by ~10%.
3. **Confusing angular velocity units** (rad/s vs rpm) → factor of 60 error.
4. **Negative efficiency** if calculated from a slip condition (very high load where motor stalls) — KTU expects you to comment on this.
5. **Ignoring $R_{sh}$** when given the field current directly — the field copper loss formula requires $V$ and $I_{sh}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When the value of $S_2$ is greater than $S_1$ due to a reversal mistake in tightening the brake, the student often reports a negative output. This is a sign that the experiment was done incorrectly — the **spring balance on the side where the rope is being pulled UP by friction should show the higher reading**. Always re-check the direction of rope pull.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply 220V DC Mains] --> B[DPST Switch and Fuse Unit]
    B --> C[Three Point Starter with NO Volt Coil]
    C --> D[DC Shunt Motor under Test]
    D --> E[Armature Circuit Voltmeter V]
    D --> F[Line Ammeter I_L]
    D --> G[Field Rheostat in Series with Shunt Winding]
    D --> H[Rotating Drum Coupled to Shaft]
    H --> I[Rope Wound Around Drum Water Cooled]
    I --> J[Spring Balance S1 Tight Side]
    I --> K[Spring Balance S2 Slack Side]
    H --> L[Tachometer Reading N in rpm]
    M[Water Inlet for Drum Cooling] --> I
    N[Water Outlet Drain] --> I
    O[Brake Wheel Tightening Screw Load Control] --> I
    P[Observations Table: V, I_L, N, S1, S2] --> Q[Calculation of T_sh, P_out, P_in, Efficiency]
    Q --> R[Plot: P_out vs Efficiency]
    Q --> S[Plot: P_out vs I_L]
    Q --> T[Plot: P_out vs N]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow and Energy Accounting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Electrical Input P_in = V times I_L] --> A1[Armature Copper Loss I_a squared R_a]
    IN --> A2[Field Copper Loss V times I_sh]
    IN --> A3[Stray Loss]
    IN --> A4[Friction and Windage Loss]
    IN --> A5[Brush Contact Loss]
    A1 --> MECH[Gross Mechanical Power Developed P_d = E_b times I_a]
    A2 --> MECH
    A3 --> MECH
    MECH --> FW[Friction and Windage Rotational Loss]
    FW --> OUT[Shaft Output Power P_out = 2 pi N T_sh / 60]
    A4 --> OUT
    A5 --> OUT
    OUT --> LOAD[Rope Brake Friction Load]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Procedural Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Step 1: Identify Name Plate Ratings of Motor] --> ST2[Step 2: Check all Connections and Continuity]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3: Set Field Rheostat to Minimum Resistance]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4: Start Motor with No Load using 3 Point Starter]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5: Adjust Field Rheostat to Rated Speed]
    ST5 --> ST6[Step 6: Take No Load Reading: V, I_L, N]
    ST6 --> ST7[Step 7: Wind Rope Around Drum Tighten Slightly for Light Load]
    ST7 --> ST8[Step 8: Take Reading 1: V, I_L, N, S1, S2]
    ST8 --> ST9[Step 9: Increase Load by Tightening Brake Screw]
    ST9 --> ST10[Step 10: Take Reading 2]
    ST10 --> ST11[Step 11: Repeat for 5 to 6 Loads up to 125 percent of Rated]
    ST11 --> ST12[Step 12: Loosen Brake, Switch Off, Record Cooling Water Observations]
    ST12 --> ST13[Step 13: Calculate T_sh, P_out, P_in, Efficiency for Each Load]
    ST13 --> ST14[Step 14: Plot Characteristic Curves on Graph Paper]
    ST14 --> ST15[Step 15: Comment on Nature of Curves and Maximum Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic — Description for Diagram Drawing in Lab Record

Since a Mermaid block cannot perfectly replicate a physical diagram, the lab record should include the following hand-drawn schematic with these key elements:

1. **Three-phase diagram orientation:**
   - **Left side:** DC mains → DPST switch → fuse → 3-point starter → field rheostat (parallel) → motor terminals
   - **Center:** Motor with shaft extending to the right
   - **Right side:** Pulley/drum on the shaft, rope around the drum, two spring balances hanging on either side
   - **Below the drum:** Water inlet on one side, water outlet on the other

2. **Instruments placement (KTU standard):**
   - Voltmeter **directly across armature terminals** (not at mains)
   - Ammeter in series with the **line** (measures total $I_L$)
   - Tachometer pointed at the **non-drive end** of the shaft

3. **Cooling water flow path:**
   - Inlet → upper chamber of drum → centrifugal force → drum wall cooling → lower chamber → outlet

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative to spring balances:** Many KTU labs now use a **digital load cell** in place of mechanical spring balances. The reading is in kgf or N directly, and the principle remains identical.

## 4.5 Equipment Diagram Labels Reference Table

| Component | Mermaid Node ID | Function |
|---|---|---|
| DC Supply | nodeA | Provides 220 V DC input |
| DPST Switch | nodeB | Double-pole ON/OFF control |
| Starter | nodeC | Limits starting current |
| Voltmeter | nodeE | Measures armature voltage |
| Ammeter | nodeF | Measures line current |
| Motor | nodeD | DC shunt machine under test |
| Drum | nodeH | Friction surface for load |
| Rope | nodeI | Transfers friction force |
| S1 (tight) | nodeJ | Measures higher tension |
| S2 (slack) | nodeK | Measures lower tension |
| Tachometer | nodeL | Measures speed N in rpm |
| Load screw | nodeO | Varies frictional force |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is the purpose of the brake test on a DC shunt motor? Why cannot it be performed on a DC generator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The brake test is performed on a DC shunt motor to **directly determine the output power and efficiency of the motor under varying load conditions**. In this test, a mechanical load is applied to the motor shaft using a friction brake (rope and drum arrangement), and the retarding torque is measured using spring balances, while the input is measured electrically.

The brake test **cannot be performed on a DC generator** because:

1. A generator is meant to convert mechanical input into electrical output. To apply a brake test, we would need to mechanically drive the generator and absorb its output as heat through friction — but the generator's output is electrical, not mechanical.
2. A generator is **driven by a prime mover** (like a motor), and loading it via friction would simply load the prime mover, not the generator itself.
3. The brake test is fundamentally a **motor loading test** — it requires the machine to develop mechanical torque to overcome the frictional load.

**[Marking scheme: 1 mark for purpose + 2 marks for why not on generator = 3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: In a brake test, two spring balances of readings $S_1 = 8$ kgf and $S_2 = 1.5$ kgf are noted when the drum radius is 0.08 m and the motor speed is 1440 rpm. Calculate the shaft torque and the output power.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Net force on the rope:
$$F = S_1 - S_2 = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5 \ \text{kgf} = 6.5 \times 9.81 = 63.765 \ \text{N}$$

Shaft torque:
$$T_{sh} = F \times R = 63.765 \times 0.08 = 5.10 \ \text{N·m}$$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1440}{60} = 150.80 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = 5.10 \times 150.80 = 769.1 \ \text{W} \approx 0.77 \ \text{kW}$$

**Alternatively using direct formula:**
$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 0.08 \times 6.5 \times 9.81}{60} = 768.9 \ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

**[Marking scheme: 1 mark for force + 1 mark for torque + 1 mark for output power = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the experimental procedure to conduct the brake test on a DC shunt motor. List all the instruments and their connections. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The following observations were taken during a brake test on a DC shunt motor:

| Load | $V$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $S_1$ (kgf) | $S_2$ (kgf) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No load | 220 | 4.0 | 1500 | 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 1 | 220 | 12.0 | 1480 | 3.5 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 220 | 20.0 | 1460 | 6.0 | 1.0 |
| 3 | 220 | 28.0 | 1440 | 8.0 | 1.5 |

**Given:** $R_a = 0.4 \ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 220 \ \Omega$, Drum radius $R_d = 0.075$ m.

Calculate: **(i)** Output power at each loaded condition, **(ii)** Input power at each loaded condition, **(iii)** Efficiency at each loaded condition, **(iv)** All losses at the rated loaded condition. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

#### Circuit Diagram Description:
- A 220 V DC supply is connected through a **DPST switch** and **fuse unit** to a **3-point starter**.
- The starter's overload release (OLR) and no-volt coil (NVC) protect the motor.
- The starter output is connected to the **armature terminals** of the DC shunt motor.
- A **voltmeter** is connected directly across the armature.
- An **ammeter** is connected in series with the line (before the starter) to measure total line current.
- The **shunt field winding** is connected in parallel with the armature, with a **field rheostat** in series for speed control.
- The motor shaft is coupled to a **rope brake drum** with water-cooling arrangement.
- Two **spring balances** are attached to the ends of the rope, hanging vertically.

#### Step-by-Step Procedure:

1. **Identify name-plate ratings** of the motor: voltage, current, speed, power, insulation class.
2. **Make connections** as per the circuit diagram, ensuring tight, clean contacts.
3. **Set the field rheostat** to minimum resistance (maximum field current) to ensure safe starting.
4. **Keep the starter handle** in the OFF position. Switch ON the DPST.
5. **Gradually move the starter handle** from OFF to ON position, allowing the motor to start smoothly.
6. **Adjust the field rheostat** to obtain the **rated no-load speed**.
7. **Take no-load reading:** $V, I_L, N$ (with brake fully loose, so $S_1 = S_2 = 0$).
8. **Slightly tighten the brake** to apply a small load, wait for steady state.
9. **Take the first load reading:** $V, I_L, N, S_1, S_2$.
10. **Repeat** for 5–6 increasing loads up to about **125% of rated current**.
11. For each load, ensure the drum is **adequately cooled** by the water flow.
12. After the test, **loosen the brake fully, switch off the supply**, and record cooling observations.
13. **Tabulate** all readings and calculate $T_{sh}, P_{out}, P_{in}, \eta$ for each load.
14. **Plot** the three characteristic curves on graph paper.

**[Mark split: 2 marks for circuit + 3 marks for procedure + 1 mark for precautions + 1 mark for plotting]**

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Pre-calculated values:**

Field current: $I_{sh} = 220/220 = 1.0 \ \text{A}$

**Load 1: $I_L = 12$ A**
- $I_a = 12 - 1 = 11$ A
- Net force: $F = 3.5 - 0.5 = 3.0$ kgf $= 29.43$ N
- Torque: $T_{sh} = 29.43 \times 0.075 = 2.207$ N·m
- Angular velocity: $\omega = 2\pi \times 1480/60 = 154.97$ rad/s
- $P_{out} = 2.207 \times 154.97 = 342.0$ W
- $P_{in} = 220 \times 12 = 2640$ W
- $\eta = (342/2640) \times 100 = 12.95\%$

**Load 2: $I_L = 20$ A**
- $I_a = 19$ A
- $F = 6.0 - 1.0 = 5.0$ kgf $= 49.05$ N
- $T_{sh} = 49.05 \times 0.075 = 3.679$ N·m
- $\omega = 2\pi \times 1460/60 = 152.89$ rad/s
- $P_{out} = 3.679 \times 152.89 = 562.5$ W
- $P_{in} = 220 \times 20 = 4400$ W
- $\eta = (562.5/4400) \times 100 = 12.78\%$

**Load 3: $I_L = 28$ A**
- $I_a = 27$ A
- $F = 8.0 - 1.5 = 6.5$ kgf $= 63.77$ N
- $T_{sh} = 63.77 \times 0.075 = 4.783$ N·m
- $\omega = 2\pi \times 1440/60 = 150.80$ rad/s
- $P_{out} = 4.783 \times 150.80 = 721.3$ W
- $P_{in} = 220 \times 28 = 6160$ W
- $\eta = (721.3/6160) \times 100 = 11.71\%$

**Summary Table:**

| Load | $I_L$ (A) | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12.0 | 2.21 | 342.0 | 2640 | 12.95 |
| 2 | 20.0 | 3.68 | 562.5 | 4400 | 12.78 |
| 3 | 28.0 | 4.78 | 721.3 | 6160 | 11.71 |

**Losses at Load 3 (rated loaded condition):**
- $P_{cu,a} = (27)^2 \times 0.4 = 291.6$ W
- $P_{cu,f} = 220 \times 1 = 220$ W
- $P_{total\ loss} = 6160 - 721.3 = 5438.7$ W
- $P_{stray+f+w} = 5438.7 - 291.6 - 220 = 4927.1$ W

**[Mark split: 2 marks for $P_{out}$ table + 2 marks for $P_{in}$ and $\eta$ + 2 marks for losses + 1 mark for final summary]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle of the brake test. What is the significance of using two spring balances? What is the role of water in the cooling drum? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A DC shunt motor rated 5 hp, 220 V, 21 A, 1500 rpm is tested by brake test. The no-load reading is $V = 220$ V, $I_L = 3$ A, $N = 1520$ rpm. At full load, the readings are $V = 220$ V, $I_L = 21$ A, $N = 1450$ rpm, $S_1 = 7.2$ kgf, $S_2 = 1.3$ kgf, $R_d = 0.08$ m. Given $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$, find: **(i)** Full-load efficiency, **(ii)** Constant losses, **(iii)** Stray losses at full load, **(iv)** Condition for maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Principle of Brake Test (3 marks):**

The brake test works on the principle of **absorbing mechanical power through friction**. A rope or band is wound around a pulley/drum mounted on the motor shaft, and the friction between the rope and the drum surface produces a **retarding torque** that loads the motor. The retarding torque is measured using spring balances attached to the rope ends, and the speed is measured using a tachometer. The product of retarding torque and angular velocity gives the mechanical output power of the motor.

Mathematically:
$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \times \omega = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times 9.81 \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Significance of Two Spring Balances (2 marks):**

1. **Accuracy:** Two balances measure the net tangential force $(S_1 - S_2)$ directly, eliminating errors due to rope weight, drum weight, and friction in the balance itself.
2. **Direct net force measurement:** A single balance with a dead-weight cannot compensate for the difference between tight-side and slack-side tensions.
3. **Smooth operation:** Two balances allow the rope to be held firmly on both sides, preventing slippage and ensuring the frictional contact angle is maintained.

**Role of Water in the Cooling Drum (2 marks):**

1. The brake test dissipates the entire mechanical output as **heat due to friction**. At full load of a 5-hp motor, this is over **3700 W of heat** that must be removed.
2. The water circulates through the **hollow interior of the drum** and absorbs the heat through the drum walls, preventing the drum, rope, and bearings from overheating.
3. Without cooling, the rope would **char or catch fire**, and the drum bearings would seize, causing a dangerous mechanical failure.
4. The water flow rate is critical: too little flow and the drum overheats; too much flow and the drum temperature drops below the **dew point**, causing condensation on electrical parts.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Full load: $V = 220$ V, $I_L = 21$ A, $N = 1450$ rpm, $S_1 = 7.2$ kgf, $S_2 = 1.3$ kgf
- $R_d = 0.08$ m, $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 220$ V $/ 1$ A → $R_{sh} = 220$ Ω
- No load: $V = 220$ V, $I_L = 3$ A

**Field current:** $I_{sh} = 220/220 = 1$ A
**Armature current at full load:** $I_a = 21 - 1 = 20$ A

**Step (i) — Full-load efficiency:**

Net force on rope:
$$F = S_1 - S_2 = 7.2 - 1.3 = 5.9 \ \text{kgf} = 5.9 \times 9.81 = 57.879 \ \text{N}$$

Shaft torque:
$$T_{sh} = 57.879 \times 0.08 = 4.630 \ \text{N·m}$$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1450}{60} = 151.844 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = 4.630 \times 151.844 = 703.0 \ \text{W}$$

Wait — let me recheck: $5.9 \times 9.81 = 57.879$, and $57.879 \times 0.08 = 4.6303$ N·m, and $4.6303 \times 151.844 = 703.1$ W.

Input power:
$$P_{in} = 220 \times 21 = 4620 \ \text{W}$$

Full-load efficiency:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{703.1}{4620} \times 100 = 15.22\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 5-hp motor (3728 W) at full load should give an output of ~3000+ W. The low value here suggests a lab-scale test with very small drum or high losses. The calculation methodology is what matters, not the absolute values.

**Step (ii) — Constant losses:**

From no-load data: $V_{NL} = 220$ V, $I_{L,NL} = 3$ A
- $I_{a,NL} = 3 - 1 = 2$ A
- No-load armature copper loss: $I_{a,NL}^2 R_a = (2)^2 \times 0.5 = 2.0$ W
- Field copper loss: $V \times I_{sh} = 220 \times 1 = 220$ W
- No-load input: $P_{in,NL} = 220 \times 3 = 660$ W

At no load, the output is zero (no useful mechanical work), so all the input goes to losses:
$$P_{const} = P_{in,NL} - P_{cu,a,NL} = 660 - 2 = 658 \ \text{W}$$

This 658 W includes the field copper loss (220 W) plus the rotational losses (friction, windage, stray). So the **rotational + field + stray constant losses together = 658 W**, of which the **field loss alone is 220 W**.

**Step (iii) — Stray losses at full load:**

Total losses at full load:
$$P_{total,FL} = P_{in,FL} - P_{out,FL} = 4620 - 703.1 = 3916.9 \ \text{W}$$

Armature copper loss at full load:
$$P_{cu,a,FL} = (20)^2 \times 0.5 = 200 \ \text{W}$$

Field copper loss = 220 W (constant)

Stray + rotational losses:
$$P_{stray} = P_{total,FL} - P_{cu,a,FL} - P_{cu,f} = 3916.9 - 200 - 220 = 3496.9 \ \text{W}$$

**Step (iv) — Condition for maximum efficiency:**

For maximum efficiency, **variable loss = constant rotational losses** (NOT including field loss, but including stray + friction + windage).

Constant rotational losses = Total constant losses - Field copper loss:
$$P_{rot} = 658 - 220 = 438 \ \text{W}$$

But the problem states that the **no-load armature copper loss** is small and treated as part of constant losses in this analysis. So we use the simple condition:

Setting armature copper loss = constant losses (excluding field):
$$I_a^2 R_a = P_{const} - P_{cu,f}$$
$$I_a^2 \times 0.5 = 658 - 220 = 438$$
$$I_a^2 = 876$$
$$I_a = 29.6 \ \text{A at max efficiency}$$

Line current at max efficiency:
$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 29.6 + 1 = 30.6 \ \text{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU observation:** Maximum efficiency in a DC shunt motor occurs at a load **slightly greater than full load** because the field copper loss is always present and reduces the available constant loss for matching. The exact match point is when $I_a^2 R_a = P_{const} - P_{cu,f}$ (constant rotational loss only).

**[Mark split: 2 marks for full-load efficiency + 2 marks for constant losses + 1 mark for stray losses + 2 marks for maximum efficiency calculation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_a$** in the efficiency formula (most common deduction).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kgf to N** by multiplying by 9.81 — this alone causes 0.5–1 mark deduction per question.
> 3. **Mixing up developed torque $T_d$ and shaft torque $T_{sh}$** — they are different by the rotational loss torque.
> 4. **Using $N$ in rps instead of rpm** or vice versa — the 60 in the denominator is a frequent omission.
> 5. **Not including the field copper loss** in the constant losses for max efficiency condition.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

### ✅ Key Concepts (MUST KNOW)
- [x] Brake test is a **direct loading test**, only applicable to **motors** (not generators).
- [x] Uses **rope + drum + two spring balances** + water cooling.
- [x] Measures **shaft torque** and **output power directly**; input measured electrically.
- [x] **Three graphs** are plotted: $P_{out}$ vs $\eta$, $P_{out}$ vs $I_L$, $P_{out}$ vs $N$.
- [x] DC shunt motor has **nearly constant speed** (slight droop) with load.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [x] $F = (S_1 - S_2) \ \text{kgf}$
- [x] $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81 \ \text{N·m}$
- [x] $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N \cdot (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81}{60} \ \text{W}$
- [x] $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$
- [x] $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100\%$
- [x] $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$, where $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$
- [x] Condition for max efficiency: $I_a^2 R_a = P_{const}$

### ✅ Numerical Constants (KEEP HANDY)
- [x] $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- [x] $1 \ \text{hp} = 746 \ \text{W}$
- [x] $1 \ \text{kgf} = 9.81 \ \text{N}$

### ✅ Procedure (CAN BE ASKED FOR 7 MARKS)
- [x] Identify nameplate → make connections → set field rheostat to minimum → start motor → adjust to rated speed → no-load reading → 5–6 load readings → calculate → plot curves → comment.

### ✅ Precautions (FREQUENTLY ASKED 2–3 MARK SUB-QUESTION)
- [x] Keep **rope away from oil/grease** (reduces friction unpredictably).
- [x] Ensure **water flow is adequate** to prevent rope charring.
- [x] **Do not run the motor at > 125% rated load** for more than 30 seconds.
- [x] The **rope should not slip** on the drum.
- [x] Take readings only when the **tachometer reading is stable**.

### ✅ Comparisons (FOR HIGH-ORDER THINKING QUESTIONS)
- [x] **Brake test vs Swinburne's test:** Brake is direct, loading test; Swinburne is indirect, no-load test.
- [x] **DC shunt vs DC series motor speed characteristics:** Shunt is nearly constant, series falls sharply with load.
- [x] **Stray losses** in DC shunt motor ≈ (1–2% of input) at full load.

### ✅ One-Liners (FOR 2-MARK QUICK ANSWERS)
- [x] The brake test is called a **"wasted power test"** because all output is dissipated as heat.
- [x] The water cooling drum is sometimes called a **"friction dynamometer"**.
- [x] If the rope slips, $S_1$ and $S_2$ become nearly equal, giving **zero net force and zero output**.
- [x] The drum is usually made of **cast iron or mild steel** for good heat capacity.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Brake Test on DC Shunt Motor**

topics.title: Brake test on DC series motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Brake test on DC series motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Brake Test (Direct Load Test / Pony Brake Test)**
> The brake test is a **direct method** of loading an electrical motor in which a measured mechanical frictional load is applied to the motor shaft using a rope-and-drum (or band-and-drum) brake arrangement. By measuring the dead weights, spring balance reading, drum radius, armature current, terminal voltage, and speed, the **output torque, output power, input power, and efficiency** of the motor are determined under varying load conditions.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this experiment is classified as a **direct-loading performance test** and is mandatory for DC machines because:

- The no-load speed of a DC series motor is theoretically infinite (dangerous).
- Hence, a series motor **must never be started on no-load** — the brake test inherently provides a safe minimum load.
- It yields the **complete load characteristics** of the machine in a single experiment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hand Brake on a Bullock Cart Wheel"**
> Imagine tying a thick rope around a rotating wheel (the brake drum mounted on the motor shaft). You pull the rope's two ends with unequal forces — a heavy stone on one side (tight side, weight $W_1$) and a spring balance on the other (slack side, reading $S$). The friction between the rope and the wheel *resists* rotation. The motor must therefore *push harder* (develop more torque) to keep turning. The "effort" the motor exerts to overcome this rope friction is exactly the **braking torque** the machine is delivering as useful output.

- The two weights emulate a controllable mechanical load.
- The **spring balance** ensures only the *net tangential force* is recorded (since one rope end is anchored to the dynamometer frame).
- The rope and drum arrangement is essentially a **friction dynamometer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Under **CO2 – Perform experiments on DC machines to obtain performance characteristics**, the brake test on a DC series motor is a **core lab experiment** requiring students to (a) plot the **load characteristics** ($N$ vs $I_a$, $T$ vs $I_a$, $\eta$ vs $I_a$, $P_{out}$ vs $I_a$) and (b) explain why a DC series motor **cannot be started without a mechanical load**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value | Used In |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$ | Converting kgf → N |
| $\pi$ | $3.14159$ | $P_{out}$ calculation |
| Conversion: 1 kgf | $= 9.81$ N | Torque equation |
| Rated full-load current | As per nameplate | Loading limit |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The brake test relies on converting the **rotational mechanical output** of the motor into a **measurable linear frictional force**, which is then used to compute torque.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Coupling**
- A **brake drum / pulley** is rigidly fixed to the motor shaft.

**Step 2 — Rope Winding**
- A flexible rope (or band) is wound around the drum. The two free ends hang vertically.
- A **dead weight** ($W_1$, in kg) is suspended from one end — this is the **tight side**.
- A **spring balance** (reading $S$, in kg) is attached to the other end — this is the **slack side**.

**Step 3 — Friction Force Generation**
- As the drum rotates, friction between the rope and drum creates a **tangential retarding force** $F$.
- The force is the **net of the two side weights** because the rope is in equilibrium:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque Generation**
- This net tangential force, acting at the effective drum radius $R$, produces a **braking torque**:

$$
T_b = F_{\text{net}} \times R = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81 \times R \ \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-State Condition**
- Under steady load, the **electromagnetic torque** developed by the motor equals the **braking torque** (neglecting rotational losses such as friction and windage, which are absorbed into $T_b$).

**Step 6 — Output Power Extraction**
- Output power is the rotational mechanical power delivered to the brake:

$$
P_{out} = T_b \times \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60} \ \text{W}
$$

**Step 7 — Input Power Measurement**
- Electrical input is measured directly:

$$
P_{in} = V \times I_a
$$

**Step 8 — Efficiency Computation**
- The overall efficiency at that load is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \%
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers **every formula** required for the KTU end-semester lab viva and record.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|
| 1 | Tight-side weight | $W_1$ | Read from hanger | kg |
| 2 | Spring balance reading | $S$ (or $W_2$) | Read from balance | kg |
| 3 | Net tangential force | $F$ | $F = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81$ | N |
| 4 | Effective drum radius | $R$ | $R = D/2$ (from vernier) | m |
| 5 | **Braking Torque** | $T_b$ | $T_b = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81 \times R$ | N·m |
| 6 | Motor speed | $N$ | Read from tachometer | RPM |
| 7 | Angular velocity | $\omega$ | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s |
| 8 | **Output Power** | $P_{out}$ | $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_b / 60$ | W |
| 9 | **Input Power** | $P_{in}$ | $P_{in} = V \times I_a$ | W |
| 10 | **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | $\eta = (P_{out} / P_{in}) \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Output in kW | $P_{out(kW)}$ | $P_{out} / 1000$ | kW |
| 12 | Slip-side belt correction | — | Use *effective* radius $R$ (not $D/2$); rope thickness $t$ reduces $R$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** Brake tests are routinely performed by **manufacturers of traction motors (loco motors, EVs, forklift motors, crane motors)** because these applications almost exclusively use DC series motors. The test validates the motor's ability to deliver high starting torque and allows plotting of the **commutation curve** and **commutating zone characteristics** in industry.

## 2.3 Why $R$ Must Be the **Effective** Drum Radius

The rope has a finite thickness $t$, and it contacts the drum **outer surface**. Hence the true radius at which the tangential force acts is:

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{D}{2} + \frac{t}{2}
$$

where $D$ is the bare drum diameter and $t$ is the rope diameter. Forgetting this **adds a 3–5 % error** — examiners deduct marks for this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Braking Torque from First Principles

Consider a brake drum of effective radius $R$ (in metres) rotating clockwise. Two vertical forces act on the rope:

- **Tight side** pulling **down** with weight $W_1$ (kg) → downward force $W_1 g$ N
- **Slack side** pulling **up** on the spring balance with reading $S$ (kg) → downward force $S g$ N

The **net tangential force** on the drum is the difference because both forces try to drag the drum (one with, one against rotation):

$$
F_{\text{net}} = (W_1 g) - (S g) = (W_1 - S) \cdot g \ \text{N}
$$

The **torque** is force times perpendicular distance (which is the radius $R$):

$$
T_b = F_{\text{net}} \times R = (W_1 - S) \cdot g \cdot R
$$

Substituting $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$
\boxed{T_b = 9.81 \ (W_1 - S) \ R \ \ \text{N·m}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The torque $T_b$ the motor delivers is the *only* mechanical opposition to the load. Therefore $T_b$ equals the **gross shaft torque**, which includes useful load torque + friction + windage losses. (In rigorous KTU calculations we treat $T_b$ as gross output torque.)

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Power Derivation

Mechanical power is torque times angular velocity:

$$
P_{\text{mech}} = T_b \times \omega
$$

Speed $N$ is in **RPM** (revolutions per minute). To convert to **radians per second**:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \ \text{rad/s}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{out} = T_b \times \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60} \ \text{W} = \frac{\pi N T_b}{30} \ \text{W}}
$$

**Conversion to kW:**

$$
P_{out(kW)} = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60000} = \frac{\pi N T_b}{30000}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency Relationship

Electrical input power (assuming DC, no field current separate for a series motor):

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I_a \ \text{W}
$$

(Note: for a series motor, the **same** current flows through armature and field, so $I_a = I_L = I_{se}$.)

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \% = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60 \, V I_a} \times 100 \ \%
$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **typical 14-mark KTU calculation problem**. Practice it thoroughly.

**Given Data (from observation table):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Terminal voltage, $V$ | $220$ V |
| Armature current, $I_a$ | $20$ A |
| Speed, $N$ | $1200$ RPM |
| Tight-side weight, $W_1$ | $12$ kg |
| Spring balance reading, $S$ | $3$ kg |
| Effective drum radius, $R$ | $0.10$ m |
| $g$ | $9.81$ m/s² |

**Step 1 — Net tangential force**

$$
F_{net} = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81 = (12 - 3) \times 9.81 = 9 \times 9.81 = 88.29 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Braking torque**

$$
T_b = F_{net} \times R = 88.29 \times 0.10 = 8.829 \ \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Output power**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T_b}{60} = \frac{2 \times 3.14159 \times 1200 \times 8.829}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{66{,}564.4}{60} = 1109.4 \ \text{W} = 1.109 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Input power**

$$
P_{in} = V \times I_a = 220 \times 20 = 4400 \ \text{W} = 4.4 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1109.4}{4400} \times 100 = 25.21 \ \%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**
- The low efficiency is expected at light load (here $I_a = 20$ A is well below typical rated value).
- As $I_a$ rises (more mechanical load), $T_b$ rises as $\propto I_a^2$ and $\eta$ increases up to a maximum, then falls.

## 3.5 Speed and Torque Characteristics (Analytical Justification)

For a DC series motor with negligible armature reaction and saturation:

$$
N \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \qquad \text{(Hyperbolic drop)}
$$

$$
T \propto I_a^2 \qquad \text{(Quadratic rise)}
$$

Hence, the **load characteristic** $N$ vs $I_a$ is a **rectangular hyperbola**, and $T$ vs $I_a$ is a **parabola through the origin**. These are mandatory graphs in the KTU record.

## 3.6 Typical Multi-Load Observation Table Format

| Sl. No. | $V$ (V) | $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (RPM) | $W_1$ (kg) | $S$ (kg) | $T_b$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|---------|---------|-----------|-----------|------------|----------|-------------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | 220 | 5 | 1800 | 1.5 | 0.2 | 1.28 | 240.5 | 1100 | 21.86 |
| 2 | 220 | 10 | 1300 | 4.0 | 0.8 | 3.14 | 427.4 | 2200 | 19.43 |
| 3 | 220 | 15 | 1050 | 7.5 | 1.5 | 5.89 | 647.6 | 3300 | 19.62 |
| 4 | 220 | 20 | 900 | 12.0 | 3.0 | 8.83 | 831.4 | 4400 | 18.89 |
| 5 | 220 | 25 | 780 | 17.0 | 5.0 | 11.77 | 961.3 | 5500 | 17.48 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
**"Why is a DC series motor never started on no-load? Justify your answer with a suitable speed equation."**

**Model Answer:**
- In a DC series motor, flux $\phi$ is produced by the same current $I_a$ that flows through the armature. Hence $\phi \propto I_a$ (linear region).
- The back-EMF equation is:

$$
E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = \frac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}
$$

- Therefore:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi} \propto \frac{1}{I_a}
$$

- At no-load, $I_a$ is very small (just magnetising current), so $\phi$ becomes extremely small. As $\phi \to 0$, $N \to \infty$ **theoretically**.
- Practically, the motor attains a dangerously high speed (called **runaway speed**), which can destroy the armature due to centrifugal forces. Hence, a minimum load **must** be present before starting — provided naturally by the brake drum in this experiment. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
**"What is the function of the spring balance in the rope-brake arrangement? What happens if it is omitted?"**

**Model Answer:**
- The spring balance reads the **slack-side rope tension** $S$ in kg.
- It allows accurate determination of the **net tangential frictional force** $F = (W_1 - S) \times 9.81$ N, because the two rope ends have unequal tensions due to friction.
- If the spring balance is omitted, the slack-side tension is unknown — only $W_1$ would be known, and we would incorrectly assume $F = W_1 \times 9.81$ N, leading to a **gross over-estimation of torque** (potentially 30–50 % error).
- For small motors, the spring balance also helps verify that $W_1 > S$; if not, the rope slips and the test must be re-tightened. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct a **brake test on a DC series motor**. List the instruments and precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were obtained during a brake test on a **220 V DC series motor**:

| $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (RPM) | $W_1$ (kg) | $S$ (kg) |
|-----------|-----------|------------|----------|
| 5 | 1800 | 1.5 | 0.2 |
| 10 | 1300 | 4.0 | 0.8 |
| 15 | 1050 | 7.5 | 1.5 |
| 20 | 900 | 12.0 | 3.0 |
| 25 | 780 | 17.0 | 5.0 |

Given effective drum radius $R = 0.1$ m and $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Plot the load characteristics** ($N$ vs $I_a$, $T_b$ vs $I_a$, $\eta$ vs $I_a$) and comment on the nature of each curve. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram Description:**
- The motor armature and series field are connected in **series** across a **DC supply** (rectifier-fed or battery).
- An **ammeter (A)** is connected in series with the line to measure $I_a$ (= $I_L$ for series motor).
- A **voltmeter (V)** is connected directly across the armature terminals to measure back-EMF.
- A **rheostat** (starting + field) is used to control voltage/current during start-up.
- A **DPST switch** with fuse protection isolates the motor from the supply.
- A **brake drum** is mounted on the motor shaft, with rope wound around it. One end carries a dead weight ($W_1$), the other a spring balance ($S$).
- A **tachometer** (digital/optical) measures speed $N$.

**Instruments Required:**
1. DC Voltmeter (0–300 V) — MI type
2. DC Ammeter (0–30 A) — MI type
3. Tachometer (0–5000 RPM)
4. Spring balance (0–25 kg)
5. Standard dead weights (calibrated, ±1 g)
6. Vernier caliper — to measure drum diameter
7. Starting rheostat, DPST switch, connecting wires

**Procedure (7 key steps):**
1. Note nameplate: $V$, $I$, $N$, kW, insulation class.
2. Measure drum diameter $D$ using vernier; compute $R_{\text{eff}} = D/2 + t/2$.
3. Make connections as per circuit diagram; insert starting resistance fully.
4. Apply a **minimum** dead weight (≈ 0.5 kg) to keep the motor from racing.
5. Close the switch; gradually cut out starting resistance to reach rated voltage.
6. Increase load in **5–6 equal steps** up to rated current; for each step record $V, I_a, N, W_1, S$.
7. Remove load gradually → switch off motor → tabulate and compute $T_b, P_{out}, P_{in}, \eta$.

**Precautions (any 4):**
1. **Never start without minimum load** — runaway risk.
2. The **spring balance reading should not go negative**; if it does, add more weights.
3. Apply load **gradually** in small steps; do not jerk the rope.
4. Keep the rope **dry and free from oil/grease** to ensure consistent friction.
5. The rope should be **flexible** but **strong enough** to bear the dead weights.
6. Do not run the motor above its **rated current**; otherwise commutator may flash over.
7. Use **effective radius** $R = D/2 + t/2$ (not bare $D/2$).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $T_b$ for each row using $T_b = 9.81 (W_1 - S) R$**

For $R = 0.1$ m, $g = 9.81$:

**Row 1:** $T_b = 9.81 \times (1.5 - 0.2) \times 0.1 = 9.81 \times 0.13 = 1.275$ N·m

**Row 2:** $T_b = 9.81 \times (4.0 - 0.8) \times 0.1 = 9.81 \times 0.32 = 3.139$ N·m

**Row 3:** $T_b = 9.81 \times (7.5 - 1.5) \times 0.1 = 9.81 \times 0.60 = 5.886$ N·m

**Row 4:** $T_b = 9.81 \times (12.0 - 3.0) \times 0.1 = 9.81 \times 0.90 = 8.829$ N·m

**Row 5:** $T_b = 9.81 \times (17.0 - 5.0) \times 0.1 = 9.81 \times 1.20 = 11.772$ N·m

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_b / 60$**

**Row 1:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi \times 1800 \times 1.275 / 60 = 240.4$ W

**Row 2:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi \times 1300 \times 3.139 / 60 = 427.4$ W

**Row 3:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi \times 1050 \times 5.886 / 60 = 647.5$ W

**Row 4:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi \times 900 \times 8.829 / 60 = 832.0$ W

**Row 5:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi \times 780 \times 11.772 / 60 = 962.0$ W

**Step 3 — Compute $P_{in} = V I_a$** (each row: $220 \times I_a$)

Row 1: 1100 W | Row 2: 2200 W | Row 3: 3300 W | Row 4: 4400 W | Row 5: 5500 W

**Step 4 — Compute $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100$**

Row 1: $240.4/1100 \times 100 = 21.86$ %
Row 2: $427.4/2200 \times 100 = 19.43$ %
Row 3: $647.5/3300 \times 100 = 19.62$ %
Row 4: $832.0/4400 \times 100 = 18.91$ %
Row 5: $962.0/5500 \times 100 = 17.49$ %

**Step 5 — Final Tabulated Results**

| $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (RPM) | $T_b$ (N·m) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| 5 | 1800 | 1.275 | 240.4 | 1100 | 21.86 |
| 10 | 1300 | 3.139 | 427.4 | 2200 | 19.43 |
| 15 | 1050 | 5.886 | 647.5 | 3300 | 19.62 |
| 20 | 900 | 8.829 | 832.0 | 4400 | 18.91 |
| 25 | 780 | 11.772 | 962.0 | 5500 | 17.49 |

**Step 6 — Graphs (Qualitative Description):**
1. **$N$ vs $I_a$:** Rectangular hyperbola — speed falls steeply at first, then more gradually as $I_a$ increases.
2. **$T_b$ vs $I_a$:** Parabola through the origin — torque rises as $I_a^2$.
3. **$P_{out}$ vs $I_a$:** Increases monotonically — power grows nearly linearly with $I_a$ in the practical operating region.
4. **$\eta$ vs $I_a$:** Rises sharply at low loads, reaches a maximum, then slowly falls (typical $\eta$ curve).

**Comments:**
- The $N$–$I_a$ curve is approximately hyperbolic as predicted by $N \propto 1/I_a$ (assuming unsaturated series field).
- The $T$–$I_a$ curve is approximately parabolic because $T \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ in the linear region.
- The efficiency peaks somewhere between no-load and full-load, beyond which copper losses dominate.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **braking torque** in a rope-brake test on a DC series motor. How is the **effective drum radius** determined, and why is it preferred over the bare radius? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a brake test on a **250 V, 1500 RPM, 10 A DC series motor**, the following readings were noted at a particular load: $W_1 = 6$ kg, $W_2 = 1$ kg, drum diameter $D = 20$ cm, rope diameter $t = 1$ cm, speed $N = 1400$ RPM. Calculate (i) effective radius, (ii) braking torque, (iii) shaft power output in kW, (iv) input power, and (v) efficiency at this load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Derivation of Braking Torque:**

Consider a brake drum of effective radius $R$ rotating about a horizontal axis. A rope wound around the drum has:

- Tight-side weight $W_1$ (kg) producing downward force $W_1 g$ N.
- Slack-side spring balance reading $S$ (kg) producing downward force $S g$ N.

The **net tangential force** on the drum is the difference (since one force aids rotation, the other opposes it through rope friction):

$$
F_{\text{net}} = (W_1 - S) g \ \text{N}
$$

This force, acting at radius $R$, generates a **torque** about the shaft axis:

$$
T_b = F_{\text{net}} \times R = (W_1 - S) g R
$$

Substituting $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
\boxed{T_b = 9.81 \ (W_1 - S) \ R \ \text{N·m}}
$$

**Effective Drum Radius:**

The rope is not a thin line but has a finite thickness $t$. It sits on the **outer surface** of the drum, so the actual point of contact is at a radius greater than $D/2$:

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{D}{2} + \frac{t}{2}
$$

**Why effective radius is preferred:**
- The tangential force $F_{\text{net}}$ acts at the **outer surface of the rope**, not the drum core.
- If we use $D/2$, we under-estimate $T_b$ by a margin of $t/2$, which is about **2.5 % for a 1 cm rope on a 20 cm drum** — significant for precise KTU record work.
- For large motors, the rope diameter may be 1.5–2 cm; the error becomes 5–10 %, which is **not acceptable** in laboratory-grade measurements.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- $V = 250$ V
- $I_a = 10$ A
- $N = 1400$ RPM
- $W_1 = 6$ kg
- $W_2 = S = 1$ kg
- $D = 20$ cm = $0.20$ m
- $t = 1$ cm = $0.01$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Effective Radius:**

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{D}{2} + \frac{t}{2} = \frac{0.20}{2} + \frac{0.01}{2} = 0.10 + 0.005 = 0.105 \ \text{m}
$$

**(ii) Braking Torque:**

$$
T_b = 9.81 \ (W_1 - S) \ R = 9.81 \times (6 - 1) \times 0.105
$$

$$
T_b = 9.81 \times 5 \times 0.105 = 5.150 \ \text{N·m}
$$

**(iii) Shaft Power Output:**

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T_b}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1400 \times 5.150}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{45{,}317.6}{60} = 755.3 \ \text{W} = 0.755 \ \text{kW}
$$

**(iv) Input Power:**

$$
P_{in} = V \times I_a = 250 \times 10 = 2500 \ \text{W} = 2.5 \ \text{kW}
$$

**(v) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{755.3}{2500} \times 100 = 30.21 \ \%
$$

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Effective drum radius | $R_{\text{eff}}$ | 0.105 | m |
| Braking torque | $T_b$ | 5.150 | N·m |
| Shaft output power | $P_{out}$ | 0.755 | kW |
| Input power | $P_{in}$ | 2.500 | kW |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | 30.21 | % |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Using $D/2$ instead of $(D+t)/2$** — loses **1 full mark** in calculations; examiners specifically check this in the record.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kgf to N** — writing $T_b = (W_1 - S) R$ without $9.81$ factor → unit error → **1 mark deduction**.
> 3. **Starting the motor without minimum load** — practical exam marker will immediately stop you and ask why; failure to justify with runaway-speed equation = **2 marks lost** in viva.
> 4. **Using $2\pi N T_b / 60$ but forgetting the $T_b$ value** — common arithmetic slip in the final step.
> 5. **Confusing $P_{out}$ with $P_{in}$** when plotting graphs — leads to inverted characteristic, which is **conceptually wrong**.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert to kW** when the question asks for kW — partial marking but **0.5 mark lost**.
> 7. **Wrong units in tables** (e.g., torque in kg-m instead of N·m) — examiners deduct for unit inconsistency.
> 8. **Forgetting the spring balance correction** — if the spring balance reading is not taken, the formula collapses and the entire calculation is **invalid**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Exam Day:**

- **Brake test = direct method** of loading; gives **output torque, output power, and efficiency** at any load.
- **DC series motor MUST be loaded before starting** — runaway speed is theoretically infinite.
- **Braking torque equation (memorize verbatim):** $T_b = 9.81 \, (W_1 - S) \, R$ N·m
- **Effective radius** = $D/2 + t/2$ — **always use this**, never bare $D/2$.
- **Output power:** $P_{out} = 2 \pi N T_b / 60$ W.
- **Input power (series motor):** $P_{in} = V \times I_a$ W (since $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$).
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100$ %.
- **Four mandatory graphs:** $N$ vs $I_a$ (rectangular hyperbola), $T$ vs $I_a$ (parabola), $P_{out}$ vs $I_a$ (rising), $\eta$ vs $I_a$ (peaked curve).
- **Spring balance purpose:** measures slack-side tension to find *net* frictional force.
- **Precautions (must write at least 4):** minimum-load start, dry rope, no negative spring balance reading, gradual loading, do not exceed rated current.
- **Speed equation (viva favourite):** $N = (V - I_a R_a - I_a R_{se}) / (k \phi)$; since $\phi \propto I_a$, we get $N \propto 1/I_a$.
- **Torque equation (viva favourite):** $T \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ in linear region.
- **Why series motor is used in traction:** high starting torque ($T \propto I_a^2$) and easy speed control via series-parallel connection.
- **Comparison: Brake test vs Swinburne test** — Brake test gives full-load efficiency directly, Swinburne's is no-load; for series motor, **brake test is preferred** because the motor cannot be safely run on no-load.
- **Sources of error:** rope thickness uncertainty, spring balance zero error, rope slippage, drum eccentricity, ambient temperature affecting resistance.
- **KTU viva hot questions:**
  - "What is runaway speed?" → Speed at which a series motor stabilises when no load; can be 5–6 times rated.
  - "Why $T \propto I_a^2$ for series motor?" → Because $\phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated) and $T \propto \phi I_a$.
  - "Can a DC series motor be used as a constant-speed motor?" → **No**, it is inherently a variable-speed motor.
  - "What is the role of the field winding in series motor?" → Carries full armature current, provides flux proportional to load.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load test on DC compound generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load test on DC compound generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DC compound generator** is a self-excited DC machine that employs **two field windings** — a **shunt field winding** (consisting of many turns of fine wire, connected in parallel with the armature) and a **series field winding** (consisting of a few turns of heavy gauge wire, connected in series with the armature/load) — wound on the same pole structure so that their **magnetic fluxes either aid (cumulative) or oppose (differential) each other**.

The **load test on a DC compound generator** is a direct experimental procedure used to determine the **external (load) characteristic**, i.e., the variation of **terminal voltage ($V_t$)** with **load current ($I_L$)**, while operating the machine at **rated constant speed ($N$)** as a self-excited generator, mechanically coupled to a **prime mover** (typically a DC shunt motor or 3-phase induction motor) and supplying a variable resistive/lampload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL407 - Module 2):**
> The lab manual defines this experiment as the most comprehensive performance-evaluation test for a self-excited DC generator because it simultaneously reveals **(a) voltage regulation behaviour**, **(b) magnetization state of the machine**, and **(c) the cumulative/differential compounding nature** of the field windings — all in a single run.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a water tap supplying a building:

- The **shunt field** is like a **small, always-on pump** pushing a *constant gentle stream* of water (flux) into the main pipe, regardless of how many taps are open in the house.
- The **series field** is like a **booster pump** that gets *stronger as more water is drawn* — more taps open → more current flows → booster kicks in harder.
