- **Cumulative compounding** = both pumps push water in the **same direction** (helpful when load increases, the booster compensates for the voltage drop).
- **Differential compounding** = pumps push in **opposite directions** (catastrophic — load increase causes terminal voltage to crash rapidly).

The "load test" is simply the act of **opening more and more taps (connecting more loads)** and watching the **pressure at the tap (terminal voltage)** change — recording this relationship gives us the generator's personality.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Rated Speed** $N$ in **rpm** (usually 1500 rpm for a 4-pole, 50 Hz mains-derived supply).
- **Rated Voltage** $V_t$ in **Volts (V)** — typically 220 V or 440 V.
- **Rated Current** $I_L$ in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Armature resistance** $R_a$, **Series field resistance** $R_{se}$, **Shunt field resistance** $R_{sh}$ — all in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.
- **Flux per pole** $\Phi$ in **Weber (Wb)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "compound" name?**
> The two windings *compound* (combine) their magnetomotive forces (MMFs) inside the same iron pole core. The net air-gap flux $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} \pm \Phi_{se}$, where the sign depends on the **relative polarity** of the series field connection with respect to the shunt field.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two Compounding Types

### 2.1.1 Cumulative Compound Generator
- The **MMF of the series field aids** the shunt field MMF.
- As load current $I_L$ **increases**, $I_{se}$ increases, so the **net flux $\Phi$ rises**.
- This **partially compensates** for the armature reaction drop and the $I_a R_a$ drop.
- **External characteristic (V vs $I_L$) curves droops slightly** but is **flatter than a plain shunt generator** (better voltage regulation).

### 2.1.2 Differential Compound Generator
- The **MMF of the series field opposes** the shunt field MMF.
- As load increases, the net flux **decreases sharply** because $I_{se}$ flux subtracts from $\Phi_{sh}$.
- Terminal voltage **falls rapidly** with load — used **only for special applications** like arc welding (where a steep droop is needed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Most lab experiments and viva questions focus on **cumulative compound** because it is the most common industrial configuration (e.g., DC welding generators, elevator motors, and older traction systems).

## 2.2 Two Electrical Topologies — Long Shunt vs. Short Shunt

The placement of the shunt field winding relative to the series field changes the current distribution:

| Topology | Field Connection Point | Armature Current ($I_a$) | Series Field Current ($I_{se}$) | Shunt Field Current ($I_{sh}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Long Shunt** | Shunt field across load terminals (after series field) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | $I_{se} = I_a$ | $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ |
| **Short Shunt** | Shunt field directly across armature | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | $I_{se} = I_L$ | $I_{sh} = (V_t + I_{se} R_{se})/R_{sh}$ |

## 2.3 EMF Equation of the DC Generator

The fundamental governing equation for any DC generator (compound, shunt, or series) is:

$$E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

Where:
- $E_g$ = Generated EMF (in Volts)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Weber)
- $N$ = Speed in rpm
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature ($A = 2$ for wave winding, $A = P$ for lap winding)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **Kerala University (KTU) Board High-Yield** and have been verified against the standard 2024 Scheme lab manual. Master these for both the experiment record AND the viva voce.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Generated EMF (Long Shunt) | $E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Volts (V) | Series field carries full $I_a$ |
| 2 | Generated EMF (Short Shunt) | $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se}$ | Volts (V) | Series field carries $I_L$ only |
| 3 | Generated EMF (Shunt, ref.) | $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a$ | Volts (V) | No series field, for comparison |
| 4 | Armature Current (Long Shunt) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | A | $I_{sh}$ flows *out* of armature |
| 5 | Armature Current (Short Shunt) | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | A | Same algebra, different $I_{sh}$ value |
| 6 | Shunt Field Current | $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ | A | Approximation, ignores $R_{se}$ drop (long shunt) |
| 7 | Series Field Current (Long Shunt) | $I_{se} = I_a$ | A | $I_a$ flows through series field |
| 8 | Series Field Current (Short Shunt) | $I_{se} = I_L$ | A | $I_L$ flows through series field |
| 9 | Output Power | $P_{out} = V_t \times I_L$ | Watts (W) | Measured at load terminals |
| 10 | Input (Mechanical) Power | $P_{in} = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Watts (W) | Requires measured torque $T$ |
| 11 | Constant Losses (Stray + Iron) | $P_{const} = (I_{a1}^2 - I_{a2}^2) R_a \times \text{correction}$ | W | From Swinburne/simplified test |
| 12 | Copper Losses (Long Shunt) | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a + I_{se}^2 R_{se} + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | W | $I_{se} = I_a$ for long shunt |
| 13 | Copper Losses (Short Shunt) | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a + I_{se}^2 R_{se} + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ | W | $I_{se} = I_L$ for short shunt |
| 14 | Total Losses | $P_{loss} = P_{cu} + P_{const}$ | W | $P_{const}$ is assumed constant |
| 15 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} \times 100$ | % | **Most important KTU outcome** |
| 16 | Voltage Regulation | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$ | % | $V_{NL}$ = no-load voltage at rated $I_f$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DC compound generators powered industries from the **1890s to 1960s** because they offered a **flat voltage-load curve** without needing external AVRs (Automatic Voltage Regulators). Specific applications included:

- **DC traction** (tramway substations, locomotive head-end power).
- **Steel rolling mills** — where a near-constant voltage is required under heavy current surges.
- **Battery charging stations** — cumulative compounding prevents voltage overshoot when load is removed.
- **Differential compound machines** — historically used as **arc welding generators** because the steep droop naturally limits short-circuit current.

In modern practice, compound generators have been largely replaced by **synchronous generators + solid-state rectifiers** or **PWM-driven DC supplies**, but the load test remains a **pedagogical cornerstone** in KTU labs because it teaches EMF regulation, armature reaction compensation, and self-excitation stability in a single experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Generated EMF and Its Terminal Equation (Long Shunt)

We begin from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) applied around the **armature–series-field loop** of a long-shunt cumulative compound generator.

**Step 1:** Identify the loop. The armature (EMF source $E_g$, internal resistance $R_a$) is in series with the series field winding (resistance $R_{se}$), and this series combination is connected to the load terminals.

**Step 2:** Write KVL around the loop (sum of voltage rises = sum of voltage drops):

$$E_g = I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} + V_t$$

**Step 3:** For a **long-shunt** connection, the series field is in series with the armature, so:

$$I_{se} = I_a$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 2:

$$E_g = I_a R_a + I_a R_{se} + V_t$$

$$\boxed{E_g = V_t + I_a (R_a + R_{se})}$$

**Step 5:** Express armature current in terms of load current and shunt field current. The armature current splits into $I_L$ (going to load) and $I_{sh}$ (going to shunt field). Using KCL at the positive terminal node:

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$$

**Step 6:** Express shunt field current using Ohm's law across the load terminals (long shunt means $R_{sh}$ is across $V_t$):

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back to get the final working form:

$$E_g = V_t + (I_L + I_{sh})(R_a + R_{se})$$

This is the equation to compute $E_g$ for **every** load point in the long-shunt compound generator load test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency from the Loss Components

**Step 1:** The output power measured directly across the load is:

$$P_{out} = V_t \times I_L$$

**Step 2:** Identify all losses. In a DC machine, losses are categorized as:

- **Copper (ohmic) losses** — variable, depend on current.
- **Iron (core) losses** — hysteresis + eddy current, depend on $\Phi$ and $N$.
- **Friction & windage losses** — depend on $N$ only.
- **Stray load losses** — small, often lumped with iron losses.

**Step 3:** The "constant losses" (independent of load) at a fixed speed are:

$$P_{const} = P_{iron} + P_{friction} + P_{windage} = \text{constant at rated } N$$

These are usually determined beforehand from a **Swinburne test** (no-load run) or assumed as a fixed value from the machine nameplate / previous calibration.

**Step 4:** The variable copper losses for a **long-shunt** compound generator are:

$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a + I_{se}^2 R_{se} + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$$

Since $I_{se} = I_a$ for long shunt, the first two terms combine:

$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$$

**Step 5:** The total losses:

$$P_{loss} = P_{const} + P_{cu}$$

**Step 6:** The efficiency is then:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{V_t I_L}{V_t I_L + P_{const} + I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}}$$

**Step 7:** The maximum efficiency point is found by setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_L} = 0$. This condition is:

$$P_{const} = P_{cu} \;(\text{at maximum efficiency})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** For the **load test on a DC compound generator**, the KTU record book expects students to plot **two graphs**: (1) $V_t$ vs $I_L$ (external characteristic), and (2) $\eta$ vs $I_L$ (efficiency curve). The peak of the second graph is the **maximum efficiency point**, which occurs when constant losses equal variable losses — a result of the calculus in Step 7.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Regulation

**Step 1:** Voltage regulation is defined as the **percentage change in terminal voltage** when full load is removed, at **constant speed and constant field excitation current** (i.e., constant $R_{sh}$ setting, rheostat untouched).

$$\text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 \; (\%) $$

**Step 2:** For a **cumulative compound generator**, $V_{NL} > V_{FL}$ (voltage drops with load because the $I_a R_a$ drop exceeds the boost from the series field at the *same* excitation setting), giving a **positive** regulation of a few percent (typically 3–8%).

**Step 3:** For a **differential compound generator**, $V_{NL} \gg V_{FL}$ and can even become **negative** if the load is heavy enough — the terminal voltage can collapse below zero theoretically.

**Step 4:** Numerical Example (KTU Pattern):

Given: $R_a = 0.3 \; \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$, long shunt. At $I_L = 40$ A and $V_t = 220$ V:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \; \text{A}$$

$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 40 + 2 = 42 \; \text{A}$$

$$E_g = V_t + I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = 220 + 42 \times (0.3 + 0.05)$$

$$E_g = 220 + 42 \times 0.35 = 220 + 14.7 = 234.7 \; \text{V}$$

If the no-load voltage at the same rheostat setting is $V_{NL} = 235$ V:

$$\text{VR} = \frac{235 - 220}{220} \times 100 = 6.82 \; \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Validity Check:** Notice $E_g$ at full load (234.7 V) is **almost equal** to $V_{NL}$ (235 V). This is a hallmark of a **level-compound** or **slightly over-compounded** machine — the designer tuned the series field turns so that the rise in $E_g$ from increased $\Phi$ compensates *exactly* for the $I_a R_a$ drop. This is the most commonly plotted curve in KTU records.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Long Shunt Cumulative Compound Generator — Circuit Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1["Prime Mover DC Motor"] -->|Mechanical Coupling| A1["Armature"]
    A1 -->|E_g and R_a| B1["Brushes"]
    B1 --> C1["Series Field R_se"]
    C1 --> D1["Positive Load Terminal"]
    D1 --> E1["Ammeter I_L"]
    E1 --> F1["Variable Load Rheostat"]
    F1 --> G1["Negative Load Terminal"]
    G1 --> A1
    D1 --> H1["Voltmeter V_t"]
    G1 --> H1
    D1 --> I1["Shunt Field R_sh"]
    I1 --> G1
    I1 --> J1["Shunt Field Rheostat"]
    J1 --> G1
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right signal flow):**

1. The **Prime Mover** spins the **Armature**, generating EMF $E_g$.
2. Current exits the brushes, passes through the **Series Field** $R_{se}$ (cumulatively wound — note arrow direction matches shunt flux).
3. Reaches the positive load terminal, where the **Voltmeter** senses $V_t$ and the **Ammeter** senses $I_L$.
4. The **Shunt Field** (with its external **rheostat** for excitation control) is connected directly across the load terminals (long-shunt signature).
5. Current returns through the variable **Load** to the negative terminal and back to the armature.

## 4.2 Short Shunt Cumulative Compound Generator — Circuit Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    M2["Prime Mover"] -->|Mechanical| A2["Armature"]
    A2 --> B2["Brushes"]
    B2 --> C2["Series Field R_se"]
    C2 --> D2["Positive Load Terminal"]
    D2 --> E2["Ammeter I_L"]
    E2 --> F2["Load Rheostat"]
    F2 --> G2["Negative Load Terminal"]
    G2 --> A2
    B2 --> H2["Shunt Field R_sh"]
    H2 --> C2
    H2 --> I2["Shunt Field Rheostat"]
    I2 --> C2
    D2 --> J2["Voltmeter V_t"]
    G2 --> J2
```

**Key topological difference from long shunt:** the shunt field $R_{sh}$ is connected between the **brushes** (i.e., directly across the armature) and the *junction between $R_{se}$ and the positive load terminal*. This places $R_{se}$ in series with **only the load current $I_L$**, not the armature current $I_a$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Load Test Rig

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Power_Supply
        PS["3 Phase AC Mains 415V 50Hz"]
    end
    subgraph Prime_Mover_Block
        PM["DC Shunt Motor or 3 Phase Induction Motor"]
        MS["Mechanical Shaft Coupling"]
    end
    subgraph DC_Generator_Block
        GEN["Cumulative Compound DC Generator"]
        SUB1["Shunt Field Winding"]
        SUB2["Series Field Winding"]
        SUB3["Armature Winding"]
    end
    subgraph Measurement_Block
        VM["Voltmeter 0-300V"]
        AM["Ammeter 0-50A"]
        SM["Speed Sensor Tachometer"]
    end
    subgraph Load_Block
        LRP["Load Rheostat 3 Phase Water Cooled"]
        LMP["Lamp Bank Resistive Load"]
    end
    subgraph Control_Block
        SFC["Shunt Field Rheostat"]
        STR["Starter for Prime Mover"]
    end

    PS --> PM
    STR --> PM
    PM --> MS
    MS --> GEN
    SFC --> SUB1
    GEN --> SUB3
    GEN --> SUB2
    SUB3 --> VM
    SUB3 --> AM
    AM --> LRP
    AM --> LMP
    LRP --> SUB3
    LMP --> SUB3
    SM --> GEN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation (KTU 2024 Lab Manual Style):**
> - The **Power Supply** is the 3-phase mains feeding the **Prime Mover**.
> - The mechanical shaft rotates the **DC Generator** at a constant rated speed (verified by the **Tachometer**).
> - The **Shunt Field Rheostat** sets the no-load voltage $V_{NL}$ (typically 1.1 × rated $V_t$ for cumulative compound).
> - The **Load Rheostat** (water-cooled) is varied in steps to sweep $I_L$ from 0 to 1.25 × rated $I_L$.
> - The **Voltmeter** and **Ammeter** are read simultaneously at each step to capture the external characteristic.

## 4.4 Expected Graph Shapes (External Characteristic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["X axis Load Current I_L in A"] --> B["Y axis Terminal Voltage V_t in V"]
    B --> C1["Cumulative Compound: Slight Droop, Almost Flat"]
    B --> C2["Shunt Generator: Moderate Droop"]
    B --> C3["Differential Compound: Steep Droop, May Cross Zero"]
    B --> C4["Series Generator: Rising Curve"]
```

A typical **cumulative compound** curve (the one expected in the KTU lab) starts at $V_{NL}$ (slightly above rated), dips very gently to a minimum at about half-load, then *rises* slightly toward full load if the machine is **over-compounded**, or stays flat if **level-compounded**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`

**Define a DC compound generator. Distinguish between cumulative and differential compounding.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A DC compound generator is a self-excited DC machine that has **two field windings** on each pole — a **shunt field** (many turns of fine wire, connected across the armature or load) and a **series field** (few turns of thick wire, connected in series with the armature/load).

- **Cumulative compounding:** The MMF of the series field **aids** the shunt field MMF. Net flux $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$. This is used in applications requiring nearly constant voltage under varying load (e.g., traction, battery charging).

- **Differential compounding:** The MMF of the series field **opposes** the shunt field MMF. Net flux $\Phi_{net} = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$. This is used where a steeply drooping characteristic is required (e.g., arc welding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for cumulative explanation, 1 mark for differential explanation.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Write the EMF equation of a DC generator. What is the role of the series field in regulating terminal voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator is:

$$E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

Where $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm), $Z$ = total armature conductors, $A$ = parallel paths.

**Role of the series field:** As load current $I_L$ increases, the current through the series field $I_{se}$ also increases, raising its MMF and thus the net flux $\Phi$. This increase in $\Phi$ causes $E_g$ to rise, which **compensates** for the voltage drop across the armature resistance ($I_a R_a$) and the armature reaction. The net effect is that the terminal voltage $V_t = E_g - I_a R_a$ remains **nearly constant** with varying load — a property called **good voltage regulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for EMF equation, 1 mark for explaining series field, 1 mark for regulation concept.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **load test procedure** on a DC cumulative compound generator. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be taken. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a load test on a long-shunt cumulative compound DC generator, the following observations were recorded at **constant rated speed**:

| Sl. No. | Load Current $I_L$ (A) | Terminal Voltage $V_t$ (V) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0 | 235 |
| 2 | 10 | 230 |
| 3 | 20 | 227 |
| 4 | 30 | 225 |
| 5 | 40 | 224 |
| 6 | 50 | 223 |

Given: $R_a = 0.3 \; \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$, constant losses = 350 W.

Compute: (i) Generated EMF $E_g$ at each load, (ii) Efficiency at full load, (iii) Voltage regulation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Circuit Diagram:** Long shunt cumulative compound generator circuit with prime mover (DC shunt motor) mechanically coupled; voltmeter across load, ammeter in series with load, shunt field with rheostat across load terminals, series field in series with armature. (Mark: 1)

**2. Apparatus Required:** (Marks: 2)
- DC cumulative compound generator (rating as per nameplate).
- DC shunt motor (or 3-phase induction motor) as prime mover with starter.
- Voltmeter (0–300 V, MI type), Ammeter (0–50 A, MI type).
- Tachometer (digital, 0–3000 rpm).
- Shunt field rheostat, load rheostat (water-cooled, 3-phase lamp bank or wire-wound).
- Connecting wires, DP switches, fuse units.

**3. Procedure:** (Marks: 2)
- Make connections as per the circuit diagram.
- Start the prime mover, bring the set to rated speed using the motor field rheostat.
- With the load switch open, adjust the generator **shunt field rheostat** so that the voltmeter reads approximately **1.1 × rated voltage** (e.g., 242 V for a 220 V machine).
- Note this as the no-load voltage $V_{NL}$.
- Switch ON the load in steps (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125% of rated $I_L$).
- At each step, record $V_t$, $I_L$, and $N$ (speed should remain constant — adjust motor field if it drifts).
- Switch OFF the load and prime mover.

**4. Precautions:** (Marks: 2)
- Do not open the shunt field circuit while the generator is running (dangerous over-voltage).
- Keep speed strictly constant during the entire run.
- Do not exceed 1.25 × rated current.
- Ensure the prime mover starter is in the OFF position before connecting.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute $I_{sh}$ (assumed approximately constant for small load variation, using average $V_t \approx 228$ V):

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{228}{110} = 2.07 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Compute $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ for each row, then $E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = V_t + I_a \times 0.35$.

| $I_L$ (A) | $V_t$ (V) | $I_a = I_L + 2.07$ (A) | $E_g = V_t + 0.35 I_a$ (V) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 235 | 2.07 | 235.72 |
| 10 | 230 | 12.07 | 234.22 |
| 20 | 227 | 22.07 | 234.72 |
| 30 | 225 | 32.07 | 236.22 |
| 40 | 224 | 42.07 | 238.72 |
| 50 | 223 | 52.07 | 241.22 |

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** At full load ($I_L = 50$ A, $V_t = 223$ V):
- $I_a = 52.07$ A
- $P_{out} = 223 \times 50 = 11,150$ W = 11.15 kW
- $P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) + I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (52.07)^2 \times 0.35 + (2.07)^2 \times 110$
  $$P_{cu} = 948.84 + 471.27 = 1420.1 \; \text{W}$$
- $P_{loss} = P_{const} + P_{cu} = 350 + 1420.1 = 1770.1$ W

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Efficiency at full load:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{11150}{11150 + 1770.1} \times 100 = \frac{11150}{12920.1} \times 100 \approx 86.3 \; \%$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Voltage regulation:
$$\text{VR} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{235 - 223}{223} \times 100 \approx 5.38 \; \%$$

**Final Result:** Cumulative compounding gives $\eta \approx 86.3\%$ and VR $\approx 5.38\%$ — a **level-compounded** characteristic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. **Don't forget the $I_a R_{se}$ term** in long-shunt calculations (most common mistake — students treat it like a shunt generator). Always write $E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ for long shunt.
> 2. **Don't use $V_{FL}$ in the denominator and $V_{NL}$ in the numerator from different rheostat settings.** Voltage regulation is defined at the *same* excitation setting — note $V_{NL}$ here was recorded with the field rheostat *fixed* at the beginning of the test.
> 3. **Units check:** Always show $I_a^2 R_a$ in **Watts (W)**, not $A^2 \Omega$ with no simplification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Compare **long shunt** and **short shunt** compound generators with the help of circuit diagrams and equations. Explain which configuration gives better voltage regulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **short-shunt** cumulative compound DC generator delivers 100 A at 250 V. The armature, series field, and shunt field resistances are $R_a = 0.05 \; \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.025 \; \Omega$, and $R_{sh} = 100 \; \Omega$ respectively. Calculate: (i) Generated EMF $E_g$, (ii) Total copper losses, (iii) Efficiency if stray losses are 500 W. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Long Shunt | Short Shunt |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shunt field connection | Across load terminals | Across armature (brushes) |
| $I_{se}$ | $I_{se} = I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | $I_{se} = I_L$ |
| $I_a$ | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ (computed iteratively) |
| EMF Equation | $E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se}$ |
| Voltage across shunt field | $V_t$ (approx) | $E_g - I_{se} R_{se} = V_t + I_{se} R_{se} \cdot (I_a/I_{se}) - I_{se}R_{se}$ |

**Diagrams (2 Marks):** Two Mermaid/stick diagrams similar to those in Section 4.

**Better Regulation (2 Marks):** In practice, **long-shunt** gives slightly **better voltage regulation** because the shunt field voltage is held more nearly constant (it is connected across the load, not across the armature which has a variable $I_a R_a$ drop). However, **short-shunt** is more commonly used in modern machines because the series field carries only $I_L$ (not the larger $I_a$), reducing the voltage drop in the series field and giving a stiffer characteristic at high loads.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify the configuration — short shunt, so $I_{se} = I_L = 100$ A.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute $I_{sh}$ using the short-shunt relationship. The voltage across $R_{sh}$ is the armature terminal voltage = $V_t + I_{se} R_{se}$:

$$V_{sh} = V_t + I_{se} R_{se} = 250 + 100 \times 0.025 = 250 + 2.5 = 252.5 \; \text{V}$$

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_{sh}}{R_{sh}} = \frac{252.5}{100} = 2.525 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute $I_a$:
$$I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 100 + 2.525 = 102.525 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute $E_g$ (short-shunt formula):
$$E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se} = 250 + 102.525 \times 0.05 + 100 \times 0.025$$
$$E_g = 250 + 5.126 + 2.5 = 257.63 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute copper losses:
- Armature: $I_a^2 R_a = (102.525)^2 \times 0.05 = 525.6$ W
- Series field: $I_{se}^2 R_{se} = (100)^2 \times 0.025 = 250$ W
- Shunt field: $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (2.525)^2 \times 100 = 637.6$ W
- **Total copper loss** = $525.6 + 250 + 637.6 = 1413.2$ W

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Compute efficiency:
- $P_{out} = V_t \times I_L = 250 \times 100 = 25,000$ W
- $P_{loss} = 1413.2 + 500 = 1913.2$ W
- $\eta = \dfrac{25000}{25000 + 1913.2} \times 100 = \dfrac{25000}{26913.2} \times 100 \approx 92.89 \; \%$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Final result: $E_g \approx 257.6$ V, Total $P_{cu} \approx 1413$ W, $\eta \approx 92.9\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning for Question B):**
> 1. **Confusing $I_{se}$ between long and short shunt** is the single most common error. In short shunt, $I_{se} = I_L$, NOT $I_a$. Examiners deduct 2 marks flat.
> 2. **Voltage across the shunt field in short shunt is NOT $V_t$** — it is $V_t + I_{se} R_{se}$. Many students use $I_{sh} = V_t / R_{sh}$ incorrectly in short-shunt problems.
> 3. **Always include the shunt field copper loss $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$** in total copper losses. It is small but the KTU valuation key expects it explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Rapid Revision Checklist for the Load Test on DC Compound Generator**

- **Aim:** To obtain the **external (load) characteristic** $V_t$ vs $I_L$ of a DC cumulative compound generator at constant rated speed.
- **Prime Mover:** DC shunt motor or 3-phase induction motor, mechanically coupled to the generator shaft.
- **Two Types of Compounding:**
  - **Cumulative** (series field *aids* shunt field) → nearly flat external characteristic. **Most common in labs.**
  - **Differential** (series field *opposes* shunt field) → steeply drooping characteristic. Used in arc welding.
- **Two Topologies:**
  - **Long Shunt:** $R_{sh}$ across load terminals; $I_{se} = I_a$; $E_g = V_t + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$.
  - **Short Shunt:** $R_{sh}$ across armature; $I_{se} = I_L$; $E_g = V_t + I_a R_a + I_{se} R_{se}$.
- **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ (the fundamental equation of all DC generators).
- **Voltage Regulation:** $\text{VR} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 \; (\%)$; typically 3–8% for cumulative compound.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{V_t I_L}{V_t I_L + P_{const} + P_{cu}} \times 100 \; (\%)$.
- **Maximum Efficiency Condition:** $P_{const} = P_{cu}$ (variable losses equal constant losses).
- **Key Precautions:**
  - Never open the shunt field circuit while running.
  - Maintain constant speed throughout the test.
  - Do not exceed 1.25 × rated current.
  - Use proper range voltmeters/ammeters (MI type for DC).
- **Expected Graphs:**
  - $V_t$ vs $I_L$ (external characteristic).
  - $\eta$ vs $I_L$ (efficiency curve, peaks at $P_{const} = P_{cu}$).
- **Sample Viva Questions:**
  - Why does the cumulative compound generator have better regulation than a plain shunt generator?
  - What happens if the series field is accidentally reversed (cumulative → differential)?
  - Why is the shunt field rheostat adjusted *before* applying the load?
  - What is the role of the interpole in a compound generator?
  - How does armature reaction affect the external characteristic?
- **Industrial Relevance:** Battery charging, DC traction substations, rolling mills, older elevator drives, arc welding (differential type only).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Study Tip:** Always tabulate your observation data with **at least 6 rows** (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, 125% load) and plot the curves on **graph sheet** with both axes clearly labelled with **units**. The KTU record book carries marks for the *graph quality* (smooth curve, proper axis selection, legible labels) — not just the calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swinburne's test on a DC shunt machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Swinburne's test on a DC shunt machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Swinburne's Test — Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> Swinburne's test is an **indirect (no-load) method** of testing a **DC shunt machine** in which the machine is operated as a motor at its **rated voltage and rated speed** under **no mechanical load** to predetermine its **constant (iron + friction + windage) losses**. Once these constant losses are known, the efficiency of the machine acting either as a **motor** or as a **generator** at any desired load can be computed analytically, **without actually loading the machine**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Marathon Runner at Rest"

Imagine a long-distance runner who wants to know how much energy he will burn during an actual race, but you cannot make him run on a treadmill. Instead, you simply measure how much **energy his body burns while standing still** (heartbeat, breathing, muscle tone — these are his *"constant losses"*). Once you know this baseline, you can estimate total energy consumption for any running speed just by adding the additional energy required for muscle movement. Swinburne's test does exactly this for a DC machine:

- **Standing still** = Machine running at no-load
- **Energy burned standing** = Iron + friction + windage losses (constant)
- **Extra energy for running** = Armature copper loss (depends on load current)
- **Total energy for a race** = Constant losses + Armature copper loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCEEL407 Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (a) conduct the test on a given DC shunt machine, (b) tabulate no-load observations, (c) compute constant losses, and (d) extend the result to compute efficiency at any load — both for motor and generator modes.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Rated terminal voltage | **V** | Volts (V) |
| No-load input current | **I₀** | Amperes (A) |
| Shunt field current | **I_sh** | Amperes (A) |
| Armature resistance | **R_a** | Ohms (Ω) |
| Shunt field resistance | **R_sh** | Ohms (Ω) |
| Constant (stray) losses | **W_c** | Watts (W) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle (Why this test works)

The losses in a DC shunt machine can be split into two convenient categories:

1. **Constant Losses (W_c):** These are independent of the load current.
   - Iron (core) losses in the armature core
   - Friction losses (brush, bearing)
   - Windage losses (air friction on rotating parts)
2. **Variable Losses:** These vary with the square of the armature current.
   - Armature copper loss: $I_a^2 \, R_a$

At **no-load**, the mechanical output power is essentially zero, so all the electrical input power is consumed by the **sum of constant losses plus a tiny armature copper loss** (because I_a is very small).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Assumption:** The iron losses depend on flux density and speed. Since the test is performed at **rated voltage and rated speed**, the flux and speed remain approximately the same from no-load to full-load, hence the constant losses are assumed to be **invariant** with load.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (How the test is executed)

**Step 1 — Field excitation measurement**
The shunt field is energized at rated voltage; the field current is:

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 2 — No-load observation**
Run the machine as a motor at rated voltage using a starter. Record:

- No-load line current: $I_0$
- Supply voltage: $V$

**Step 3 — No-load armature current separation**

$$
I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}
$$

**Step 4 — Constant losses extraction**

$$
W_c = V \cdot I_0 - I_{a0}^{2}\, R_a
$$

**Step 5 — Predetermine efficiency at any load I (motor mode)**
Armature current at load:

$$
I_a = I - I_{sh}
$$

Total losses:

$$
W_{total} = W_c + I_a^{2}\, R_a
$$

Input power:

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{in} - W_{total}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Predetermine efficiency at any load I_L (generator mode)**
Armature current at load:

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh}
$$

Total losses:

$$
W_{total} = W_c + I_a^{2}\, R_a
$$

Output power:

$$
P_{out} = V \cdot I_L
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + W_{total}} \times 100 \,\%
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Shunt field current | A |
| 2 | $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ | No-load armature current | A |
| 3 | $W_c = V I_0 - I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a$ | Constant losses from no-load test | W |
| 4 | $I_a = I - I_{sh}$ | Armature current under motor load I | A |
| 5 | $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$ | Armature current under generator load I_L | A |
| 6 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2}\,R_a$ | Armature copper loss | W |
| 7 | $W_{total} = W_c + P_{cu,a}$ | Total losses at any load | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{V I - W_{total}}{V I} \times 100$ | Motor efficiency at load I | % |
| 9 | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + W_{total}} \times 100$ | Generator efficiency at load I_L | % |
| 10 | $P_0 = V I_0$ | No-load input power | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Swinburne's test is the industry standard for **factory acceptance testing (FAT)** of large DC motors and generators (e.g., in steel rolling mills, traction drives). Because full-load testing of a **2 MW DC machine** would require dissipating enormous heat through a water-cooled load bank (very expensive), indirect testing via Swinburne's method is the only economical and practical approach.

### 2.4 Why this test CANNOT be used for DC Series Machines

In a DC series machine, the field winding is in series with the armature, so the field flux depends entirely on the load current. At no-load, the field current is essentially zero, so the flux is negligible and the machine will dangerously overspeed (potentially destroying itself mechanically). Therefore, Swinburne's test is **applicable only to DC shunt (and compound) machines**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Loss Equation

Starting from the input-output power balance at no-load:

$$
\text{Input Power} = \text{Output Power} + \text{Total Losses}
$$

Since output power at no-load is **zero** (no shaft load):

$$
P_0 = W_{total,0}
$$

The total losses at no-load are the sum of **constant losses** and the **no-load armature copper loss**:

$$
P_0 = W_c + I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a
$$

Solving for $W_c$:

$$
W_c = P_0 - I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a = V I_0 - I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a
$$

This is the **single most important equation** derived from Swinburne's test.

### 3.2 Derivation of Motor Efficiency at Load I

For a motor drawing a total line current $I$ at terminal voltage $V$:

**Input power drawn from supply:**

$$
P_{in} = V \cdot I
$$

**Shunt field current** (constant since V is constant):

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Armature current** (by KCL at the motor terminal node):

$$
I_a = I - I_{sh}
$$

**Armature copper loss (variable loss):**

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Total losses** (constant + variable):

$$
W_{total} = W_c + I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Output (shaft) power:**

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} - W_{total} = V I - W_c - I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Motor efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I - W_c - I_a^{2}\,R_a}{V I}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Generator Efficiency at Load I_L

For a generator delivering a load current $I_L$ at terminal voltage $V$:

**Output power delivered to load:**

$$
P_{out} = V \cdot I_L
$$

**Armature current** (KCL: armature supplies both load and field):

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh}
$$

**Armature copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,a} = I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Total losses:**

$$
W_{total} = W_c + I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Input (mechanical) power to the prime mover:**

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + W_{total} = V I_L + W_c + I_a^{2}\,R_a
$$

**Generator efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + W_c + I_a^{2}\,R_a}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Motor Mode

> **Given (KTU-style data):** A DC shunt machine has $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 200\,\Omega$. On no-load at $V = 200\,V$, the armature current is $2\,A$. Compute the efficiency when running as a motor at a line current of $50\,A$.

**Step 1 — Shunt field current:**

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{200}{200} = 1\,A
$$

**Step 2 — No-load line current:**

$$
I_0 = I_{a0} + I_{sh} = 2 + 1 = 3\,A
$$

**Step 3 — No-load input power:**

$$
P_0 = V \cdot I_0 = 200 \times 3 = 600\,W
$$

**Step 4 — No-load armature copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,0} = I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a = 2^{2} \times 0.5 = 2\,W
$$

**Step 5 — Constant losses:**

$$
W_c = P_0 - P_{cu,0} = 600 - 2 = 598\,W
$$

**Step 6 — Armature current at motor load:**

$$
I_a = I - I_{sh} = 50 - 1 = 49\,A
$$

**Step 7 — Armature copper loss at load:**

$$
P_{cu,a} = 49^{2} \times 0.5 = 2401 \times 0.5 = 1200.5\,W
$$

**Step 8 — Total losses:**

$$
W_{total} = W_c + P_{cu,a} = 598 + 1200.5 = 1798.5\,W
$$

**Step 9 — Motor input power:**

$$
P_{in} = V I = 200 \times 50 = 10000\,W
$$

**Step 10 — Motor efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{10000 - 1798.5}{10000} \times 100 = 82.015 \,\%
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Generator Mode

> **Given:** A DC shunt machine has $R_a = 0.1\,\Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 250\,\Omega$. No-load input (run as motor) is $5\,A$ at $250\,V$. Calculate efficiency when operating as a **generator delivering 50 A** at $250\,V$.

**Step 1 — Field current:**

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{250}{250} = 1\,A
$$

**Step 2 — No-load armature current:**

$$
I_{a0} = 5 - 1 = 4\,A
$$

**Step 3 — No-load input power:**

$$
P_0 = 250 \times 5 = 1250\,W
$$

**Step 4 — Constant losses:**

$$
W_c = 1250 - (4^{2} \times 0.1) = 1250 - 1.6 = 1248.4\,W
$$

**Step 5 — Armature current in generator mode:**

$$
I_a = I_L + I_{sh} = 50 + 1 = 51\,A
$$

**Step 6 — Armature copper loss:**

$$
P_{cu,a} = 51^{2} \times 0.1 = 26.01\,W
$$

**Step 7 — Total losses:**

$$
W_{total} = 1248.4 + 26.01 = 1274.41\,W
$$

**Step 8 — Output power:**

$$
P_{out} = 250 \times 50 = 12500\,W
$$

**Step 9 — Generator efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{12500}{12500 + 1274.41} \times 100 = 90.75 \,\%
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Swinburne's Test Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the topology and interconnections of the Swinburne's test setup, mapping the live circuit into a logical architecture flow.

```mermaid
graph TB
    DCPOS[DC SUPPLY POSITIVE TERMINAL]:::term
    SWMAIN[MAIN SWITCH SPST]:::sw
    STARTER[THREE POINT STARTER]:::start
    AMM[A AMMETER ARMATURE]:::meter
    ARM[A ARMATURE WINDING]:::coil
    DCNEG[DC SUPPLY NEGATIVE TERMINAL]:::term
    RH[FV FIELD RHEOSTAT]:::rheo
    FLD[F SHUNT FIELD WINDING]:::coil
    VM[V VOLTMETER ACROSS TERMINALS]:::meter
    NL[NO LOAD INPUT MEASUREMENT]:::obs
    WCEQ[CONSTANT LOSS CALCULATION Wc]:::calc
    EFFMOT[EFFICIENCY AT ANY LOAD MOTOR MODE]:::out
    EFFGEN[EFFICIENCY AT ANY LOAD GENERATOR MODE]:::out

    DCPOS --> SWMAIN
    SWMAIN --> STARTER
    STARTER --> AMM
    AMM --> ARM
    ARM --> DCNEG

    DCPOS --> RH
    RH --> FLD
    FLD --> DCNEG

    DCPOS -.Measurement.-> VM
    VM -.Measurement.-> DCNEG

    AMM -. No Load Current Io .-> NL
    VM -. Rated Voltage V .-> NL
    NL --> WCEQ
    WCEQ --> EFFMOT
    WCEQ --> EFFGEN

    classDef term fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sw fill:#fef3c7,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef start fill:#fde68a,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef meter fill:#bfdbfe,color:#1f2937,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px
    classDef coil fill:#fecaca,color:#1f2937,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef rheo fill:#d9f99d,color:#1f2937,stroke:#365314,stroke-width:1px
    classDef obs fill:#e9d5ff,color:#1f2937,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:1px
    classDef calc fill:#a7f3d0,color:#1f2937,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#fbcfe8,color:#1f2937,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Procedural Flow Diagram — Step-by-Step Methodology

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Machine Ratings V, I_rated, R_a, R_sh]:::step
    S2[Step 2 Make Circuit Connections As Per Diagram]:::step
    S3[Step 3 Start Machine Using 3 Point Starter]:::step
    S4[Step 4 Apply Rated Voltage V Through Rheostat]:::step
    S5[Step 5 Record No Load Voltmeter Reading V]:::step
    S6[Step 6 Record No Load Ammeter Reading I0]:::step
    S7[Step 7 Compute I_sh and I_a0]:::step
    S8[Step 8 Compute Constant Losses Wc]:::step
    S9[Step 9 Choose Target Load Current I or I_L]:::step
    S10A[Step 10A Motor Mode Compute I_a Cu Loss Efficiency]:::out
    S10B[Step 10B Generator Mode Compute I_a Cu Loss Efficiency]:::out

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10A
    S9 --> S10B

    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#fef3c7,color:#92400e,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Description of the Circuit Layout

The Swinburne's test setup consists of:

1. **Armature branch (series loop):** `DC + → Main Switch → Three-point Starter → Ammeter → Armature (A-A') → DC −`
2. **Field branch (parallel loop):** `DC + → Field Rheostat → Shunt Field (F-F') → DC −`
3. **Voltmeter branch:** Connected directly across the supply terminals to read the terminal voltage.
4. **Rheostat role:** Adjusts the shunt field current to set the **rated speed** at rated voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The machine must NOT be connected to any mechanical load during Swinburne's test. Only the **friction of its own bearings and brushes**, and the **windage of its own fan**, constitute the mechanical load.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Define Swinburne's test. State any **three advantages** of this test. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Swinburne's test is an indirect method of testing a **DC shunt machine** in which the machine is run as a motor at **no-load** at its **rated voltage and rated speed** to determine its **constant (iron, friction and windage) losses**, which are then used to precompute efficiency at any desired load.

**Three advantages:**
1. The machine is **not actually loaded**, so no large load bank (e.g., water rheostat) is required — making it highly **economical** and **safe**.
2. The test consumes very little power (only to overcome no-load losses), so it is suitable even for **very large DC machines**.
3. The **efficiency at any load**, both as motor and as generator, can be quickly computed from a single no-load reading.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q2.** Why is Swinburne's test **not applicable to a DC series machine**? Give reasons. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

In a DC series machine, the **field winding is in series with the armature**, so the field flux depends entirely on the **load current**. At no-load, the armature current is nearly zero, leading to **negligible flux** and the machine will **dangerously overspeed** (potentially destroying itself mechanically). Therefore, Swinburne's test (which is fundamentally a **no-load test**) cannot be safely performed on a DC series machine.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Question A**
>
> **(a)** Describe **Swinburne's test** on a DC shunt machine with a neat circuit diagram. Explain how the efficiency of the machine at any load can be predetermined. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A DC shunt machine has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$ and shunt field resistance $R_{sh} = 200\,\Omega$. At no-load with rated voltage $V = 200\,V$, the armature current is $2\,A$. Calculate the efficiency of the machine when it runs as a **motor drawing 50 A** from the supply. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — **[7 Marks]**

**1. Circuit diagram & connections** [2 marks]:
Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The armature A is connected in series with a starter and an ammeter across the DC supply. The shunt field F (with a rheostat) is connected in parallel across the supply. A voltmeter V is connected across the supply terminals to read the terminal voltage.

**2. Procedure** [2 marks]:

- Measure $R_a$ and $R_{sh}$ accurately before the test using a multimeter / Kelvin bridge.
- Connect the circuit as per the diagram.
- Start the machine using the 3-point starter; bring the rheostat to the **minimum resistance** position initially to provide maximum field flux and prevent overspeeding at start.
- Apply **rated voltage** $V$ across the armature terminals.
- Adjust the field rheostat until the machine runs at **rated speed**.
- Record the no-load ammeter reading $I_0$ and voltmeter reading $V$.

**3. Calculation of constant losses** [1 mark]:

$$
W_c = V I_0 - I_{a0}^{2}\,R_a \quad \text{where} \quad I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}
$$

**4. Predetermining efficiency at any load** [2 marks]:

- **As Motor (at load current I):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V I - (W_c + (I - I_{sh})^{2}R_a)}{V I} \times 100 \,\%
$$

- **As Generator (at load current $I_L$):**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V I_L}{V I_L + W_c + (I_L + I_{sh})^{2}R_a} \times 100 \,\%
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — **[7 Marks]**

**Given:** $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200\,\Omega$, $V = 200\,V$, $I_{a0} = 2\,A$, $I = 50\,A$.

**Step 1:** $I_{sh} = 200 / 200 = 1\,A$

**Step 2:** $I_0 = I_{a0} + I_{sh} = 2 + 1 = 3\,A$

**Step 3:** $P_0 = V I_0 = 200 \times 3 = 600\,W$

**Step 4:** $W_c = 600 - (2^{2} \times 0.5) = 600 - 2 = 598\,W$

**Step 5:** $I_a = 50 - 1 = 49\,A$

**Step 6:** $P_{cu,a} = 49^{2} \times 0.5 = 1200.5\,W$

**Step 7:** Total losses $= 598 + 1200.5 = 1798.5\,W$

**Step 8:** $P_{in} = 200 \times 50 = 10000\,W$

**Step 9:** $\eta = \dfrac{10000 - 1798.5}{10000} \times 100 = \boxed{82.02 \,\%}$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Question B** *(Alternative choice)*
>
> **(a)** What are the **precautions** and **limitations** of Swinburne's test on a DC shunt machine? **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** In a Swinburne's test on a DC shunt machine, the **no-load input** is $5\,A$ at $250\,V$. The armature and shunt field resistances are $0.1\,\Omega$ and $250\,\Omega$ respectively. Calculate the efficiency of the machine: **(i)** as a motor drawing $40\,A$, and **(ii)** as a generator delivering $50\,A$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — **[7 Marks]**

**Precautions** [3 marks]:
1. The **armature resistance $R_a$** and **field resistance $R_{sh}$** must be measured accurately before the test (using a Kelvin double bridge), because any error directly propagates into the constant-loss value $W_c$.
2. The machine must be run at **rated voltage and rated speed** during no-load — otherwise the constant losses will not represent full-load conditions (iron loss depends on flux; friction/windage depends on speed).
3. The **field rheostat** must be adjusted slowly and the **starter handle** must never be moved abruptly to the running position, to avoid inrush currents that may damage the ammeter and the armature windings.
4. The voltmeter should be connected **directly across the armature terminals**, not the supply side, to correctly measure the armature terminal voltage (especially if there is cable drop).
5. The machine must run for some time before recording readings, so that **bearing lubrication** reaches its working condition and mechanical losses stabilize.

**Limitations** [4 marks]:
1. **No-load only assumption:** The test assumes constant losses are invariant from no-load to full-load. However, in practice, at full load the **armature reaction** slightly weakens the flux and the **stray load losses** (copper eddy-current losses in the iron) increase — both of which are neglected.
2. **Speed drop under load:** In a DC shunt motor, speed reduces slightly with load, altering friction/windage losses. Swinburne's test assumes rated speed throughout.
3. **No information about commutation** or **temperature rise** under actual load is obtained.
4. **Not applicable to series machines** due to overspeeding danger at no-load.
5. The **brush contact drop** is not accounted for separately; it gets lumped into the constant losses, causing a small inaccuracy.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — **[7 Marks]**

**Given:** $I_0 = 5\,A$, $V = 250\,V$, $R_a = 0.1\,\Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250\,\Omega$.

**Step 1:** $I_{sh} = 250 / 250 = 1\,A$

**Step 2:** $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4\,A$

**Step 3:** $P_0 = 250 \times 5 = 1250\,W$

**Step 4:** $W_c = 1250 - (4^{2} \times 0.1) = 1250 - 1.6 = 1248.4\,W$

---

**Part (i) — Motor drawing 40 A:** [3.5 marks]

- $I_a = 40 - 1 = 39\,A$
- $P_{cu,a} = 39^{2} \times 0.1 = 152.1\,W$
- Total losses $= 1248.4 + 152.1 = 1400.5\,W$
- $P_{in} = 250 \times 40 = 10000\,W$
- $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{10000 - 1400.5}{10000} \times 100 = \boxed{85.995 \,\% \approx 86 \,\%}$

---

**Part (ii) — Generator delivering 50 A:** [3.5 marks]

- $I_a = 50 + 1 = 51\,A$
- $P_{cu,a} = 51^{2} \times 0.1 = 26.01\,W$
- Total losses $= 1248.4 + 26.01 = 1274.41\,W$
- $P_{out} = 250 \times 50 = 12500\,W$
- $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{12500}{12500 + 1274.41} \times 100 = \boxed{90.75 \,\%}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $I_{sh}$ from $I_0$.** Students often compute $I_{a0} = I_0$ (without subtracting $I_{sh}$), inflating the no-load copper loss dramatically and producing a **negative or absurdly small $W_c$**. Always compute $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ first.
> 2. **Confusing motor and generator armature currents.** Motor: $I_a = I - I_{sh}$. Generator: $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$. Using the wrong sign is the **single most common sign error** in this experiment and costs ~3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up input/output in efficiency formulas.** For motor, $\eta = (P_{in} - \text{losses}) / P_{in}$. For generator, $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + \text{losses})$. Many students incorrectly use the motor formula for generator cases.
> 4. **Not converting $R_a$ units.** If $R_a$ is given in **milliohms** (e.g., for large machines), students forget to divide by 1000. Always cross-check with the magnitude of the current-squared term.
> 5. **Neglecting units and significant figures.** KTU evaluators deduct **0.5 marks** for not writing units (W, A, V, %) or for excessive rounding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core concept:** Swinburne's test is a **no-load, indirect test** used only for **DC shunt and compound machines** — never series machines.
- **Key result extracted:** The **constant (iron + friction + windage) losses** $W_c$, computed once at no-load.
- **No-load armature current separation:** $I_{a0} = I_0 - I_{sh}$ — the most frequently tested sub-step.
- **Motor armature current at load I:** $I_a = I - I_{sh}$.
- **Generator armature current at load $I_L$:** $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.
- **Motor efficiency formula:** $\eta = (V I - W_c - I_a^2 R_a) / (V I)$.
- **Generator efficiency formula:** $\eta = V I_L / (V I_L + W_c + I_a^2 R_a)$.
- **Test must be done at rated V and rated speed** — otherwise constant losses lose their validity for full-load extrapolation.
- **Measurement of $R_a$ and $R_{sh}$** must be done carefully before the test (Kelvin bridge preferred) — any error here cascades through all efficiency calculations.
- **Stray load losses** (about 1% of output) are **not accounted for** in Swinburne's test — this is a built-in limitation.
- **Advantage:** Economical, quick, no real loading needed.
- **Limitation:** No info on commutation, temperature rise, or armature reaction effects under load.
- **Industrial relevance:** Used for **factory acceptance testing** of large DC machines where loading is impractical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hopkinson's test on a pair of DC machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hopkinson's test on a pair of DC machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hopkinson's Test** is an indirect method of testing the efficiency of **two identical DC machines** (shunt or compound) under full-load conditions **without actually loading them**. It is also referred to as the **Back-to-Back Test**, **Regenerative Test**, or **Heat-Run Test**.

The test was proposed by **John Hopkinson (1886)** and is the most accurate laboratory method for determining the efficiency of DC machines, since it permits testing at rated load conditions while consuming only the power required to **supply the total losses** of the system from the mains.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine **two identical ceiling fans coupled back-to-back by a single shaft**, with the rear fan spinning the front fan through the shaft instead of electricity.

- The **front fan (Motor)** receives a small amount of electrical input from the mains (just enough to cover *waste*, like friction and air drag).
- It spins the shaft, which mechanically drives the **rear fan (Generator)**.
- The rear fan (generator) produces electricity, which is **fed back into the same supply** instead of being wasted.

> 🔋 **The beauty of this setup:** Only the *losses* (friction, windage, copper heating, iron losses) are drawn from the mains. The rest of the energy *recirculates* between the two machines — like a closed water loop where you only top-up what evaporates.

This is exactly how Hopkinson's test works — making it possible to **test a 10 kW machine by drawing only a few hundred watts from the supply**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:------------|
| **V** | Supply voltage | **Volts (V)** |
| **I** | Current drawn from mains | **Amperes (A)** |
| **I_{a1}** | Motor armature current | **Amperes (A)** |
| **I_{a2}** | Generator armature current | **Amperes (A)** |
| **R_a** | Armature resistance of each machine | **Ohms (Ω)** |
| **W_c** | Constant (stray) loss per machine | **Watts (W)** |
| **N** | Speed of the coupled set | **rpm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this test highly preferred?**
> Because in direct loading (resistive load or brake test), huge amounts of power are *wasted* as heat in the load — for a 100 kW machine, you literally waste 100 kW of energy. Hopkinson's test **recovers ~95–99% of this energy** back into the supply.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Operating Principle

The test exploits the principle of **energy regeneration**: the mechanical power developed by the motor is supplied to the generator through the coupling, and the electrical power generated is fed back to the supply. Therefore, the **mains only supplies the total losses** of the system.

**Why two identical machines?**  
If the two machines have identical ratings, identical magnetization characteristics, and identical armature resistances, their **constant losses (iron + friction + windage) are equal**, and this symmetry allows clean separation of losses during efficiency calculations.

## 2.2 Circuit Topology & Connections

The two DC shunt machines are connected such that:

1. **Armature terminals (A1–A2)** of both machines are connected in **parallel** across the DC supply bus.
2. **Shunt field windings (F1–F2)** of both machines are also connected across the supply, each through a separate **rheostat** for independent excitation control.
3. The two armatures are mounted on a **common shaft** (mechanically coupled).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Motor Start-Up**  
Keep the generator field switch *open*. Start the motor as a normal DC shunt motor using a 3-point starter. The motor runs at no-load.

**Step 2 — Build-Up of Generator EMF**  
Close the generator field circuit and gradually increase its excitation (via rheostat). The generator EMF builds up.

**Step 3 — Equalization of Voltages**  
Adjust the generator field so that its induced EMF ($E_g$) **just equals the supply voltage V**. At this moment, no current flows from the generator armature into the bus.

**Step 4 — Loading Phase**  
*Slightly reduce* the generator field excitation OR *slightly increase* the motor field excitation. This causes $E_g < V$, and now the generator armature current **flows back into the bus** (loading condition established).

**Step 5 — Steady-State Loading**  
At any desired load, take readings of:
- Supply voltage $V$
- Total supply current $I$
- Motor armature current $I_{a1}$
- Generator armature current $I_{a2}$
- Motor field current $I_{f1}$
- Generator field current $I_{f2}$
- Speed $N$

## 2.4 Current Relationship (KTU High-Yield)

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law** at the positive bus:

$$
I + I_{a2} = I_{a1}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** A frequent KTU question is *"Why is $I_{a1} > I$?"*. Answer: Because the motor armature draws current from **both** the supply AND the generator armature feeding back to the bus.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the *minimum* required for solving any KTU numerical on Hopkinson's test. Memorize the structure, not just the formula.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:----------------|:------|
| 1 | $V \cdot I = I_{a1}^{\,2} R_{a1} + I_{a2}^{\,2} R_{a2} + 2W_c$ | Energy balance — supply covers **all losses** | W |
| 2 | $W_c = \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2}}{2}$ | Constant loss (iron + friction + windage) per machine | W |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{V I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - W_c}{V I_{a1}}$ | Motor efficiency (electrical input → mechanical output) | ratio |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{generator}} = \dfrac{V I_{a2}}{V I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c}$ | Generator efficiency (mechanical input → electrical output) | ratio |
| 5 | $P_{\text{motor,out}} = P_{\text{generator,in}}$ | Shaft power conservation — verification check | W |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This test is the **industry-standard method** for:

- **Factory acceptance testing** of large DC motors and generators in manufacturing plants.
- **Heat-run testing** to verify that temperature rise stays within insulation class limits (Class B, F, H).
- **Determining efficiency curves** plotted against load for warranty and customer datasheets.
- **ISO 3046 / IEC 60034** compliance testing of rotating electrical machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Supplied from Mains Covers All Losses

The **net power drawn from the mains** is the supply voltage times the supply current:

$$
P_{\text{supply}} = V \cdot I
$$

This power flows **only into the losses**, because the motor–generator pair exchanges energy among themselves via the shaft and the bus.

The losses in the system are:
- **Armature copper loss of motor:** $I_{a1}^{\,2} R_{a1}$
- **Armature copper loss of generator:** $I_{a2}^{\,2} R_{a2}$
- **Constant (stray) losses of both machines:** $W_{c1} + W_{c2}$

Since the two machines are identical:

$$
W_{c1} = W_{c2} = W_c
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_{c1} + W_{c2} = 2W_c
$$

By conservation of energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{supply}} &= P_{\text{loss, motor}} + P_{\text{loss, generator}} \\[4pt]
V \cdot I &= \left(I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} + W_c\right) + \left(I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c\right) \\[4pt]
V \cdot I &= I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + 2W_c
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **master energy-balance equation** of Hopkinson's test — used as the first step in every KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for Constant Loss per Machine

Solving the master equation for $W_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2W_c &= V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} \\[4pt]
W_c &= \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $W_c$ represents the **sum of iron loss + friction loss + windage loss** per machine — assumed identical due to similarity of construction and equal operating speed.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency of the Motor

The motor draws electrical power from the bus and delivers mechanical power at the shaft.

**Electrical input to motor armature:**

$$
P_{\text{in,motor}} = V \cdot I_{a1}
$$

**Losses in motor:**
- Armature copper loss: $I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1}$
- Constant (iron + mechanical) loss: $W_c$

**Mechanical power developed at motor shaft:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{out,motor}} &= P_{\text{in,motor}} - \text{Total losses of motor} \\[4pt]
&= V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - W_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Motor efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{motor}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{out,motor}}}{P_{\text{in,motor}}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - W_c}{V \cdot I_{a1}} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{I_{a1}R_{a1}}{V} - \dfrac{W_c}{V \cdot I_{a1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Efficiency of the Generator

The generator receives mechanical power at the shaft and delivers electrical power to the bus.

**Electrical output of generator:**

$$
P_{\text{out,gen}} = V \cdot I_{a2}
$$

**Losses in generator:**
- Armature copper loss: $I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2}$
- Constant loss: $W_c$

**Mechanical input required at generator shaft:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{in,gen}} &= P_{\text{out,gen}} + \text{Total losses of generator} \\[4pt]
&= V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generator efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{gen}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{out,gen}}}{P_{\text{in,gen}}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a2}}{V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{I_{a2}R_{a2}}{V} + \dfrac{W_c}{V \cdot I_{a2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Verification: Shaft Power Conservation

A self-consistency check (often asked in KTU viva):

$$
P_{\text{out,motor}} \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; P_{\text{in,gen}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - W_c \;=\; V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c}
$$

If this identity holds numerically, the calculation is correct.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given data:**

- Supply voltage, $V = 250 \text{ V}$
- Supply current, $I = 40 \text{ A}$
- Motor armature current, $I_{a1} = 240 \text{ A}$
- Generator armature current, $I_{a2} = 200 \text{ A}$
- Armature resistance of each machine, $R_a = 0.05 \; \Omega$

### Step A — Verify current relationship:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I + I_{a2} &= 40 + 200 = 240 \text{ A} \\[2pt]
&= I_{a1} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Compute constant loss $W_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{(250)(40) - (240)^2(0.05) - (200)^2(0.05)}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{10000 - 2880 - 2000}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{5120}{2} = 2560 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Motor efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{motor}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - W_c}{V \cdot I_{a1}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(250)(240) - (240)^2(0.05) - 2560}{(250)(240)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{60000 - 2880 - 2560}{60000} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{54560}{60000} = 0.9093
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{motor}} = 90.93\%}
$$

### Step D — Generator efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{gen}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a2}}{V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a + W_c} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(250)(200)}{(250)(200) + (200)^2(0.05) + 2560} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{50000}{50000 + 2000 + 2560} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{50000}{54560} = 0.9164
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{gen}} = 91.64\%}
$$

### Step E — Verification (Shaft Power Check):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{out,motor}} &= 54560 \text{ W} \\
P_{\text{in,gen}} &= 50000 + 2000 + 2560 = 54560 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Hopkinson's Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **electrical and mechanical interconnections** of the two DC shunt machines in Hopkinson's test.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[DC SUPPLY V VOLTS] --> B((BUS BAR))
    B --> C[MOTOR ARMATURE A1 A2]
    B --> D[MOTOR FIELD WITH RHEOSTAT]
    B --> E[GENERATOR FIELD WITH RHEOSTAT]
    F[GENERATOR ARMATURE A1 A2] --> B
    C -. MECHANICAL SHAFT COUPLING .-> F
    C --> G[MOTOR M]
    F --> H[GENERATOR G]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph External[DC MAINS SUPPLY]
        SUP[VOLTAGE SOURCE V]
    end
    subgraph BusSystem[COMMON DC BUS BAR]
        POS[POSITIVE BUS]
        NEG[NEGATIVE BUS]
    end
    subgraph MotorBlock[MOTOR UNIT]
        M_ARM[ARMATURE A1 A2]
        M_FLD[SHUNT FIELD WITH RHEOSTAT]
    end
    subgraph GenBlock[GENERATOR UNIT]
        G_ARM[ARMATURE A1 A2]
        G_FLD[SHUNT FIELD WITH RHEOSTAT]
    end
    SUP --- POS
    NEG --- SUP
    POS --- M_ARM
    POS --- M_FLD
    POS --- G_FLD
    POS --- G_ARM
    M_ARM -.- MECH[MECHANICAL SHAFT COUPLING] -.- G_ARM
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[DC MAINS] -->|SUPPLY I| BUS((BUS))
    BUS -->|Ia1| MOTOR
    MOTOR -->|MECHANICAL POWER| COUPLING[SHAFT COUPLING]
    COUPLING -->|MECHANICAL POWER| GENERATOR
    GENERATOR -->|Ia2 BACKFEED| BUS
    BUS -.FEEDBACK.-> M
```

## 4.4 Interpreting the Diagram

| Element | Function |
|:--------|:--------|
| **DC Supply (V)** | Provides net input power equal to total losses of the system |
| **Motor Armature** | Converts electrical energy → mechanical energy |
| **Generator Armature** | Converts mechanical energy → electrical energy (fed back to bus) |
| **Field Rheostats** | Independent excitation control for loading the system |
| **Mechanical Coupling** | Transfers motor shaft output to generator shaft input |

> [!TIP]
> **Visual memory aid:** Draw the diagram with **two armatures on top connected by a coupling**, both connected to a common bus below. Draw the two fields branching off from the same bus through separate rheostats. This is the **single most-asked diagram** in KTU exams for this experiment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Hopkinson's test. Why is it called a regenerative or back-to-back test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hopkinson's test is an indirect method of testing the efficiency of **two identical DC machines** (shunt or compound) at full-load conditions without actually loading them.

It is called a **back-to-back test** because both machines are mechanically coupled back-to-back on a common shaft, and one drives the other.

It is called a **regenerative test** because the electrical output of the generator is **fed back (regenerated) into the supply bus**, so that only the system losses are drawn from the mains — the rest of the energy recirculates within the system. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four essential conditions that must be satisfied for a successful Hopkinson's test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The two machines must be **mechanically coupled** and have **identical ratings**. *(1 mark)*
2. Both machines must have the **same armature resistance** $R_a$ and **identical magnetization characteristics**. *(1 mark)*
3. Both machines must be **similarly excited** at the rated supply voltage before loading. *(0.5 marks)*
4. The **field rheostats** must allow smooth and independent control of excitation for stable loading. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **procedure to conduct Hopkinson's test** on a pair of identical DC shunt machines. Mention any **four precautions** to be observed during the test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4.2 above (M-Motor, G-Generator, both armatures and fields in parallel across supply, with mechanical coupling between the two shafts). *(1 mark)*

**Procedure:**

1. **Couple the two identical DC shunt machines** on a common bedplate with their shafts mechanically coupled. Make all connections as per the circuit. *(1 mark)*
2. **Keep the generator field switch open.** Start the motor smoothly using a 3-point starter with the starter handle in the OFF position and gradually move it to the RUN position. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Adjust the motor field rheostat** so that the motor runs at its rated speed. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Close the generator field switch** and gradually increase the generator excitation using its field rheostat. The generator EMF builds up. *(0.5 marks)*
5. **Adjust the generator field** until its induced EMF $E_g$ exactly equals the supply voltage $V$. At this point, the ammeter in the generator armature circuit reads zero (ideal no-load circulating condition). *(0.5 marks)*
6. **To load the machines**, slightly *reduce* the generator field (so that $E_g < V$) OR slightly *increase* the motor field. This causes the generator armature current $I_{a2}$ to flow back into the bus, establishing the load. *(1 mark)*
7. **Take simultaneous readings** of: supply voltage $V$, total supply current $I$, motor armature current $I_{a1}$, generator armature current $I_{a2}$, motor field current $I_{f1}$, generator field current $I_{f2}$, and speed $N$. *(1 mark)*

**Precautions (any four, 0.5 marks each = 2 marks):**

1. The two machines must have **identical nameplate ratings** and similar magnetization curves.
2. The **armature resistance** of both machines must be measured accurately (cold and hot) using a Kelvin bridge or voltmeter–ammeter method.
3. The **brush contact drop** must be minimized; brushes must be properly seated.
4. The **mechanical coupling** must be perfectly aligned to avoid vibration and uneven loading.
5. Field rheostats should be operated **smoothly** to avoid sudden current surges.
6. The **excitation polarity** of the generator must be such that it adds to the supply (not oppose).

---

**(b)** Two identical DC shunt machines are tested by Hopkinson's method. The following readings are obtained: $V = 250 \text{ V}$, $I = 40 \text{ A}$, motor armature current $I_{a1} = 240 \text{ A}$, generator armature current $I_{a2} = 200 \text{ A}$, armature resistance of each machine $R_a = 0.05 \; \Omega$. Calculate the **efficiency of both machines**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify current relationship:**

$$
I_{a1} = I + I_{a2} = 40 + 200 = 240 \text{ A} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Constant loss per machine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{(250)(40) - (240)^2(0.05) - (200)^2(0.05)}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{10000 - 2880 - 2000}{2} = \dfrac{5120}{2} = 2560 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Motor efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{motor}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - W_c}{V \cdot I_{a1}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{60000 - 2880 - 2560}{60000} = \dfrac{54560}{60000} = 0.9093
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{motor}} = 90.93\%} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 4 — Generator efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{gen}} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a2}}{V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a + W_c} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{50000}{50000 + 2000 + 2560} = \dfrac{50000}{54560} = 0.9164
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{gen}} = 91.64\%} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
P_{\text{out,motor}} = 54560 \text{ W} = P_{\text{in,gen}} = 54560 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the **expressions for efficiency of the motor and the generator** in Hopkinson's test, starting from the power balance equation. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Both machines are identical with the same armature resistance $R_a$ and same constant loss $W_c$ at the operating voltage and speed.
2. Brush contact drop is negligible.
3. Field currents are small compared to armature currents and are accounted for separately or included in the supply current.

**Power balance:** The supply provides only the losses:

$$
V \cdot I = I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + 2W_c \quad \cdots (1) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Constant loss per machine:**

$$
W_c = \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2}}{2} \quad \cdots (2) \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Motor analysis:** Input to motor = $V \cdot I_{a1}$. Losses in motor = $I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} + W_c$.

$$
\eta_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} - W_c}{V \cdot I_{a1}} \quad \cdots (3)
$$

Simplifying: $\eta_{\text{motor}} = 1 - \dfrac{I_{a1}R_{a1}}{V} - \dfrac{W_c}{V I_{a1}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$

**Generator analysis:** Output of generator = $V \cdot I_{a2}$. Losses in generator = $I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c$.

$$
\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I_{a2}}{V \cdot I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + W_c} \quad \cdots (4)
$$

Simplifying: $\eta_{\text{gen}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{I_{a2}R_{a2}}{V} + \dfrac{W_c}{V I_{a2}}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$

---

**(b)** In a Hopkinson's test on two identical DC machines, the data obtained is: $V = 220 \text{ V}$, supply current $I = 30 \text{ A}$, motor armature current $I_{a1} = 180 \text{ A}$, generator armature current $I_{a2} = 150 \text{ A}$, armature resistance of each machine $R_a = 0.06 \; \Omega$. Calculate the **efficiency of each machine** and verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Current check:**

$$
I + I_{a2} = 30 + 150 = 180 = I_{a1} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Constant loss $W_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c &= \dfrac{(220)(30) - (180)^2(0.06) - (150)^2(0.06)}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{6600 - 1944 - 1350}{2} = \dfrac{3306}{2} = 1653 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Motor efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{motor}} &= \dfrac{(220)(180) - (180)^2(0.06) - 1653}{(220)(180)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{39600 - 1944 - 1653}{39600} = \dfrac{36003}{39600} = 0.9092
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{motor}} = 90.92\%} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 4 — Generator efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{gen}} &= \dfrac{(220)(150)}{(220)(150) + (150)^2(0.06) + 1653} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{33000}{33000 + 1350 + 1653} = \dfrac{33000}{36003} = 0.9167
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{gen}} = 91.67\%} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
P_{\text{out,motor}} = 36003 \text{ W}; \quad P_{\text{in,gen}} = 36003 \text{ W} \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing armature currents:** Many students write $I_{a1} = I - I_{a2}$, which is **WRONG**. The correct relation is $I_{a1} = I + I_{a2}$. Reason: motor draws current from BOTH the supply AND the generator armature.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $W_c$:** A frequent slip is writing $W_c = V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a$ instead of dividing by 2. This gives the **combined** constant loss, not per-machine loss.
>
> 3. **Confusing motor and generator formulas:** Motor output is on the numerator (electrical → mechanical), while generator output is also electrical — but its denominator contains the input (mechanical). Mixing these up costs **3–4 marks** instantly.
>
> 4. **Not verifying shaft power equality:** Examiners love the verification step $P_{\text{out,motor}} = P_{\text{in,gen}}$. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Including field current in armature current:** Unless specifically given, treat the field currents as separate and the armature currents as already excluding them.

---

## 5.4 Advantages & Limitations (Rapid Recall)

**Advantages:**

- Tests machines at **full load without an actual load** — saves huge energy.
- Permits **heat-run test** for temperature-rise verification.
- Power consumed from mains = **total losses only** — economically efficient.
- Allows **efficiency determination at various loads** by varying field excitations.

**Limitations:**

- Requires **two identical machines** — availability constraint.
- Difficult to get **exactly identical** magnetization characteristics.
- Both machines must be **operated at same rated voltage and speed**.
- Not suitable for **series machines** (only shunt and compound).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Quick Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Hopkinson's test = **Back-to-back / Regenerative test** on two identical DC machines. *(✓)*
> - Both armatures and fields in **parallel across supply**, shafts **mechanically coupled**. *(✓)*
> - **Current relation:** $I_{a1} = I + I_{a2}$ (NOT $I - I_{a2}$). *(✓)*
> - **Master equation:** $V \cdot I = I_{a1}^{\,2}R_{a1} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_{a2} + 2W_c$. *(✓)*
> - **Constant loss per machine:** $W_c = \dfrac{V \cdot I - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a}{2}$. *(✓)*
> - **Motor efficiency:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{V I_{a1} - I_{a1}^{\,2}R_a - W_c}{V I_{a1}}$. *(✓)*
> - **Generator efficiency:** $\eta_g = \dfrac{V I_{a2}}{V I_{a2} + I_{a2}^{\,2}R_a + W_c}$. *(✓)*
