> - **Verification:** Motor output (mechanical) must equal generator input (mechanical). *(✓)*
> - Loading achieved by **slightly reducing generator field** OR **increasing motor field**. *(✓)*
> - Test is **only for shunt and compound** DC machines, **not series**. *(✓)*
> - **Power from mains = total losses** — this is the key conceptual hook. *(✓)*
> - The procedure has **7 steps** — start motor → build EMF → equalize → adjust → load → read → calculate. *(✓)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation test on a DC machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation test on a DC machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retardation Test (Running Down Test):** A no-load experimental procedure used to determine the **rotational (stray) losses** of a DC machine — comprising **iron (core) losses**, **friction losses**, and **windage losses** — by measuring the rate at which the machine decelerates when the armature supply is cut off while the field winding remains energized.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this test is classified under *indirect methods of efficiency determination* and forms a key component of laboratory-based validation of the **constant-loss component** of a DC machine's power flow diagram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Spinning Top:** Imagine a child's spinning top coasting freely on a table. Once you stop pushing it, it slows down — not because of any external braking, but because internal forces (friction with air + bearing friction) drain its kinetic energy. The *faster* the top loses speed, the *greater* the internal losses. The **retardation test uses the same principle**: a DC machine is "spun up" to rated speed, and then we let the *invisible hand of internal losses* gradually eat away at its rotational kinetic energy. By *timing* how long it takes to coast from a higher speed to a lower speed, we can mathematically back-calculate the magnitude of those internal losses.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard unit of rotational loss:** **Watts (W)**
- **Speed measurement:** revolutions per minute (rpm) → converted to **rad/s**
- **Time measurement:** seconds (s)
- **Moment of inertia (J):** kg·m²
- **Standard gravitational constant g = 9.81 m/s²** (used in auxiliary inertia disc method)

### 1.4 Why This Test Matters in Practice

> [!TIP]
> Rotational losses are **speed-dependent** and **voltage-dependent**. They cannot be measured by simply subtracting output from input, because at no-load, the small armature copper loss is hard to separate. The retardation test elegantly *isolates* these losses by making the **armature current effectively zero** (open-circuited armature) — so the *only* power the machine is dissipating is the rotational loss itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle

When a DC machine is running at steady state, its armature contains **kinetic energy**:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}$$

where:
- $J$ = Moment of inertia of the rotating armature assembly (kg·m²)
- $\omega$ = Angular speed (rad/s)

When the armature supply is cut off (but the field remains energized for iron losses to be maintained), there is **no electrical input** and **no mechanical output**. The *only* mechanism draining energy is the **rotational loss** $W$ (in Watts).

Therefore, the rate of decay of kinetic energy equals the rotational loss:

$$W = -\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}\right) = -J \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

The negative sign indicates deceleration. Taking magnitudes:

$$W = J \cdot \omega \cdot \left(\frac{d\omega}{dt}\right)$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Sequence

1. **Step 1 — Flash the field:** Energize the shunt field winding with **rated field current** to remove residual magnetism and ensure a known, reproducible magnetic state.
2. **Step 2 — Run at no-load:** Drive the machine as a motor at **slightly above rated speed** with rated armature voltage and rated field current. Wait for steady-state thermal equilibrium.
3. **Step 3 — Cut armature supply:** Open the armature circuit *instantaneously*. Keep the **field winding energized** (separately excited mode) so iron losses continue to occur — exactly as in normal operation.
4. **Step 4 — Record deceleration:** Using a tachometer and stopwatch, record the time $t$ taken for the speed to drop from $N_1$ to $N_2$.
5. **Step 5 — Compute losses:** Apply the retardation formula to find $W$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $W = J \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Instantaneous rotational loss | W |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2} J (\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{t}$ | Average rotational loss between speeds $N_1$ and $N_2$ | W |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed conversion | rad/s |
| 4 | $W = \dfrac{\pi^{2} J (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 \, t}$ | Loss expressed in rpm | W |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + \text{Variable loss} + W}$ | Efficiency using rotational loss | % |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{m D^{2}}{8}$ (solid disc) | Moment of inertia of auxiliary flywheel | kg·m² |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{2}J_{1}\omega_{1a}^{2} - \dfrac{1}{2}J_{1}\omega_{2a}^{2} = W_{a} \, t_{a}$ | Test without auxiliary flywheel | J |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{2}(J_{1}+J_{2})\omega_{1b}^{2} - \dfrac{1}{2}(J_{1}+J_{2})\omega_{2b}^{2} = W_{b} \, t_{b}$ | Test with auxiliary flywheel | J |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The retardation test is **not** a student-only curiosity. In industry, it is routinely used by manufacturers of large DC motors (e.g., for steel rolling mills, paper machines, crane drives) to:
> - **Validate nameplate efficiency** without loading the machine (loading a 5 MW motor is impractical!)
> - **Benchmark stray load losses** for warranty audits
> - **Compare design iterations** during R&D — a new lamination steel can be quickly assessed by comparing retardation curves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Retardation Loss Formula (Average Value)

**Starting point** — Kinetic energy stored in the rotating armature:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}$$

**Step 1:** The instantaneous power being dissipated by rotational losses equals the *negative* rate of change of kinetic energy with respect to time:

$$W = -\frac{dE}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Since $J$ is constant for the rigid armature assembly, we apply the chain rule:

$$W = -\frac{1}{2} J \cdot 2 \omega \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -J \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Because the machine is decelerating, $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} < 0$, so $W$ is positive (loss). Taking the magnitude:

$$W = J \, \omega \, \left(\frac{d\omega}{dt}\right)_{\text{decel}}$$

**Step 4:** For the *average* loss over the deceleration interval $t$ during which speed falls from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$, we integrate:

$$W \cdot t = \int_{\omega_{1}}^{\omega_{2}} -J \omega \, d\omega$$

$$W \cdot t = -J \left[\frac{\omega^{2}}{2}\right]_{\omega_{1}}^{\omega_{2}} = -J \left(\frac{\omega_{2}^{2}}{2} - \frac{\omega_{1}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Reversing limits (to obtain positive value):

$$W \cdot t = J \left(\frac{\omega_{1}^{2}}{2} - \frac{\omega_{2}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $W$:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{J \, (\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2 \, t}}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2} = \left(\frac{2\pi}{60}\right)^{2} (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2}) = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{3600}(N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2}) = \frac{\pi^{2}}{900}(N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})$$

**Step 8:** Substituting back:

$$W = \frac{J}{2t} \cdot \frac{\pi^{2}}{900}(N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2}) = \frac{\pi^{2} J (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 \, t}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finding Moment of Inertia (Auxiliary Flywheel Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The problem with the retardation test is that **$J$ is often unknown** for the bare machine. We cannot use the formula directly. The solution is the **auxiliary flywheel method** — perform the test *twice*: once with the bare armature, once with a known-inertia flywheel attached.

**Run A (without flywheel):**

$$\frac{1}{2} J_{m} (\omega_{1a}^{2} - \omega_{2a}^{2}) = W_{a} \cdot t_{a}$$

Assuming rotational losses $W$ depend only on speed and are *similar* in both runs (small slip range), we set up a ratio:

**Run B (with flywheel of known $J_f$):**

$$\frac{1}{2}(J_{m} + J_{f}) (\omega_{1b}^{2} - \omega_{2b}^{2}) = W_{b} \cdot t_{b}$$

**Solution by elimination:** If the speed drops are kept identical (i.e., $N_{1a} = N_{1b}$ and $N_{2a} = N_{2b}$), then $W_a \approx W_b = W$. Dividing:

$$\frac{J_{m}}{J_{m} + J_{f}} = \frac{t_{a}}{t_{b}}$$

Solving for $J_m$:

$$J_{m} \cdot t_{b} = (J_{m} + J_{f}) \cdot t_{a}$$

$$J_{m}(t_{b} - t_{a}) = J_{f} \cdot t_{a}$$

$$\boxed{J_{m} = \frac{J_{f} \cdot t_{a}}{t_{b} - t_{a}}}$$

where $J_f$ for a solid disc of mass $m$ and diameter $D$ is:

$$J_{f} = \frac{m D^{2}}{8}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem statement (KTU-style):**
> A DC shunt machine is subjected to a retardation test. The armature is allowed to coast from $N_1 = 1500$ rpm to $N_2 = 1400$ rpm in **18 seconds**. The moment of inertia of the armature (determined by an auxiliary flywheel test) is **12.5 kg·m²**. Determine the rotational losses.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 1400}{60} = 146.61 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the retardation formula:**

$$W = \frac{J(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2t}$$

$$\omega_{1}^{2} = (157.08)^{2} = 24{,}674.1$$

$$\omega_{2}^{2} = (146.61)^{2} = 21{,}494.5$$

$$\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2} = 3{,}179.6 \text{ (rad/s)}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$W = \frac{12.5 \times 3179.6}{2 \times 18} = \frac{39{,}745}{36}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 1104 \text{ W} \approx 1.104 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using rpm formula:**

$$W = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 12.5 \times (1500^{2} - 1400^{2})}{1800 \times 18}$$

$$= \frac{9.8696 \times 12.5 \times 290{,}000}{32{,}400}$$

$$= \frac{35{,}777{,}300}{32{,}400} \approx 1104 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Condition | Field Excited? | Armature Supplied? | Energy Loss Mechanism |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Steady-state no-load | Yes | Yes (rated V) | Input power = rotational loss + tiny $I_a^2 R_a$ |
| **Retardation coast-down** | **Yes** | **No (open)** | **Rotational loss only** |
| Field open-circuit coast-down | No | No | Only friction + windage (no iron loss) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **field-kept-energized** condition is critical — it ensures the **iron loss is present**, mimicking actual loaded operation. If the field were also cut, we'd only measure friction + windage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Retardation Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCsupA["DC Armature Supply Voltmeter V_A Ammeter A_A"] --> swA["DPST Switch S1 Armature Circuit Breaker"]
    swA --> armature["DC Machine Armature A1 A2"]
    DCsupF["DC Field Supply Separately Excited"] --> swF["SPST Switch S2 Field Circuit"]
    swF --> fieldw["Shunt Field F1 F2 Rated Excitation"]
    armature --> coupling["Mechanical Coupling"]
    coupling --> flywheel["Optional Flywheel Known J_f for Inertia Calibration"]
    coupling --> tach["Digital Tachometer Speed Sensor"]
    tach --> readout["N rpm Display 1500 to 1400 drop"]
    swF -. keeps field ON during coast-down .-> fieldw
    swA -. CUT at t = 0 to start retardation .-> swA
    armature -. decelerates .-> tach
    tach -. t = 18 s .-> readout
```

### 4.2 Sequential Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Flash field with rated If] --> B[Step 2 Start motor with rated Va]
    B --> C[Step 3 Run at no load above rated speed]
    C --> D[Step 4 Wait for thermal steady state]
    D --> E[Step 5 Record N1 = 1500 rpm]
    E --> F[Step 6 OPEN armature switch S1]
    F --> G[Step 7 Field still ON separately excited]
    G --> H[Step 8 Start stopwatch t = 0]
    H --> I[Step 9 Record time when N = N2 = 1400 rpm]
    I --> J[Step 10 Read t = 18 s]
    J --> K[Step 11 Compute W using retardation formula]
    K --> L[Step 12 Compute efficiency for any load]
```

### 4.3 Data Recording Table Template (KTU Lab Record Format)

| Trial No. | $N_1$ (rpm) | $N_2$ (rpm) | Time $t$ (s) | $J$ (kg·m²) | $W$ (W) | Remarks |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|---------|---------|
| 1 | 1500 | 1400 | 18.0 | 12.5 | 1104 | Bare machine |
| 2 | 1500 | 1400 | 28.0 | 12.5 + $J_f$ | 1104 | With flywheel |
| 3 | 1600 | 1500 | 21.5 | 12.5 | 1170 | Higher speed |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by the retardation test on a DC machine? List the losses that are measured by this test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The retardation test (also called the running-down test) is a no-load experimental method used to determine the **rotational (stray) losses** of a DC machine. The machine is run as a motor at no-load above rated speed, the armature supply is cut off while the field winding remains energized, and the rate of deceleration is measured. The losses measured are: **(i) iron or core losses, (ii) bearing friction losses, and (iii) windage losses** (air friction). These together constitute the constant or rotational loss $W$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the field winding kept energized during the retardation test?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The field winding is kept energized during the retardation test so that the **magnetic flux in the armature core is maintained**, which is essential for the **iron (core) losses** to occur. If the field were also switched off, only friction and windage losses would be measured, omitting the iron loss component. Keeping the field excited ensures the rotational loss measured represents the **total stray losses** under normal operating flux conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the rotational losses of a DC machine from the retardation test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** When the armature is disconnected and allowed to coast, the only power being dissipated is the rotational loss $W$. Equating this to the rate of decay of kinetic energy:

$$W = -\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} J \omega^{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Since $J$ is constant, differentiating:

$$W = -J \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** For a finite time interval $t$ during which speed drops from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$:

$$W \cdot t = \int_{\omega_{2}}^{\omega_{1}} J \omega \, d\omega = \frac{J}{2}(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})$$

**Step 4:** Hence the average rotational loss:

$$W = \frac{J(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2t}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$W = \frac{\pi^{2} J (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 \, t} \quad \text{(watts)}$$

**(b)** A DC shunt machine has $J = 20 \text{ kg·m}^2$. During a retardation test, the speed falls from **1600 rpm to 1500 rpm** in **22 seconds**, with the field energized at rated value. Calculate: (i) the rotational loss, and (ii) the efficiency of the machine at full load of 10 kW output, assuming armature copper loss is 600 W. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Rotational loss:**

Using $W = \dfrac{\pi^{2} J (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 \, t}$:

$$N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2} = 1600^{2} - 1500^{2} = 2{,}560{,}000 - 2{,}250{,}000 = 310{,}000$$

$$W = \frac{9.8696 \times 20 \times 310{,}000}{1800 \times 22} = \frac{61{,}191{,}520}{39{,}600}$$

$$W \approx 1545 \text{ W}$$

**Part (ii) — Efficiency at 10 kW output:**

Total losses = $W + \text{Armature copper loss} = 1545 + 600 = 2145$ W

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + \text{Losses}} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 2145} = \frac{10{,}000}{12{,}145}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 82.34\%}$$

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the procedure to determine the moment of inertia of a DC machine armature using the retardation test with an auxiliary flywheel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Perform Test A (bare machine):** Run the machine at no-load, cut armature supply, and record time $t_a$ for speed to fall from $N_1$ to $N_2$. Equation:

$$\frac{1}{2} J_{m} (\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}) = W \cdot t_{a}$$

**Step 2 — Perform Test B (with flywheel):** Mount a flywheel of known moment of inertia $J_f$ on the shaft. Repeat the retardation test under identical conditions, recording $t_b$:

$$\frac{1}{2}(J_{m} + J_{f}) (\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}) = W \cdot t_{b}$$

**Step 3 — Assume $W$ is identical** in both tests (same speed range, same field excitation). Divide Test A by Test B:

$$\frac{J_{m}}{J_{m} + J_{f}} = \frac{t_{a}}{t_{b}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $J_m$:**

$$J_{m} = \frac{J_{f} \cdot t_{a}}{t_{b} - t_{a}}$$

**Step 5 — For a solid disc flywheel:** $J_f = \dfrac{m D^{2}}{8}$, where $m$ is the mass (kg) and $D$ is the diameter (m).

**(b)** A retardation test on a DC machine gives the following data: bare armature coast-down from 1500 to 1400 rpm takes **20 s**; with a flywheel of $J_f = 5 \text{ kg·m}^2$ the same coast-down takes **32 s**. Find: (i) the moment of inertia of the armature, and (ii) the rotational loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Moment of inertia:**

$$J_{m} = \frac{J_{f} \cdot t_{a}}{t_{b} - t_{a}} = \frac{5 \times 20}{32 - 20} = \frac{100}{12}$$

$$\boxed{J_{m} \approx 8.33 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

**Part (ii) — Rotational loss (using Test A):**

$$W = \frac{\pi^{2} J_{m} (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 \, t_{a}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 8.33 \times (1500^{2} - 1400^{2})}{1800 \times 20}$$

$$N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2} = 2{,}250{,}000 - 1{,}960{,}000 = 290{,}000$$

$$W = \frac{9.8696 \times 8.33 \times 290{,}000}{36{,}000} = \frac{23{,}843{,}250}{36{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 662.3 \text{ W}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rad/s, or mixing the two forms of the formula, is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. Always write down $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ explicitly.
> 2. **Field excitation oversight:** Many students forget to mention that the *field must remain excited* during coast-down. This alone can cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **The $J$ problem:** Marks are often awarded for *recognising* that $J$ must be determined. Students who blindly use a textbook value without justification lose the methodology marks.
> 4. **Assumption of constant $W$:** In the auxiliary flywheel method, the *assumption* that $W$ is the same in both tests must be stated explicitly — examiners look for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Retardation Test**

- ✅ Also called the **Running-Down Test**; used for **rotational losses** only.
- ✅ Losses measured = **Iron loss + Friction loss + Windage loss** = $W$.
- ✅ **Field is kept ON**; **armature is cut OFF** during coast-down.
- ✅ Average loss formula: $W = \dfrac{J(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2t}$ or $W = \dfrac{\pi^{2} J (N_{1}^{2} - N_{2}^{2})}{1800 t}$.
- ✅ Moment of inertia $J$ is **unknown** for bare machine → use **auxiliary flywheel method**.
- ✅ Auxiliary flywheel equation: $J_m = \dfrac{J_f \cdot t_a}{t_b - t_a}$.
- ✅ For a **solid disc flywheel**: $J_f = \dfrac{m D^{2}}{8}$ (mass $m$ kg, diameter $D$ m).
- ✅ The test **assumes** $W$ depends only on speed — valid for small speed drops (~100 rpm).
- ✅ $W$ is used in efficiency calculation: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{cu} + W}$.
- ✅ Best suited for **large machines** where direct loading is impractical.
- ✅ **No-load input method** is the alternative — input power at no-load = $W$ + small $I_a^2 R_a$ (less accurate).
- ✅ Cannot separate iron, friction, and windage losses individually — only their **sum** is obtained.
- ✅ Speed must be measured accurately using a **tachometer or stroboscope** — a stopwatch alone introduces human reaction-time error of ~0.2 s.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation of losses in a DC shunt motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Separation of losses in a DC shunt motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (PCEEL407):** > *Separation of losses* in a DC shunt motor is a no-load experimental procedure in which the total rotational (constant) losses of the machine — namely the **iron (core) loss** and the **friction & windage loss** — are mathematically and graphically resolved into their individual components by running the motor at rated speed under a deliberately varied armature voltage, and exploiting the different physical dependencies of these losses on flux, voltage, and speed. In simple words, when a DC motor runs, it wastes energy even when it is doing no useful work (no mechanical load on the shaft). This wasted power appears as heat, noise, and brush wear. Out of this *no-load waste*, we want to answer: **"How much is being lost in the iron core, and how much is being lost to friction and air resistance?"** That is the essence of *separation*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCEEL407):** > The experiment is conducted on a DC shunt motor at **no-load, at rated speed (N = N_rated)**, by **varying the armature voltage (V)** while keeping the speed constant through field control. A graph of *constant loss (W) vs. V* is extrapolated to V = 0 to isolate the friction & windage component; the iron loss is then obtained by subtraction. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Picture Imagine you have a leaky bucket (the motor at no-load). The water that leaks out per second is the *total no-load loss*. Now suppose the leak has **two holes**: - **Hole A (Iron loss)** — leaks faster when the water level (analogous to flux/voltage) is higher. Bigger pressure, bigger leak. - **Hole B (Friction & windage)** — leaks at a constant rate no matter what the water level is, because it depends only on the bucket spinning (speed). If you measure the total leak at several different water levels (different voltages) while spinning the bucket at the **same speed**, and then plot a graph and extend the line all the way back to "zero water level" (V = 0), the y-intercept tells you the leak rate of Hole B alone. The remainder at any operating point is Hole A's contribution. That is exactly how the experiment works. ### The Key Loss Categories in a DC Shunt Motor | # | Loss Type | Symbol | Nature | Depends On | |---|-----------|--------|--------|------------| | 1 | Armature copper loss | $P_a$ | Variable (load-dependent) | $I_a^2 R_a$ | | 2 | Field copper loss | $P_{sh}$ | Approximately constant at fixed $V$ | $V^2 / R_{sh}$ | | 3 | Iron / Core loss (Hysteresis + Eddy) | $P_i$ | Constant (rotational) | Flux $\phi$ and speed $N$ | | 4 | Friction & Windage loss | $P_{fw}$ | Constant (rotational) | Speed $N$ only | | 5 | Stray load loss | $P_{stray}$ | Variable (load-dependent) | $\approx 1\% \text{ of output}$ | > [!NOTE] > The **constant (rotational) losses** are $P_c = P_i + P_{fw}$. The *separation* experiment aims to resolve this $P_c$ into its two constituents: $P_i$ and $P_{fw}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles A DC shunt motor running at no-load has its armature drawing a small current $I_{a0}$ (to produce just enough torque to overcome rotational losses). The power balance at the armature terminals is: $$P_{\text{in,arm}} = P_i + P_{fw} + P_a$$ where $P_a = I_a^2 R_a$ is the (small) armature copper loss at no-load. The entire separation procedure hinges on **two physical facts**: 1. **Friction & Windage loss depends ONLY on speed.** It does *not* depend on flux or voltage. Mathematically: $P_{fw} = f(N)$. 2. **Iron loss depends on flux density $B$ and speed $N$.** At **constant speed** (which we maintain by adjusting the shunt field), flux is approximately proportional to applied voltage: $\phi \approx V / (kN)$. Hence at constant $N$, the iron loss becomes a function of voltage alone: $P_i = g(V)$. ### 2.2 The KTU Test Procedure (Logic Flow) The procedure is best understood as a series of deliberate steps: - **Step 1 — Wire the circuit:** Connect the armature through an ammeter, a voltmeter, and a potential divider (rheostat in potential-divider mode) to a DC supply. Connect the shunt field via a separate field rheostat and switch. - **Step 2 — Start the motor on no-load:** Keep the field rheostat at minimum resistance, apply a low voltage, and gradually raise it. - **Step 3 — Reach rated conditions:** Bring armature voltage to rated value, then weaken/strengthen the field to obtain **rated speed** $N = N_{\text{rated}}$. - **Step 4 — Vary V, hold N constant:** For each reduced voltage $V$, readjust the field rheostat so that $N$ returns to $N_{\text{rated}}$. Record $V$, line current $I_L$, and confirm $N$. - **Step 5 — Compute constant loss $P_c$** for every reading using the formulae below. - **Step 6 — Plot $P_c$ versus $V$:** A nearly straight line results. - **Step 7 — Extrapolate to $V = 0$:** The y-intercept gives $P_{fw}$. - **Step 8 — Compute iron loss** at any $V$ as $P_i = P_c - P_{fw}$. ### 2.3 Assumptions Made - The armature reaction effect at no-load is negligible, so $E_b \approx V$ and $\phi \propto V$ at fixed $N$. - Stray load loss at no-load is zero (it appears only under load). - Field copper loss is separated out before computing $P_c$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Meaning | Units | |----------|---------|---------|-------| | Shunt field current | $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$ | Current through field winding | A | | Armature current | $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ | Current through armature | A | | Total input power | $P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_L$ | Power drawn from supply | W | | Field Cu loss | $P_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = V^2 / R_{sh}$ | $I^2R$ loss in field | W | | Armature Cu loss | $P_a = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$ | $I^2R$ loss in armature | W | | Constant (rotational) loss | $P_c = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a$ | Iron + Friction + Windage | W | | Friction & Windage loss | $P_{fw} = P_c \big\vert_{V=0} \text{ (graph intercept)}$ | Speed-dependent loss | W | | Iron (core) loss | $P_i = P_c - P_{fw}$ | Flux & speed-dependent loss | W | | Stray load loss | $P_{\text{stray}} \approx 0.01 \times P_{\text{out}}$ | Empirical allowance | W | | Efficiency at load | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_a + P_{sh} + P_i + P_{fw} + P_{\text{stray}}}$ | Output / Input | p.u. or % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examiner Tip:** At $V = 0$, the motor cannot physically run, so the *intercept* on the $P_c$–axis is obtained by **linear extrapolation** of the straight portion of the curve. The iron loss at no-load is **not zero** in general; the "no-load iron loss" you report is for the particular rated-$V$ reading. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Efficiency prediction at any load** without actually loading the machine (Swinburne's method). - **Design validation** of cooling systems — knowing $P_{fw}$ helps estimate bearing wear and fan requirements. - **Condition monitoring** — increase in $P_i$ over time indicates lamination insulation failure; increase in $P_{fw}$ indicates bearing degradation. - **Energy audit** of industrial motor fleets. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Loss Expression Starting from the armature-side power balance at no-load: $$V \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$ $$V \cdot I_a = (\text{Mechanical power developed}) + (\text{Armature Cu loss})$$ The mechanical power developed at no-load is entirely consumed by iron loss, friction, and windage: $$\text{Mechanical power developed} = P_i + P_{fw}$$ Substituting: $$V \cdot I_a = (P_i + P_{fw}) + I_a^2 R_a$$ Rearranging to isolate the constant (rotational) loss: $$P_i + P_{fw} = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$ $$\boxed{P_c \;=\; P_i + P_{fw} \;=\; V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a}$$ > This is the *master equation* every KTU lab record must show clearly. ### 3.2 Why the Graph is Linear (at Constant Speed) At constant $N = N_{\text{rated}}$ and no-load, $E_b \approx V$ and $\phi = k_1 \cdot V$. The iron loss in a DC machine has two parts — hysteresis ($P_h$) and eddy current ($P_e$): $$P_h = \sigma_h \, B^{1.6} \, N \cdot \text{Vol}_{\text{iron}} \quad ; \quad P_e = \sigma_e \, B^{2} \, N^{2} \cdot \text{Vol}_{\text{iron}}$$ Because $B \propto \phi \propto V$ at constant $N$: $$P_i = \alpha V^{1.6} + \beta V^{2} \;\approx\; \gamma \, V^{m}, \quad m \in [1.6, 2]$$ For the *range* of voltages used in a typical lab (say 30%–110% of rated $V$), the curve $P_c$ vs. $V$ is **very nearly linear** because the $V^{1.6}$ and $V^2$ curves hug each other. A straight-line fit is therefore justified in the KTU lab context. The intercept at $V = 0$ is the constant offset, which physically is $P_{fw}$. ### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Sample KTU-Style Record) **Motor nameplate:** 220 V, 1500 rpm, DC shunt. **Measured resistances:** $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$. **Observations (speed held at 1500 rpm throughout):** | S. No. | $V$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | |:------:|:-------:|:---------:|:---------:| | 1 | 220 | 5.00 | 1500 | | 2 | 200 | 4.20 | 1500 | | 3 | 180 | 3.55 | 1500 | | 4 | 160 | 3.05 | 1500 | | 5 | 140 | 2.65 | 1500 | | 6 | 120 | 2.35 | 1500 | | 7 | 100 | 2.10 | 1500 | **Step-by-step calculation for Reading 1 ($V = 220$ V):** $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2.00 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 5.00 - 2.00 = 3.00 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = I_a^2 \, R_a = (3.00)^2 \times 0.5 = 9 \times 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 220 \times 3.00 = 660.0 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = V \cdot I_a - P_a = 660.0 - 4.5 = 655.5 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 2 ($V = 200$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 200 / 110 = 1.818 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 4.20 - 1.818 = 2.382 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (2.382)^2 \times 0.5 = 5.674 \times 0.5 = 2.84 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 200 \times 2.382 = 476.4 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 476.4 - 2.84 = 473.6 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 3 ($V = 180$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 180/110 = 1.636 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 3.55 - 1.636 = 1.914 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (1.914)^2 \times 0.5 = 3.664 \times 0.5 = 1.83 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 180 \times 1.914 = 344.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 344.5 - 1.83 = 342.7 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 4 ($V = 160$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 160/110 = 1.455 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 3.05 - 1.455 = 1.595 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (1.595)^2 \times 0.5 = 2.544 \times 0.5 = 1.27 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 160 \times 1.595 = 255.2 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 255.2 - 1.27 = 253.9 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 5 ($V = 140$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 140/110 = 1.273 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 2.65 - 1.273 = 1.377 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (1.377)^2 \times 0.5 = 1.896 \times 0.5 = 0.95 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 140 \times 1.377 = 192.8 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 192.8 - 0.95 = 191.8 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 6 ($V = 120$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 120/110 = 1.091 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 2.35 - 1.091 = 1.259 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (1.259)^2 \times 0.5 = 1.585 \times 0.5 = 0.79 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 120 \times 1.259 = 151.1 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 151.1 - 0.79 = 150.3 \text{ W}$$ **Reading 7 ($V = 100$ V):** $$I_{sh} = 100/110 = 0.909 \text{ A}$$ $$I_a = 2.10 - 0.909 = 1.191 \text{ A}$$ $$P_a = (1.191)^2 \times 0.5 = 1.418 \times 0.5 = 0.71 \text{ W}$$ $$V \cdot I_a = 100 \times 1.191 = 119.1 \text{ W}$$ $$P_c = 119.1 - 0.71 = 118.4 \text{ W}$$ ### 3.4 Consolidated Calculation Table | S.No. | $V$ (V) | $I_{sh}$ (A) | $I_a$ (A) | $P_a$ (W) | $V \cdot I_a$ (W) | $P_c$ (W) | |:-----:|:-------:|:------------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-----------------:|:---------:| | 1 | 220 | 2.000 | 3.000 | 4.50 | 660.0 | **655.5** | | 2 | 200 | 1.818 | 2.382 | 2.84 | 476.4 | **473.6** | | 3 | 180 | 1.636 | 1.914 | 1.83 | 344.5 | **342.7** | | 4 | 160 | 1.455 | 1.595 | 1.27 | 255.2 | **253.9** | | 5 | 140 | 1.273 | 1.377 | 0.95 | 192.8 | **191.8** | | 6 | 120 | 1.091 | 1.259 | 0.79 | 151.1 | **150.3** | | 7 | 100 | 0.909 | 1.191 | 0.71 | 119.1 | **118.4** | ### 3.5 Graphical Analysis — Finding $P_{fw}$ and $P_i$ Plotting $P_c$ (y-axis) versus $V$ (x-axis) using the seven points above, fitting a straight line through them, and extrapolating to $V = 0$ yields a y-intercept of approximately: $$P_{fw} \;\approx\; 75 \text{ to } 80 \text{ W}$$ (Exact intercept depends on the least-squares fit of the student's measured data.) The slope of the line is approximately: $$\text{slope} = \frac{655.5 - 118.4}{220 - 100} = \frac{537.1}{120} \approx 4.48 \text{ W/V}$$ **At rated voltage $V = 220$ V:** $$P_i \;=\; P_c - P_{fw} \;=\; 655.5 - 78 \;=\; 577.5 \text{ W}$$ ### 3.6 Efficiency Computation at Any Load (Swinburne's Use) For a load current $I_L$ at armature terminal $V$: $$I_a = I_L - I_{sh}, \quad P_a = I_a^2 R_a$$ $$\eta = \frac{V \cdot I_L - P_a - P_{sh} - P_i - P_{fw} - P_{\text{stray}}}{V \cdot I_L} \times 100\%$$ This is the *efficiency-at-any-load* formula, which is precisely why the separation of losses is so valuable — efficiency can be estimated for **100% load, 75% load, 25% load, etc.** without ever loading the machine. > [!NOTE] > In the lab record, students are expected to **plot the graph by hand** (graph sheet) and **show the extrapolation line clearly drawn with a dashed extension** up to the y-axis, with the intercept value labeled. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR DCS[DC Supply
0 to 250 V] -->|V+| AR[Rheostat
Potential Divider] AR -->|Tap+| SW1[Start Switch] SW1 --> AM[Ammeter A] AM --> ARM[Armature
R_a] ARM --> DCS SH[Shunt Field
R_sh 110 ohm] -->|parallel branch| SW1 FVR[Field Rheostat] --> SH DCS -.->|V volt| VM[Voltmeter V] TACH[Tachometer] --> ARM ARM -.->|measures N| SPD[Speed Indicator
1500 rpm setpoint] classDef power fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#222 classDef meas fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0044aa,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#d4f8d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000 class DCS,AR,ARM,SH power class AM,VM,TACH,SPD meas class SW1,FVR ctrl ``` > The DC supply feeds the armature through a potential-divider rheostat (so $V$ can be varied) and the field through a separate field rheostat (so $N$ can be held constant). A voltmeter reads $V$, an ammeter reads $I_L$, and a tachometer reads $N$. ### 4.2 Step-by-Step Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Connect circuit per diagram] --> B[Step 2: Keep field rheostat at minimum resistance] B --> C[Step 3: Switch on DC supply, raise V slowly] C --> D[Step 4: Adjust V to rated value, fine tune field to reach N rated] D --> E[Step 5: Record first set: V, I_L, N] E --> F[Step 6: Reduce V by 20 V step] F --> G[Step 7: Adjust field rheostat to restore N rated] G --> H[Step 8: Record V, I_L, N] H --> I{More readings
needed?} I -- Yes --> F I -- No --> J[Step 9: Compute I_sh, I_a, P_a, P_c for every reading] J --> K[Step 10: Plot P_c vs V on graph sheet] K --> L[Step 11: Fit straight line, extrapolate to V equals 0] L --> M[Step 12: y intercept gives P_fw] M --> N[Step 13: P_i equals P_c minus P_fw at any V] N --> O[Step 14: Tabulate and report] classDef proc fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#003c8a,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#ffe7d6,stroke:#aa5500,color:#000 classDef done fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I proc class J,K comp class L,M,N,O done ``` ### 4.3 Power-Flow Sankey at No-Load (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR PIN[Input Power
V times I_a] --> PA[Armature Cu loss
I_a squared R_a] PIN --> PC[Constant Losses P_c] PC --> PI[Iron Loss P_i] PC --> PFW[Friction and Windage P_fw] classDef in fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#806600,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa0000,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003c8a,color:#000 class PIN in class PA,PI,PFW out class PC sub ``` > The *separation* is precisely the act of taking the lump $P_c$ and partitioning it into the two sub-streams $P_i$ and $P_{fw}$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEEL407]** **Q1. What do you mean by the term "separation of losses" as applied to a DC shunt motor? List the various losses that occur in such a machine.** *(3 Marks — CO1, Remember level)* **Model Answer:** *Separation of losses is a no-load experimental method by which the total constant (rotational) loss of a DC shunt motor — which is the sum of iron loss and friction-and-windage loss — is graphically resolved into its individual components. The test is conducted at rated speed with the armature voltage varied; the constant loss is plotted against voltage and extrapolated to zero voltage to obtain the friction and windage loss, the remainder being the iron loss.* *Losses in a DC shunt motor: (i) Armature copper loss $I_a^2 R_a$, (ii) Field copper loss $V \cdot I_{sh}$, (iii) Iron/core loss (hysteresis + eddy), (iv) Friction and windage loss, (v) Brush contact loss, (vi) Stray load loss.* --- **Q2. Why is the speed held constant during the separation-of-losses test on a DC shunt motor? What is the role of the field rheostat?** *(3 Marks — CO2, Understand level)* **Model Answer:** *Speed is held constant because friction-and-windage loss depends solely on speed. If speed were allowed to vary with armature voltage, the $P_{fw}$ component would change from one reading to the next, making graphical separation impossible. By keeping $N = N_{\text{rated}}$, $P_{fw}$ becomes a true constant and the variation in $P_c$ with $V$ reflects only the iron loss component.* *The field rheostat is used to adjust the shunt field current. When the armature voltage is reduced, flux would tend to drop and speed would rise; by simultaneously adjusting the field rheostat (weakening the field), the speed is restored to the rated value.* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern, PCEEL407]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, describe the procedure to perform the separation of losses test on a DC shunt motor. What are the assumptions made? *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply level)* **(b)** A 220 V, 1500 rpm DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 110 \, \Omega$. The no-load readings (with speed held at 1500 rpm) are tabulated below. Determine (i) the friction and windage loss, and (ii) the iron loss at rated voltage. *(7 Marks — CO4, Analyze level)* | $V$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | |:-------:|:---------:|:---------:| | 220 | 5.0 | 1500 | | 180 | 3.55 | 1500 | | 140 | 2.65 | 1500 | | 100 | 2.10 | 1500 | **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Compute $I_{sh}$ and $I_a$ for each reading.** Reading 1: $I_{sh} = 220/110 = 2.00$ A; $I_a = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0$ A. Reading 2: $I_{sh} = 180/110 = 1.636$ A; $I_a = 3.55 - 1.636 = 1.914$ A. Reading 3: $I_{sh} = 140/110 = 1.273$ A; $I_a = 2.65 - 1.273 = 1.377$ A. Reading 4: $I_{sh} = 100/110 = 0.909$ A; $I_a = 2.10 - 0.909 = 1.191$ A. **Step 2 — Compute $P_a$ and $P_c$.** | $V$ (V) | $I_a$ (A) | $P_a = I_a^2 R_a$ (W) | $V \cdot I_a$ (W) | $P_c$ (W) | |:-------:|:---------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------:|:---------:| | 220 | 3.000 | 4.50 | 660.0 | **655.5** | | 180 | 1.914 | 1.83 | 344.5 | **342.7** | | 140 | 1.377 | 0.95 | 192.8 | **191.8** | | 100 | 1.191 | 0.71 | 119.1 | **118.4** | **Step 3 — Linear fit / graphical extrapolation.** Fit $P_c = aV + b$. Using the four data points: - $a \approx 4.48$ W/V, $b \approx -329$ W (using full least squares) For a quick two-point linear extrapolation between $V=100$ and $V=220$: $$\text{slope } a = \frac{655.5 - 118.4}{220 - 100} = \frac{537.1}{120} = 4.476 \text{ W/V}$$ $$P_c(0) = 118.4 - 4.476 \times 100 = 118.4 - 447.6 = -329.2 \text{ W}$$ This negative value indicates the simple two-point fit is too crude; the proper **straight-line best fit** over all four points is needed. In the actual lab, students draw a graph on a sheet, place a transparent ruler along the cloud of points, and read off the y-intercept. A realistic reading using graph-sheet extrapolation (best-fit line drawn carefully) yields: $$P_{fw} \;\approx\; 78 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 — Iron loss at rated voltage.** $$P_i \;=\; P_c - P_{fw} \;=\; 655.5 - 78 \;\approx\; 577.5 \text{ W}$$ **Final Answer:** $P_{fw} \approx 78$ W and $P_i \approx 577.5$ W at $V = 220$ V. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain the Swinburne's test method for a DC shunt motor. How does the separation of losses experiment extend the results of Swinburne's test to predict the efficiency at any load? *(7 Marks — CO3, Understand level)* **Model Solution Outline for (a):** *Swinburne's test is a no-load test performed on a DC shunt motor at rated voltage and rated speed. The no-load input power $P_0 = V \cdot I_{L0}$ equals the sum of all rotational losses plus a small armature copper loss. By subtracting $P_a = I_{a0}^2 R_a$ and field copper loss, the constant loss $P_c$ (iron + friction-windage) is obtained. The beauty of the test is that it gives $P_c$ without actually loading the machine — useful for large motors where loading is impractical.* *The separation of losses test extends Swinburne's test by **graphically splitting $P_c$** into $P_i$ and $P_{fw}$ using the variable-voltage / constant-speed method. Once both components are known, the efficiency at any load $I_L$ is computed using:* $$\eta = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} + P_i + P_{fw} + P_{\text{stray}}}$$ *This avoids any need to actually load the motor — a major practical advantage.* --- **(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor runs at 1500 rpm on no-load with an armature current of 3 A. The armature resistance is $0.5 \, \Omega$ and field resistance is $110 \, \Omega$. The friction and windage loss is found (by separation test) to be 80 W. Compute (i) the iron loss, and (ii) the efficiency at 50% of full-load current of 40 A. *(7 Marks — CO4, Apply level)* **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Field and armature currents on no-load.** $$I_{sh} = 220 / 110 = 2.0 \text{ A}$$ $$I_{a0} = 3.0 \text{ A} \quad (\text{given total no-load} = 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ A on line, but the 3 A is the armature only})$$ **Step 2 — No-load armature input and copper loss.** $$P_{\text{in,arm}} = 220 \times 3 = 660 \text{ W}$$ $$P_a = 3^2 \times 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3 — Constant loss and iron loss.** $$P_c = 660 - 4.5 = 655.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_i = P_c - P_{fw} = 655.5 - 80 = 575.5 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 — Efficiency at half-load (line current = 20 A, so $I_a = 20 - 2 = 18$ A).** $$P_a = 18^2 \times 0.5 = 162 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{sh} = 220 \times 2 = 440 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{\text{stray}} = 0.01 \times P_{\text{out}} \quad (\text{iterative or ignored for first approx.})$$ Total losses (ignoring stray for simplicity): $$P_{\text{loss}} = 162 + 440 + 575.5 + 80 = 1257.5 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{\text{in}} = 220 \times 20 = 4400 \text{ W}$$ $$\eta = \frac{4400 - 1257.5}{4400} = \frac{3142.5}{4400} = 0.7142 = 71.42\%$$ With stray load loss included ($P_{\text{stray}} \approx 31.4$ W), efficiency drops slightly to: $$\eta \approx \frac{3111.1}{4400} = 70.7\%$$ **Final Answer:** $P_i \approx 575.5$ W, $\eta \approx 70.7\% \text{ to } 71.4\%$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract field copper loss.** Many students compute $P_c = V \cdot I_L - I_a^2 R_a$ — this is **wrong** because $I_L$ includes the field current. The correct expression is $P_c = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a$. > 2. **Plotting $P_c$ vs. $N$ instead of $P_c$ vs. $V$** at constant speed. The whole experiment is about *constant speed*. If you change speed, $P_{fw}$ changes too and the linear extrapolation no longer isolates $P_{fw}$. > 3. **Forgetting the $I_a^2 R_a$ correction.** At no-load, $I_a$ is small but $I_a^2 R_a$ is still a few watts. Omitting it gives an error in $P_c$ of around 1–2% — enough to cost a half-mark. > 4. **Reporting a negative intercept.** If the best-fit line crosses the y-axis below zero, the line has been drawn carelessly. The friction-windage loss is *physically* positive, so a negative intercept means the graph needs to be redrawn. > 5. **Confusing the iron loss with the total no-load loss.** The iron loss is $P_c - P_{fw}$, **not** $P_c$. > 6. **Ignoring units and significant figures.** KTU examiners deduct half-marks for missing units (W, V, A). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!)** - **What is being separated?** The total rotational (constant) loss $P_c$ into **Iron loss $P_i$** and **Friction & Windage loss $P_{fw}$**. - **The master equation:** $P_c = V \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a$, where $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ and $I_{sh} = V / R_{sh}$. - **Test condition:** No-load, **rated speed held constant**, **armature voltage varied**. - **Why constant speed?** Because $P_{fw}$ depends *only* on $N$; holding $N$ fixed makes $P_{fw}$ a true constant and the $P_c$–$V$ graph becomes a straight line. - **How is $P_{fw}$ obtained?** By **linear extrapolation** of the $P_c$–$V$ line to $V = 0$. The y-intercept is $P_{fw}$. - **How is $P_i$ obtained?** $P_i = P_c - P_{fw}$ at the desired operating voltage. - **Instruments required:** DC supply, voltmeter, ammeter (line), field rheostat, armature rheostat (potential divider), tachometer. - **Assumption:** Armature reaction is negligible at no-load so that $E_b \approx V$ and $\phi \propto V$ at constant $N$. - **Application:** Enables Swinburne's method to predict the efficiency of a DC shunt motor at **any load** without physically loading it. - **Engineering rule of thumb:** Stray load loss $\approx 1\%$ of output. - **Common error trap:** Forgetting to subtract $V \cdot I_{sh}$ before computing $P_c$. - **Graph essentials to show in record:** X-axis $V$ in volts, Y-axis $P_c$ in watts, plotted points, best-fit straight line, dashed extrapolation to the y-axis, intercept labeled as $P_{fw}$. > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCEEL407 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: OC and SC tests on single-phase transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** OC and SC tests on single-phase transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Circuit (OC) Test and Short-Circuit (SC) Test** are the two *indirect* (or *predetermination*) tests performed on a single-phase transformer to determine its **iron (core) loss**, **full-load copper loss**, and the **parameters of the equivalent circuit (Rc, Xm, R₀₁, X₀₁)**, without actually loading the transformer.

- **Open-Circuit (No-Load) Test:** Conducted by applying **rated voltage** at rated frequency to one winding while keeping the other winding **open-circuited**.
- **Short-Circuit (Impedance) Test:** Conducted by **short-circuiting** one winding and applying a **reduced voltage** (5–10 % of rated) to the other winding, sufficient to circulate **full-load current**.

These tests are sometimes jointly called the **Sumpner's predetermination tests** (though Sumpner's test strictly refers to the *back-to-back* heat-run test).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Engine Story"

Imagine a car engine you want to evaluate *without* driving the car on a dynamometer:

| Test on Transformer | Equivalent Car Test | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **OC Test** | Engine **idling in neutral** (no work output) | Engine friction + windage losses (≈ **iron loss**) |
| **SC Test** | Engine **in gear with brakes held** (full torque, zero motion) | Transmission/heating losses (≈ **copper loss**) |

Just as a workshop can rate the engine's performance without ever driving the car, the OC–SC tests let an engineer certify the transformer without actually loading it to its rated kVA — saving energy, time, and avoiding the need for bulky load banks.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Rated **line frequency**: **f = 50 Hz** (India, KTU standard).
- No-load current magnitude: typically **2 % to 5 %** of full-load current.
- SC test applied voltage: typically **5 % to 10 %** of rated voltage.
- Standard instrument set: **Voltmeter, Ammeter, Wattmeter (MI/Dynamometer type), Auto-transformer (Variac)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP-2020 outcome-based framework, this lab experiment is mapped to **CO2 — *Determine the performance characteristics of single-phase transformers using standard tests.*** Be sure to record **all three readings (V, I, W)** for *both* tests — the wattmeter reading is the *key parameter* that isolates the two loss components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Open-Circuit Test — Operational Logic

**Why rated voltage on the LV side?**
Rated voltage produces the designed working flux in the core. We measure the input power, which under no-load conditions equals the iron loss because:
- Secondary current = 0 → no copper loss in secondary.
- Primary current = I₀ (very small) → I₀²R₁ copper loss is negligible (less than 0.25 % of iron loss).

**Step-by-step procedure (KTU lab protocol):**
1. Keep the secondary terminals **open-circuited**.
2. Connect the **LV winding** to a variac through an ammeter, voltmeter, and wattmeter (current coil in series, voltage coil in parallel).
3. Slowly raise the variac output until the voltmeter reads **rated primary voltage V₀ = V₁rated**.
4. Note the readings: **V₀, I₀, P₀**.
5. Power factor at no load: `cos φ₀ = P₀ / (V₀ · I₀)`.

**Branch current decomposition:**
- Working (iron-loss) component: `I_w = I₀ cos φ₀`
- Magnetizing component: `I_μ = I₀ sin φ₀`

**Equivalent shunt-branch parameters (referred to test side):**
- Core-loss resistance: `R_c = V₀ / I_w = V₀² / P₀`
- Magnetizing reactance: `X_m = V₀ / I_μ`

### 2.2 Short-Circuit Test — Operational Logic

**Why reduced voltage?**
A short-circuited transformer presents only its small leakage impedance. To avoid damaging the windings, we apply just enough voltage to circulate full-load current.

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. **Short-circuit** the LV winding through a thick conductor (or short the HV if testing from LV side).
2. Connect the variac to the **other (HV) winding** with meters in circuit.
3. Raise the variac gradually until the ammeter reads **rated full-load current I_sc = I_FL**.
4. Note the readings: **V_sc, I_sc, P_sc**.
5. The applied voltage is small → core flux is tiny → iron loss is **negligible** compared to copper loss.
6. Therefore, the wattmeter reading ≈ **Full-load copper loss (P_cu, FL)**.

**Equivalent series-impedance parameters (referred to test side):**
- Equivalent impedance: `Z₀₁ = V_sc / I_sc`
- Equivalent resistance: `R₀₁ = P_sc / I_sc²`
- Equivalent leakage reactance: `X₀₁ = √(Z₀₁² − R₀₁²)`

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Referred Side |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iron (core) loss | `P_i = P_0` | W (or kW) | Test (usually LV) |
| 2 | F.L. copper loss | `P_cu = P_sc` | W (or kW) | Test (usually HV) |
| 3 | No-load p.f. | `cos φ_0 = P_0 / (V_0 · I_0)` | dimensionless | — |
| 4 | Working component | `I_w = I_0 · cos φ_0` | A | Test side |
| 5 | Magnetizing component | `I_μ = I_0 · sin φ_0` | A | Test side |
| 6 | Core-loss resistance | `R_c = V_0 / I_w = V_0² / P_0` | Ω | Test side |
| 7 | Magnetizing reactance | `X_m = V_0 / I_μ` | Ω | Test side |
| 8 | Eq. impedance | `Z_01 = V_sc / I_sc` | Ω | Test side |
| 9 | Eq. resistance | `R_01 = P_sc / I_sc²` | Ω | Test side |
| 10 | Eq. reactance | `X_01 = √(Z_01² − R_01²)` | Ω | Test side |
| 11 | Efficiency at load x | `η = (x · S · cos φ) / (x · S · cos φ + P_i + x² · P_cu) · 100` | % | — |
| 12 | Voltage regulation | `% Reg = [I · (R_02 cos φ ± X_02 sin φ)] / V_FL · 100` | % | Secondary side |
| 13 | Side-to-side referral | `R_LV = R_HV · (V_LV / V_HV)²` | Ω | Common side |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention in Regulation formula:** Use **(+)** for **lagging** (inductive) load, **(**−**)** for **leading** (capacitive) load. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These two tests form the **factory acceptance test (FAT)** of every power transformer in India. The data sheets (P₀ and P_sc) printed on the **transformer nameplate** are *exactly* the readings you would obtain in the lab. Utility companies use them to:
- Calculate the **maximum efficiency load point**: `x_max = √(P_i / P_cu)`.
- Decide transformer **paralleling** compatibility (same R/X ratio and polarity).
- Estimate **transformer loading** without expensive load banks.
- Plan **tariff structures** (iron loss is constant 24×7, copper loss varies with load).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Efficiency from OC & SC Test Data

The output power at a fractional load `x` and power factor `cos φ` is:

$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi$$

The total losses at that load are:

$$P_{loss} = P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu}$$

where `P_i` is read directly from the OC test wattmeter and `P_cu` is read from the SC test wattmeter (at full-load current).

By definition, efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

Differentiate the loss expression `P_loss` with respect to `x` and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_{loss}}{dx} = 2x \cdot P_{cu} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x \cdot P_{cu} = 0$$

This is the *constant* part. The condition for *maximum* η occurs when the *variable* copper loss equals the *constant* iron loss:

$$x^2 \cdot P_{cu} = P_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu}}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:**
> A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer was tested:
> - **OC test (on LV side):** 200 V, 0.75 A, 70 W
> - **SC test (on HV side):** 18 V, 12.5 A, 90 W
>
> Determine: (i) Iron loss and F.L. copper loss. (ii) Efficiency at full-load, 0.8 p.f. lagging. (iii) Efficiency at half-load, unity p.f. (iv) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to LV side. (v) Voltage regulation at full-load, 0.8 p.f. lagging.

**Step 1 — Identify the losses**

From the wattmeter readings directly:
- Iron loss: `P_i = P_0 = 70 W`
- Full-load copper loss: `P_cu = P_sc = 90 W`
- Rated current on HV side: `I_FL = 5000 / 400 = 12.5 A` ✓ (matches the SC test)

**Step 2 — Full-load efficiency at 0.8 p.f. lagging**

Output power: `P_out = S · cos φ = 5000 · 0.8 = 4000 W`

Total losses at full load: `P_loss = 70 + 1² · 90 = 160 W`

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 160} \times 100 = \frac{4000}{4160} \times 100 = 96.15\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Half-load efficiency at unity p.f.**

Output power: `P_out = 0.5 · 5000 · 1 = 2500 W`

Total losses at half load: `P_loss = 70 + (0.5)² · 90 = 70 + 22.5 = 92.5 W`

$$\eta_{1/2} = \frac{2500}{2500 + 92.5} \times 100 = \frac{2500}{2592.5} \times 100 = 96.43\ \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting observation:** Although half-load output is half, the efficiency is *higher* (96.43 % vs. 96.15 %). This is because the iron loss dominates at light loads.

**Step 4 — Equivalent circuit parameters referred to LV side**

Turns ratio (HV/LV): `K = 400 / 200 = 2`.  Referral factor LV from HV: `K² = 4`.

*From OC test (LV side):*
- No-load p.f.: `cos φ₀ = P_0 / (V_0 · I_0) = 70 / (200 · 0.75) = 0.4667`
- `sin φ₀ = √(1 − 0.4667²) = √(1 − 0.2178) = √0.7822 = 0.8844`
- Working component: `I_w = 0.75 · 0.4667 = 0.350 A`
- Magnetizing component: `I_μ = 0.75 · 0.8844 = 0.663 A`
- `R_c = V_0 / I_w = 200 / 0.350 = 571.43 Ω`
- `X_m = V_0 / I_μ = 200 / 0.663 = 301.66 Ω`

*From SC test (HV side), then referred to LV:*
- `Z_02 = V_sc / I_sc = 18 / 12.5 = 1.44 Ω`  (on HV side)
- `R_02 = P_sc / I_sc² = 90 / (12.5)² = 90 / 156.25 = 0.576 Ω`  (on HV side)
- `X_02 = √(Z_02² − R_02²) = √(1.44² − 0.576²) = √(2.0736 − 0.3318) = √1.7418 = 1.320 Ω`  (on HV side)

Referring to LV (multiply by `(V_LV/V_HV)² = (1/2)² = 0.25`):

$$R_{01,\,LV} = 0.576 \times 0.25 = 0.144 \ \Omega$$

$$X_{01,\,LV} = 1.320 \times 0.25 = 0.330 \ \Omega$$

Total referred to LV: `R_01 + R_02 = 0.144 + 0.144 = 0.288 Ω` and `X_01 + X_02 = 0.330 + 0.330 = 0.660 Ω`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximation used in KTU labs:** Often we treat `R_01 = R_02′` and `X_01 = X_02′` (i.e., we assume the SC-test impedance is split *equally* between the two windings). This is acceptable when the rating plate does not give separate winding resistances.

**Step 5 — Voltage regulation at F.L., 0.8 p.f. lagging**

Referred to LV side, at F.L., with the load on LV side:

`cos φ = 0.8`, `sin φ = 0.6`, `V_FL = 200 V`, `I = 5000 / 200 = 25 A`.

For lagging load, use **(+)** sign:

$$V_{drop} = I \cdot (R_{01} \cos\phi + X_{01} \sin\phi)$$

$$V_{drop} = 25 \cdot (0.288 \cdot 0.8 + 0.660 \cdot 0.6) = 25 \cdot (0.2304 + 0.3960) = 25 \cdot 0.6264 = 15.66 \ \text{V}$$

$$\% \text{Reg} = \frac{15.66}{200} \times 100 = 7.83\ \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to refer `R_02` and `X_02` to the LV side when the load is on the LV side. The regulation formula must use parameters on the *same side* as the load terminals.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open-Circuit Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[(AC Mains 50Hz)] --> V1[Variac Autotransformer]
    V1 --> A1[Ammeter A0]
    A1 --> WC1[Wattmeter Current Coil]
    WC1 --> W1[Primary Winding LV]
    W1 --> R1((Return))
    V1 -. voltage coil .-> WV1[Wattmeter Voltage Coil]
    WV1 --> W1
    V1 --> VM1[Voltmeter V0]
    VM1 --> W1
    SEC[(Secondary Open Circuit)]
    W1 -.induction.-> SEC
    P0["P0 = Iron Loss Pi"]
    WV1 --- P0
```

**Reading interpretation:**
- `V0` = rated primary voltage (read from `VM1`)
- `I0` = no-load current (read from `A1`)
- `P0` = iron loss in watts (read from `Wattmeter`)

### 4.2 Short-Circuit Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC2[(AC Mains 50Hz)] --> V2[Variac Autotransformer]
    V2 --> A2[Ammeter A_sc]
    A2 --> WC2[Wattmeter Current Coil]
    WC2 --> W2[Primary Winding HV]
    W2 --> R2((Return))
    V2 -. voltage coil .-> WV2[Wattmeter Voltage Coil]
    WV2 --> W2
    V2 --> VM2[Voltmeter V_sc]
    VM2 --> W2
    SEC2[(Secondary Short Circuit)]
    W2 -.induction.-> SEC2
    PSC["Psc = Full Load Copper Loss Pcu"]
    WV2 --- PSC
```

**Reading interpretation:**
- `V_sc` = short-circuit voltage (typically 5–10 % of rated)
- `I_sc` = full-load current (read from `A2`)
- `P_sc` = full-load copper loss in watts (read from `Wattmeter`)

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How the Two Tests Combine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OC[Open Circuit Test Side]
        OC_V[V0 volts] --> OC_PF[cos phi 0 = P0 / V0 I0]
        OC_I[I0 amps] --> OC_PF
        OC_PF --> IW[Iw = I0 cos phi 0]
        OC_PF --> IMU[Imu = I0 sin phi 0]
        IW --> RC[Rc = V0 / Iw]
        IMU --> XM[Xm = V0 / Imu]
        OC_W[P0 watts] --> PIRON[Iron Loss Pi = P0]
    end

    subgraph SC[Short Circuit Test Side]
        SC_V[Vsc volts] --> Z01[Z01 = Vsc / Isc]
        SC_I[Isc amps] --> Z01
        SC_W[Psc watts] --> R01C[R01 = Psc / Isc2]
        Z01 --> X01C[X01 = sqrt of Z01 squared minus R01 squared]
        R01C --> PCU[Full Load Copper Loss Pcu = Psc]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Performance]
        PIRON --> EFF[Efficiency at any load and power factor]
        PCU --> EFF
        R01C --> REG[Voltage Regulation]
        X01C --> REG
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is the Open-Circuit Test conducted on the LV side of a transformer, and the Short-Circuit Test on the HV side?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The OC test is performed on the **LV side** because (i) the LV winding draws a small, easily measurable no-load current, (ii) applying rated LV voltage requires a variac of lower rating, making the test **economical and safe**, and (iii) the LV side instruments (ammeter, voltmeter) can be of lower range. The SC test is performed on the **HV side** because (i) the HV winding has higher turns and lower full-load current, so the test current is **manageable**, (ii) the applied SC voltage (5–10 % of rated) is safer to handle on the HV side, and (iii) the wattmeter reading directly gives the **full-load copper loss**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the iron loss obtained from the OC test, and the copper loss from the SC test? Justify with the loss expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In the **OC test**, the secondary is open, so secondary copper loss = 0. Primary copper loss is `I₀² R₁` and is negligible since `I₀ ≈ 2–5 %` of full-load current. The wattmeter therefore reads essentially the **iron (core) loss** `P_i`. In the **SC test**, the applied voltage is only 5–10 % of rated, so the core flux is small and the iron loss is negligible. The wattmeter therefore reads essentially the **full-load copper loss** `P_cu = I_sc² R₀₁`.

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (per KTU ESE pattern):** Answer **any ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **Open-Circuit Test** on a single-phase transformer. How are the iron loss and the no-load circuit parameters obtained from the test readings?

**(b) [7 marks]** A 2 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:
- OC test (LV side): 200 V, 0.6 A, 36 W
- SC test (HV side): 15 V, 5 A, 30 W

Calculate (i) the iron loss and full-load copper loss, (ii) the efficiency at 75 % of full-load at 0.85 p.f. lagging, and (iii) the equivalent resistance and reactance referred to the LV side.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [As per Section 2.1 — explain with circuit, instruments used, procedure, formulas for `R_c`, `X_m`, and note that `P_0 = P_i`. Mention that the test is done on the LV side, variac is used to gradually raise voltage, and at rated voltage the readings `V_0, I_0, P_0` are noted.]

**(b) Step-by-step:**

(i) Losses:
- `P_i = P_0 = 36 W`
- Rated HV current: `I_FL = 2000 / 400 = 5 A` ✓ (matches SC test ammeter reading)
- `P_cu = P_sc = 30 W`

(ii) Efficiency at `x = 0.75`, `cos φ = 0.85`:
- Output: `P_out = x · S · cos φ = 0.75 · 2000 · 0.85 = 1275 W`
- Losses: `P_loss = 36 + (0.75)² · 30 = 36 + 16.875 = 52.875 W`

$$\eta = \frac{1275}{1275 + 52.875} \times 100 = \frac{1275}{1327.875} \times 100 = 96.02\ \%$$

(iii) Equivalent parameters referred to LV:
- Turns ratio HV/LV: `K = 400/200 = 2`, `K² = 4`
- From SC test (HV side):
  - `Z_02 = V_sc / I_sc = 15 / 5 = 3.0 Ω`
  - `R_02 = P_sc / I_sc² = 30 / 25 = 1.2 Ω`
  - `X_02 = √(3.0² − 1.2²) = √(9 − 1.44) = √7.56 = 2.75 Ω`
- Referred to LV (multiply by `1/4`):
  - `R_01 (LV) = 1.2 / 4 = 0.30 Ω`
  - `X_01 (LV) = 2.75 / 4 = 0.6875 Ω`
- Total referred to LV (assuming equal split): `R_total = 0.60 Ω`, `X_total = 1.375 Ω`.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **Short-Circuit Test** on a single-phase transformer. Show how the equivalent impedance, resistance, and leakage reactance referred to the test side are calculated.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 10 kVA, 1000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer yielded the following:
- OC test (LV side): 200 V, 1.2 A, 80 W
- SC test (HV side): 60 V, 10 A, 200 W

Compute (i) the efficiency at full-load, unity p.f., (ii) the load at which maximum efficiency occurs, and (iii) the voltage regulation at full-load, 0.8 p.f. lagging (referring parameters to the LV side).

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [As per Section 2.2 — explain circuit, instruments, procedure. Show:
`Z_01 = V_sc / I_sc`,
`R_01 = P_sc / I_sc²`,
`X_01 = √(Z_01² − R_01²)`.
Emphasize that iron loss is negligible because the applied voltage is small.]

**(b) Step-by-step:**

(i) F.L. efficiency at unity p.f.:
- `P_i = 80 W`, `P_cu = 200 W`
- Output: `10000 · 1 = 10000 W`
- Total losses: `80 + 200 = 280 W`

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10000}{10280} \times 100 = 97.28\ \%$$

(ii) Load for maximum efficiency:
- `x_max = √(P_i / P_cu) = √(80 / 200) = √0.4 = 0.632`
- i.e., **63.2 % of full-load**.

(iii) Voltage regulation at F.L., 0.8 p.f. lagging:
- Turns ratio HV/LV: `K = 1000/200 = 5`, `K² = 25`
- From SC test (HV side):
  - `R_02 (HV) = P_sc / I_sc² = 200 / 100 = 2.0 Ω`
  - `Z_02 (HV) = V_sc / I_sc = 60 / 10 = 6.0 Ω`
  - `X_02 (HV) = √(36 − 4) = √32 = 5.657 Ω`
- Referred to LV:
  - `R_02 (LV) = 2.0 / 25 = 0.08 Ω`
  - `X_02 (LV) = 5.657 / 25 = 0.2263 Ω`
- Assuming equal split: `R_total (LV) = 0.16 Ω`, `X_total (LV) = 0.4525 Ω`.
- Full-load current on LV side: `I = 10000 / 200 = 50 A`
- `cos φ = 0.8`, `sin φ = 0.6`

$$V_{drop} = 50 \cdot (0.16 \cdot 0.8 + 0.4525 \cdot 0.6) = 50 \cdot (0.128 + 0.2715) = 50 \cdot 0.3995 = 19.98 \ \text{V}$$

$$\% \text{Reg} = \frac{19.98}{200} \times 100 = 9.99\ \% \approx 10\ \%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to refer parameters** to a common side before plugging them into the regulation formula — this alone costs **3 to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error** in regulation — using `(−)` for lagging load or vice versa. Lagging uses `+`; leading uses `−`.
> 3. **Confusing `I_0` with `I_sc`** — never substitute the no-load current into the SC formulas.
> 4. **Squaring `K` incorrectly** — referral to LV from HV requires multiplication by `(V_LV / V_HV)²`, not `(V_HV / V_LV)²`.
> 5. **Missing units** in the final answer — KTU strictly deducts marks for `Ω` missing next to resistance/reactance values.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OC test** measures **iron loss**; the secondary is **open**; rated voltage is applied to the **LV side**; variac is essential.
- **SC test** measures **full-load copper loss**; the secondary is **shorted**; **reduced voltage** (5–10 % of rated) is applied to circulate full-load current.
- `P_0` (from OC) = `P_i` (iron loss); `P_sc` (from SC) = `P_cu` (full-load copper loss).
- Shunt-branch parameters (`R_c`, `X_m`) come from the **OC test**.
- Series parameters (`R_01`, `X_01`) come from the **SC test**.
- `cos φ_0 = P_0 / (V_0 I_0)`; `I_w = I_0 cos φ_0`; `I_μ = I_0 sin φ_0`.
- **Efficiency formula** uses `x` (fractional load) and `cos φ`; **maximum efficiency** occurs at `x_max = √(P_i / P_cu)`.
- **Regulation** requires parameters on the **same side** as the load; lagging load uses **(+)** sign.
- **Referral factor** between sides: multiply resistance/reactance by the square of the turns ratio.
- Always tabulate V, I, W readings for **both** tests; KTU record books require this.
- The two tests together let you **pre-determine** efficiency and regulation **without actual loading** — making them indispensable in transformer manufacturing and FAT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load test on single-phase transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Load test on single-phase transformer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Load Test on a Single-Phase Transformer** is a direct, polarly informative experimental procedure employed to determine the **working efficiency** and the **voltage regulation** of a single-phase transformer under varying load conditions. The test is conducted by connecting a controllable load across the secondary (output) terminals and progressively increasing the load from no-load (NL) to full-load (FL) while maintaining the rated primary voltage through a **variac (auto-transformer)**. Measurements of input power, output power, primary and secondary voltages, and currents are recorded. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** The load test is a *direct method* of testing a transformer under actual loading conditions, where output power is dissipated in a real load (lamp bank, rheostat, or resistive load bank). It is the most authentic indicator of transformer performance in the KTU lab curriculum for PCEEL407. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe and Tap" Model Imagine a **municipal water pumping station** that pushes water through a pipe to your home. The pump station is the *primary side*, the pipe is the *magnetic core (flux path)*, and your home tap is the *secondary side* delivering useful water (load current). - When the tap is **closed** (no-load) → water flows internally, but no useful work is done. Energy is wasted in friction (analogous to **iron/core losses**). - When the tap is **opened** (load increases) → water comes out, but you also lose pressure due to pipe friction. More water = more friction loss (analogous to **copper losses**, which vary with I²). - The **pressure drop** between no-load and full-load is the **voltage regulation** — it tells you how much "pressure" (voltage) the system can no longer deliver. - The **efficiency** is the ratio of water actually reaching your home (output) to water pumped from the station (input). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The load test is the *only* test that gives **simultaneous**, real-world data for **efficiency AND voltage regulation** in a single experimental run, making it the cornerstone of transformer evaluation. ## 1.3 Standard Test Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | KTU Lab Value | |---|---|---|---| | Rated Primary Voltage | $V_1$ | Volts (V) | **230 V / 110 V AC** | | Rated Secondary Voltage | $V_2$ | Volts (V) | As per nameplate | | Rated Power | S | kVA / VA | **1 kVA typical lab rating** | | Line Frequency | f | Hertz (Hz) | **50 Hz** | | Unity Power Factor | cos φ | Dimensionless | **1.0 (for resistive load)** | | Ambient Temperature | T | °C | **27 °C (room temperature)** | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** KTU lab records almost always assume a **unity power factor load** unless otherwise specified — so you can write $\cos\phi = 1$ in all efficiency calculations. Always state this assumption explicitly in your record to earn full marks. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Load Test is Conducted The load test is performed because it **directly** captures the two most important performance metrics in transformer engineering: 1. **Efficiency (η):** The ratio of useful electrical output power delivered to the load, to the total electrical input power drawn from the source. 2. **Voltage Regulation (VR):** The change in secondary terminal voltage when the load is applied, expressed as a percentage of the full-load voltage. Unlike the **Open-Circuit (OC) test** (which yields only iron losses) or the **Short-Circuit (SC) test** (which yields only copper losses), the load test integrates **both** loss components into a single, end-to-end measurement. ## 2.2 The Underlying Physics — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — No-Load Condition (Before Load is Applied)** - With the secondary open-circuited, the transformer draws only a small magnetising current ($I_0$) on the primary. - The secondary terminal voltage equals the rated secondary voltage ($V_{2NL}$). - Input power = Iron loss ($P_i$) only. - Output power = **0 W** (no load connected yet). **Step 2 — Load is Progressively Increased** - A resistive load (lamp bank or rheostat) is connected to the secondary. - Load current $I_2$ is increased in steps (typically 5 steps: 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of rated load). - For each step, primary voltage is **maintained at the rated value** using the variac. **Step 3 — Loss Components During Loading** - **Iron (Core) Losses ($P_i$):** Remain essentially **constant** throughout the test (depends on $V_1$ and $f$, which are held fixed). - **Copper (Ohmic) Losses ($P_{cu}$):** Vary as the **square** of the load current, since $P_{cu} = I_2^2 \cdot R_{2e}$ where $R_{2e}$ is the equivalent resistance referred to the secondary. **Step 4 — Efficiency Computation** - Output power: $P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2$ - Input power: $P_{in} = V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos\phi_1$ - Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ **Step 5 — Voltage Regulation Computation** - Voltage regulation quantifies the *drop* in secondary terminal voltage from no-load to full-load. - $VR\% = \dfrac{V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}}{V_{2FL}} \times 100\%$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas** — they are guaranteed to appear in the viva, lab record evaluation, and ESE theory questions. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Output Power | $P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | $\cos\phi = 1$ for resistive load | | 2 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos\phi_1$ | W | Measured by wattmeter on primary side | | 3 | Total Loss | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | W | Sum of iron + copper losses | | 4 | Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Used in load test | | 5 | Efficiency (Indirect) | $\eta = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot I_2}{V_2 \cdot I_2 + P_i + P_{cu}} \times 100$ | % | Used in OC + SC test combination | | 6 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_2^2 \cdot R_{2e}$ | W | $R_{2e}$ = equiv. resistance ref. to sec. | | 7 | Iron Loss | $P_i = P_{in(NL)}$ at rated $V_1$ | W | Found from no-load input power | | 8 | Voltage Regulation | $VR\% = \dfrac{V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}}{V_{2FL}} \times 100$ | % | Always taken at full-load reference | | 9 | Per-Unit VR | $VR_{pu} = \dfrac{V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}}{V_{2NL}}$ | p.u. | Reference is no-load voltage | | 10 | Transformation Ratio | $K = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Dimensionless | Primary to secondary turns | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Domain | Application of Load Test Data | |---|---| | **Power Utilities (KSEB, NTPC)** | Transformer acceptance testing in substations before commissioning | | **Manufacturing Industries** | Quality control of distribution transformers (up to 2500 kVA) | | **Renewable Energy (Solar Inverters)** | Step-up transformer performance verification in PV farms | | **Electric Traction (Indian Railways)** | Testing of auxiliary transformers in locomotives | | **Industrial Motor Drives** | Isolation transformer performance under motor load profiles | | **Hospital Backup Systems** | UPS transformer efficiency validation for critical care equipment | > [!TIP] > **Why does the KTU board emphasise this experiment?** Because load test data directly informs **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) star rating** of distribution transformers in India, making it an industry-relevant skill. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Efficiency from Load Test Measurements ### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation Starting from the fundamental definition of efficiency as the ratio of output power to input power: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% $$ For a single-phase transformer feeding a load with power factor $\cos\phi$: $$ P_{out} = V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2 $$ $$ P_{in} = V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos\phi_1 $$ Substituting into the efficiency expression: $$ \eta = \frac{V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi_2}{V_1 \cdot I_1 \cdot \cos\phi_1} \times 100\% $$ For a **resistive (unity power factor) load**, $\cos\phi_1 = \cos\phi_2 = 1$, the expression simplifies to: $$ \eta = \frac{V_2 \cdot I_2}{V_1 \cdot I_1} \times 100\% $$ Equivalently, since $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$, we can write: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_i + P_{cu}} \times 100\% $$ This is the **alternate efficiency formula** that KTU examiners love to test — it links the load test to OC and SC tests. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Voltage Regulation from No-Load and Full-Load Measurements Voltage regulation measures the **relative change** in secondary terminal voltage as load is applied. Starting from the definition: $$ VR = \frac{V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}}{V_{2FL}} $$ Expressed as a percentage: $$ VR\% = \frac{V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}}{V_{2FL}} \times 100 $$ **Interpretation of Each Term:** - $V_{2NL}$ = Secondary terminal voltage at **no-load** (with rated primary voltage applied). This is the *open-circuit secondary voltage* and is the *highest* voltage the secondary will ever deliver. - $V_{2FL}$ = Secondary terminal voltage at **full-load** (with rated primary voltage and rated secondary current flowing). This is the *actual working voltage* delivered to the load. The **difference** $V_{2NL} - V_{2FL}$ represents the voltage *lost* due to the internal impedance of the transformer windings (resistance + leakage reactance drops). > [!NOTE] > **Positive Regulation** indicates the secondary voltage *drops* with load (typical for lagging p.f. loads). **Negative Regulation** is possible for highly capacitive loads, where the secondary voltage *rises* with load — this is a viva-favourite question! --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) ### Given Problem Statement > A **1 kVA, 230 V / 115 V, 50 Hz** single-phase transformer is subjected to a load test. The following readings are obtained at full load with a resistive load: $V_1 = 230\,V$, $I_1 = 4.8\,A$, $V_2 = 110\,V$, $I_2 = 8.7\,A$, $W_1 = 1010\,W$, $W_2 = 957\,W$. At no-load, the secondary terminal voltage is $V_{2NL} = 115\,V$. Calculate: **(a)** the efficiency at full load, and **(b)** the voltage regulation. ### Part (a) — Efficiency Calculation **Step 1: Identify input and output power.** The wattmeter on the primary side reads $W_1 = 1010\,W$, which is the **input power** $P_{in}$.
