The induced EMFs referred to secondary are: $$E_A = \dfrac{V_1}{a_A}, \quad E_B = \dfrac{V_1}{a_B}$$ **Step 2: Recognize the loop** Since both secondaries are connected in parallel with the load open (no-load condition), the two EMFs $E_A$ and $E_B$ oppose each other in a closed series loop containing impedances $Z_A$ and $Z_B$ (both referred to secondary). **Step 3: Apply KVL around the loop** The difference $\Delta E = E_A - E_B$ drives the circulating current $I_c$ through the series combination $Z_A + Z_B$: $$\Delta E = I_c \cdot (Z_A + Z_B)$$ **Step 4: Solve for Ic** $$I_c = \dfrac{\Delta E}{Z_A + Z_B} = \dfrac{E_A - E_B}{Z_A + Z_B}$$ **Step 5: Magnitude (for impedance in polar form)** $$\vert I_c \vert = \dfrac{\vert E_A - E_B \vert}{\vert Z_A + Z_B \vert} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\sqrt{(R_A + R_B)^2 + (X_A + X_B)^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** $I_c$ exists **even at no-load** and is **independent of the load**. It only depends on the turns-ratio mismatch and the transformer impedances. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Load Sharing (Same Turns Ratio, Different Ratings/%Z) **Step 1: Define currents** Let $I_L$ be the total load current, and $I_A$, $I_B$ be the currents supplied by Transformers A and B respectively: $$I_L = I_A + I_B \quad \text{(KCL at secondary bus)}$$ **Step 2: Voltage drop equality** The voltage drop across each transformer's secondary impedance must be equal because both transformers are connected to the **same** secondary bus voltage $V_2$: $$I_A \cdot Z_A = I_B \cdot Z_B$$ (Each transformer's internal drop = $E - V_2$) **Step 3: Solve the simultaneous equations** From Step 2: $I_B = I_A \cdot \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B}$. Substituting in Step 1: $$I_L = I_A + I_A \cdot \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B} = I_A \cdot \dfrac{Z_A + Z_B}{Z_B}$$ $$\boxed{I_A = I_L \cdot \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B} \quad , \quad I_B = I_L \cdot \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B}}$$ This is the **current divider rule** in disguise. **Step 4: Express in kVA** Multiplying both sides by $V_2$ (common secondary voltage): $$S_A = S_L \cdot \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_A + Z_B}, \quad S_B = S_L \cdot \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_B}$$ **Step 5: Special case — equal %Z (per-unit) but different ratings** If both transformers have the **same per-unit impedance** $Z_{pu}$, then: $$Z_A = Z_{pu} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2}{S_{A,rated}}, \quad Z_B = Z_{pu} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2}{S_{B,rated}}$$ Substituting, the $V_2^2 \cdot Z_{pu}$ terms cancel: $$S_A = S_L \cdot \dfrac{S_{A,rated}}{S_{A,rated} + S_{B,rated}}, \quad S_B = S_L \cdot \dfrac{S_{B,rated}}{S_{A,rated} + S_{B,rated}}$$ > Load is shared **proportionally to rating** when %Z is the same. **Step 6: General case — unequal %Z** If both rating AND %Z differ, the share becomes: $$S_A = S_L \cdot \dfrac{S_{A,rated}/\%Z_A}{S_{A,rated}/\%Z_A + S_{B,rated}/\%Z_B}, \quad S_B = S_L \cdot \dfrac{S_{B,rated}/\%Z_B}{S_{A,rated}/\%Z_A + S_{B,rated}/\%Z_B}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) > **Problem:** Two single-phase transformers are connected in parallel to supply a common load of **30 kVA at 0.8 p.f. lagging**. > - Transformer A: **20 kVA**, **200/100 V**, **%Z = 4%** > - Transformer B: **10 kVA**, **200/100 V**, **%Z = 3%** > > Both have the **same turns ratio**. Calculate: > (a) The actual impedances of each transformer referred to the secondary. > (b) The kVA load shared by each transformer. > (c) Comment on the loading. **Solution:** **Part (a): Actual impedances** Referred to the secondary side ($V_2 = 100$ V): $$Z_A = \left(\dfrac{4}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(100)^2}{20{,}000} = 0.04 \cdot \dfrac{10{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.04 \cdot 0.5 = 0.02 \text{ Ω}$$ $$Z_B = \left(\dfrac{3}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(100)^2}{10{,}000} = 0.03 \cdot \dfrac{10{,}000}{10{,}000} = 0.03 \cdot 1 = 0.03 \text{ Ω}$$ **Part (b): Load sharing** Using the general formula (unequal ratings AND unequal %Z): $$S_A = 30 \cdot \dfrac{20/4}{20/4 + 10/3} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{5}{5 + 3.333} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{5}{8.333} = 30 \cdot 0.6 = 18 \text{ kVA}$$ $$S_B = 30 \cdot \dfrac{10/3}{20/4 + 10/3} = 30 \cdot \dfrac{3.333}{8.333} = 30 \cdot 0.4 = 12 \text{ kVA}$$ **Verification:** $S_A + S_B = 18 + 12 = 30$ kVA ✅ **Part (c): Loading analysis** - Transformer A is loaded to $\dfrac{18}{20} \times 100\% = 90\%$ of its rating. - Transformer B is loaded to $\dfrac{12}{10} \times 100\% = 120\%$ of its rating — **OVERLOADED** ⚠️ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Even though both transformers are 30 kVA combined (1.5× of total rated), Transformer B (lower %Z) took more load than its share. The bank is **not safely loaded** at 30 kVA — the safe limit is when neither exceeds 100%, which occurs at: > > $$S_{L,max} = 18 \cdot \dfrac{100}{90} = 20 \text{ kVA} \text{ (or equivalently } 12 \cdot \dfrac{100}{120} = 10 \cdot \text{)}$$ --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Setup — Two Dissimilar Transformers in Parallel ```mermaid graph TB BusP["PRIMARY BUS
V1 = 200 V AC"]:::bus BusS["SECONDARY BUS
V2 = 100 V AC"]:::bus Load["COMMON LOAD
S_L kVA, p.f. cos phi"]:::load TXA["TRANSFORMER A
SA = 20 kVA
200/100 V
%ZA = 4 percent"]:::txA TXB["TRANSFORMER B
SB = 10 kVA
200/100 V
%ZB = 3 percent"]:::txB BusP -->|"I1A"| TXA TXA -->|"I2A"| BusS BusP -->|"I1B"| TXB TXB -->|"I2B"| BusS BusS -->|"IL"| Load Loop["CIRCULATING CURRENT LOOP
if turns ratios differ
Ic = dE divided by ZA plus ZB"]:::loop TXA -. "ZA" .-> Loop TXB -. "ZB" .-> Loop Loop -. "Ic" .-> BusS classDef bus fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef txA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef txB fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loop fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Analytical Decision Flowchart — Lab Procedure ```mermaid graph TB Start(["START LAB EXPERIMENT 2"]):::startNode Step1["STEP 1: Check Nameplate Ratings
Record kVA, V1, V2, percent Z"]:::stepNode Step2["STEP 2: Perform Polarity Test
HVT or DC test to mark dots"]:::stepNode Step3["STEP 3: Identify Primary and Secondary Terminals"]:::stepNode Step4["STEP 4: Connect Primaries in Parallel
Same phase, same polarity"]:::stepNode Step5["STEP 5: Connect Secondaries with Switch Open
Insert ammeter in loop"]:::stepNode Step6["STEP 6: Close Paralleling Switch Slowly
Observe no load circulating current Ic"]:::stepNode Step7["STEP 7: Apply Resistive Load in Steps
0 percent, 25 percent, 50 percent, 75 percent, 100 percent"]:::stepNode Step8["STEP 8: Record IA, IB, IL, V2 at each step"]:::stepNode Step9["STEP 9: Calculate Load Shares and Compare"]:::stepNode Step10["STEP 10: Plot Load Share vs Total Load"]:::stepNode End(["END: Verify against theoretical formulas"]):::endNode Start --> Step1 Step1 --> Step2 Step2 --> Step3 Step3 --> Step4 Step4 --> Step5 Step5 --> Step6 Step6 --> Step7 Step7 --> Step8 Step8 --> Step9 Step9 --> Step10 Step10 --> End classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef stepNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Phasor Representation (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR V2Bus["V2 = Reference
0 degrees along real axis"]:::phasor IA["IA = Load current from Tx A
Lags V2 by phi_A"]:::phasorA IB["IB = Load current from Tx B
Lags V2 by phi_B"]:::phasorB IL["IL = IA + IB
Resultant phasor"]:::phasor IC["IC = Circulating current
Purely in phase with delta E
approximately 90 deg from V2"]:::phasorIC V2Bus --> IA V2Bus --> IB IA --> IL IB --> IL IC -. "added to IA" .-> IA classDef phasor fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phasorA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phasorB fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phasorIC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Mermaid cannot draw true phasor arrows with magnitude scaling, so these are **functional block diagrams** showing the relationships between the currents. In a physical lab, you would use a CRO or power analyzer to capture actual phasor relationships. --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **State the essential conditions that must be satisfied for the successful parallel operation of two single-phase transformers. Which of these are strictly mandatory?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The essential conditions for parallel operation of two single-phase transformers are: 1. **Same primary voltage rating** (mandatory) 2. **Same secondary voltage rating** (mandatory) 3. **Same polarity** (mandatory) — opposite polarity causes dead short-circuit 4. **Same frequency** (mandatory) 5. **Same turns ratio** (preferably equal — mismatch causes circulating current) 6. **Same per-unit impedance** (preferably equal — mismatch causes improper load sharing) The first four conditions are **strictly mandatory**; if violated, the transformers cannot be paralleled safely. The last two are ideal conditions — dissimilarity leads to circulating currents and unequal load sharing, but operation is still possible. *(Mark split: List 6 conditions — 2 marks; Classify mandatory vs ideal — 1 mark.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Define *circulating current* in the context of parallel-connected transformers. On what factors does it depend?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Circulating current ($I_c$)** is the current that flows in the closed loop formed by the secondary windings of two parallel-connected transformers **even when no external load is connected**. It is caused by the difference in induced EMFs ($E_A - E_B$) of the two transformers due to a mismatch in their turns ratios. **Factors on which it depends:** $$I_c = \dfrac{E_A - E_B}{Z_A + Z_B}$$ 1. **Difference in induced EMFs** ($\Delta E = E_A - E_B$) — proportional to turns-ratio mismatch. 2. **Sum of series impedances** ($Z_A + Z_B$) — both referred to the same side. Circulating current does **not** depend on the load and produces **additional copper losses** ($I_c^2 R$) in both transformers, reducing efficiency. *(Mark split: Definition — 1 mark; Formula — 1 mark; Factors & effect — 1 mark.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **(a) [7 marks]** Two single-phase transformers A and B are connected in parallel to supply a common load of **40 kVA at 0.85 p.f. lagging**. The details are: | Transformer | Rating | Voltage Ratio | %Z | |-------------|--------|---------------|-----| | A | 25 kVA | 230/115 V | 5% | | B | 15 kVA | 230/115 V | 4% | Assuming the turns ratios are identical, calculate: (i) The actual impedances of each transformer referred to the secondary. (ii) The kVA load shared by each transformer. (iii) Whether either transformer is overloaded. **Model Solution:** **(i) Actual impedances (referred to secondary, $V_2 = 115$ V):** $$Z_A = \left(\dfrac{5}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(115)^2}{25{,}000} = 0.05 \cdot \dfrac{13{,}225}{25{,}000} = 0.05 \cdot 0.529 = 0.02645 \text{ Ω}$$ $$Z_B = \left(\dfrac{4}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(115)^2}{15{,}000} = 0.04 \cdot \dfrac{13{,}225}{15{,}000} = 0.04 \cdot 0.8817 = 0.03527 \text{ Ω}$$ **(ii) Load sharing** (using the unequal %Z formula): $$S_A = 40 \cdot \dfrac{25/5}{25/5 + 15/4} = 40 \cdot \dfrac{5}{5 + 3.75} = 40 \cdot \dfrac{5}{8.75} = 40 \cdot 0.5714 = 22.86 \text{ kVA}$$ $$S_B = 40 \cdot \dfrac{15/4}{8.75} = 40 \cdot \dfrac{3.75}{8.75} = 40 \cdot 0.4286 = 17.14 \text{ kVA}$$ **Check:** $S_A + S_B = 22.86 + 17.14 = 40$ kVA ✅ **(iii) Loading analysis:** - Transformer A loading: $\dfrac{22.86}{25} \times 100\% = 91.4\%$ — **SAFE** - Transformer B loading: $\dfrac{17.14}{15} \times 100\% = 114.3\%$ — **OVERLOADED** ⚠️ Hence Transformer B is overloaded even when the total load (40 kVA) equals the combined rating (25 + 15 = 40 kVA). The maximum safe load occurs when Transformer B is at 100%: $$S_{L,max} = 17.14 \cdot \dfrac{100}{114.3} = 15 \text{ kVA} \text{ (limited by Tx B)}$$ *(Mark split: i) 2 marks; ii) 3 marks; iii) 2 marks.)* --- **(b) [7 marks]** With reference to Part (a), if the turns ratios of the two transformers differ slightly such that the no-load secondary voltages are **117 V** and **113 V** respectively (instead of 115 V), calculate the magnitude of the no-load circulating current. Assume the impedances have $R_A = 0.02$ Ω, $X_A = 0.017$ Ω, $R_B = 0.028$ Ω, $X_B = 0.022$ Ω (referred to secondary). **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** The difference in induced EMFs: $$\Delta E = E_A - E_B = 117 - 113 = 4 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate total series impedance: $$R_{total} = R_A + R_B = 0.02 + 0.028 = 0.048 \text{ Ω}$$ $$X_{total} = X_A + X_B = 0.017 + 0.022 = 0.039 \text{ Ω}$$ $$\vert Z_A + Z_B \vert = \sqrt{(0.048)^2 + (0.039)^2} = \sqrt{0.002304 + 0.001521} = \sqrt{0.003825} = 0.06185 \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 3:** Circulating current: $$\vert I_c \vert = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\vert Z_A + Z_B \vert} = \dfrac{4}{0.06185} = 64.67 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4:** This is a very large circulating current (since 25 kVA at 115 V corresponds to rated current ≈ 217 A, this is ~30% of rated current) — clearly **unacceptable** in practice. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Students often **forget to convert %Z to actual Ω** before applying the circulating current formula. > 2. Many use the *parallel combination* $Z_A \vert\vert Z_B$ instead of the *series sum* $Z_A + Z_B$ for the circulating current loop. **Always use series sum** — the two secondaries form a closed loop. > 3. Sign convention: $|I_c|$ is always positive — use the *magnitude* of $\Delta E$. *(Mark split: ΔE – 1 mark; Z total – 3 marks; Ic calculation – 2 marks; Comment – 1 mark.)* --- ### 5.3 ALTERNATIVE QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** Two single-phase transformers, both rated **230/115 V**, are connected in parallel. Their ratings and impedances are: | Transformer | kVA | %Z (R+jX) | |-------------|-----|-----------| | A | 10 | 4 + j6 | | B | 5 | 5 + j8 | Calculate the load shared by each when a total load of **12 kVA at 0.9 p.f. lagging** is applied. Also find the maximum load that can be supplied without overloading either transformer. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate actual impedances (referred to secondary, $V_2 = 115$ V): $$Z_A = (0.04 + j0.06) \cdot \dfrac{(115)^2}{10{,}000} = (0.04 + j0.06) \cdot 1.3225 = (0.0529 + j0.0794) \text{ Ω}$$ $$Z_B = (0.05 + j0.08) \cdot \dfrac{(115)^2}{5{,}000} = (0.05 + j0.08) \cdot 2.645 = (0.1323 + j0.2116) \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 2:** Total impedance: $$Z_A + Z_B = (0.1852 + j0.291) \text{ Ω}, \quad \vert Z_A + Z_B \vert = \sqrt{0.0343 + 0.0847} = \sqrt{0.1190} = 0.3449 \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 3:** Load sharing (using current divider, all in per-unit relative to own rating): For simplicity, use the kVA-based formula: $$S_A = 12 \cdot \dfrac{10/4}{10/4 + 5/5} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{2.5}{2.5 + 1.0} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{2.5}{3.5} = 8.57 \text{ kVA}$$ $$S_B = 12 \cdot \dfrac{5/5}{3.5} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{1.0}{3.5} = 3.43 \text{ kVA}$$ **Step 4:** Loading: - Tx A: $\dfrac{8.57}{10} = 85.7\%$ — **SAFE** - Tx B: $\dfrac{3.43}{5} = 68.6\%$ — **SAFE** **Step 5:** Maximum safe load: Tx A reaches 100% first when $S_L = 12 \cdot \dfrac{10}{8.57} = 14$ kVA (Tx B at that point: $3.43 \cdot \dfrac{14}{12} = 4$ kVA = 80% of Tx B rating). So **max safe load = 14 kVA**. --- **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the experimental procedure to perform the **polarity test** on two single-phase transformers before connecting them in parallel. Why is this test critical? **Model Solution:** **Polarity Test Procedure (AC Polarity / Subtractive Polarity Test):** 1. Connect the **H1 terminals** of both transformers together (primary side) using a shorting link. 2. Connect a **voltmeter** between the **X1 terminals** of both secondaries. 3. Apply a **reduced AC voltage** (say 10–20% of rated) to the primary of one transformer. 4. Measure: - $V_1$ across the primary (say 30 V) - $V_2$ across one secondary (say 15 V) - $V_3$ across the voltmeter (X1 to X1 of the two transformers) 5. **Interpret:** - If $V_3 = V_{2A} - V_{2B}$ (subtractive) → **Correct polarity** (additive voltage would mean $V_3 = V_{2A} + V_{2B}$, indicating reverse polarity) **Why is it critical?** - If two transformers are connected in parallel with **opposite polarity**, the instantaneous voltages oppose each other, creating a **dead short-circuit**. - The resulting fault current is limited only by the small leakage impedance and is typically 10–20 times the rated current. - This causes **catastrophic mechanical and thermal damage** within milliseconds. - Hence polarity verification is the **most critical pre-paralleling test**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Not mentioning the "subtractive" nature** of the test — students just say "measure voltage" without specifying the expected result. > 2. **Forgetting to apply reduced voltage** (using full primary voltage can be dangerous if polarity is wrong). > 3. **Not explaining the consequence** of skipping this test (e.g., catastrophic short-circuit, explosion, fire). > 4. In calculation problems, **mixing up per-unit and actual impedances** — always clarify which side you are referring to. > 5. **Forgetting to add circulating current to load current** when computing the *total* current through a transformer with both turns-ratio mismatch AND load. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Read this 5 times before the exam.** - ✅ Two transformers in parallel share the **same primary bus** and the **same secondary bus**. - ✅ "Dissimilar" means different **kVA**, **%Z**, or **turns ratio** (a). - ✅ **Mandatory conditions** (cannot violate): same primary V, same secondary V, same polarity, same frequency, same phase sequence (3-phase only). - ✅ **Ideal conditions** (can be relaxed but cause issues): same turns ratio, same %Z. - ✅ **Circulating current** $I_c = \dfrac{\Delta E}{Z_A + Z_B}$ exists **at no-load** if turns ratios differ. - ✅ **No circulating current at no-load** if turns ratios are equal — but load sharing may be unequal. - ✅ Load sharing with **equal %Z** → proportional to **kVA rating**. - ✅ Load sharing with **unequal %Z** → proportional to $\dfrac{kVA}{\%Z}$. - ✅ **Lower %Z transformer takes more load** — can lead to its overloading. - ✅ Actual impedance: $Z_{actual} = \left(\dfrac{\%Z}{100}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{S_{rated}}$ — referred to the side where V is the rated voltage. - ✅ **Polarity test** is the most critical pre-paralleling test — skipping it can destroy both transformers. - ✅ Total current of a transformer with both mismatch and load = $I_{load-share} + I_c$ (phasor sum). - ✅ For **identical transformers** (same kVA, same %Z, same turns ratio), each takes **exactly half** the load. - ✅ KTU exam pattern: 2-mark definitions, 3-mark lists, 7-mark calculations with both %Z conversion and load sharing. ---

topics.title: OC and SC tests on 3-phase transformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** OC and SC tests on 3-phase transformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Circuit (OC) Test** and **Short Circuit (SC) Test** are the two indirect (non-loading) experiments performed on a 3-phase transformer to determine its complete equivalent circuit parameters, efficiency, and voltage regulation — **without actually loading the transformer**.

**Open Circuit (OC) Test** — Also called the **No-Load Test**, it is performed by applying **rated voltage at rated frequency** to one winding (usually the **LV side**) while keeping the other winding (HV side) **open-circuited**. The test yields the **iron (core) losses** *P*₀ and the **shunt branch parameters** of the equivalent circuit: core-loss resistance *R*ₒc and magnetising reactance *X*ₘ.

**Short Circuit (SC) Test** — Also called the **Impedance Test**, it is performed by **short-circuiting one winding** (usually the LV side) and applying a **reduced voltage** (typically **5 % – 10 %** of rated) to the other winding (HV side) such that **full-load current** flows. It yields the **full-load copper losses** *P*sc and the **series branch parameters**: equivalent resistance *R*₀₁ and equivalent leakage reactance *X*₀₁.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Wattmeter Method:** Because the transformer is 3-phase, the power is always measured using the **two-wattmeter method** regardless of the connection (Y or Δ). The total 3-phase power is the algebraic sum **P = W₁ + W₂**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a water pump pushing fluid through a pipe network. The **OC test** is like running the pump with the delivery tap closed — you measure how much the pump "leaks" energy just to keep the system pressurised (those are the *iron losses* due to the magnetic field being maintained with no useful work). The **SC test** is like fully opening the tap and measuring the friction in the pipes themselves (those are the *copper losses* due to current flowing through the windings). Together they account for **everything that happens inside the transformer** without ever having to connect an actual load.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Rated Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India / KTU standard).
- **Standard Short-Circuit Voltage (%Z):** Typically **5 % – 12 %** of rated voltage.
- **Standard No-Load Current (%I₀):** Typically **2 % – 6 %** of full-load current for well-designed units.
- **Star connection quantities:** *V*ph = *V*L / √3 ; *I*ph = *I*L
- **Delta connection quantities:** *V*ph = *V*L ; *I*ph = *I*L / √3
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Two Wattmeters and Why This Specific Side?

### Why two wattmeters?
A 3-phase, 3-wire system has no neutral, so power in each phase cannot be measured independently. The **two-wattmeter method** uses the line currents and the line-to-line voltages to compute total 3-phase active power without needing a neutral.

### Why OC test on LV side?
1. **Safety** — rated LV voltage is much safer to handle than HV.
2. **Economy** — voltmeters and wattmeters required are cheaper for the LV rating.
3. **Higher no-load current on LV side** — easier to measure on ammeters.
4. **Iron loss depends only on voltage magnitude and frequency**, not on the side on which it is applied.

### Why SC test on HV side?
1. HV side has **lower rated current** → full-load current can be achieved at a much smaller current magnitude, which is more economical for instrument selection.
2. The required short-circuit voltage is very small (5–10 %), so applying it to the HV side means the absolute voltmeter reading is larger and more accurate than if applied to LV.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### OC Test Procedure
1. Keep HV terminals **open**.
2. Apply **rated line voltage** *V*₀ to LV terminals through an autotransformer (variac) to ramp up smoothly.
3. Connect **two wattmeters (W₁, W₂)**, an **ammeter (A)**, and a **voltmeter (V)**.
4. Record *V*₀, *I*₀, *W*₁, *W*₂.
5. Total no-load power: *P*₀ = *W*₁ + *W*₂.

### SC Test Procedure
1. **Short-circuit the LV terminals** with a thick copper link of negligible resistance.
2. Apply a **small reduced voltage** through variac to HV terminals.
3. **Increase voltage gradually** until the ammeter reads **full-load current** *I*sc = *I*fl.
4. Record *V*sc, *I*sc, *W*₁, *W*₂.
5. Total short-circuit power: *P*sc = *W*₁ + *W*₂.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities are **line values** unless otherwise stated. For per-phase values, use *V*ph and *I*ph per the connection.

| Sl. | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|-----|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_0\,I_0}$ | Used in OC test |
| 2 | Short-circuit power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{sc}\,I_{sc}}$ | Used in SC test |
| 3 | Working (core-loss) component | $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | In phase with $V_0$ |
| 4 | Magnetising component | $I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Quadrature with $V_0$ |
| 5 | Core-loss resistance (per phase) | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{ph}^{2}}{P_{ph}}$ | Star: $R_c = V_0^{2}/P_0$ ; Delta: $R_c = 3V_0^{2}/P_0$ |
| 6 | Magnetising reactance (per phase) | $X_m = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{I_{\mu(ph)}}$ | Star: $X_m = V_0/(\sqrt{3}\,I_\mu)$ ; Delta: $X_m = V_0/I_\mu$ |
| 7 | Equivalent impedance (per phase) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc(ph)}}{I_{sc(ph)}}$ | Referred to test side |
| 8 | Equivalent resistance (per phase) | $R_{01} = Z_{01}\cos\phi_{sc}$ | |
| 9 | Equivalent reactance (per phase) | $X_{01} = Z_{01}\sin\phi_{sc}$ | |
| 10 | Per-unit impedance | $\%Z = \dfrac{V_{sc} \text{ at full-load current}}{V_{rated}}\times 100$ | |
| 11 | Efficiency at load fraction $x$, PF $\cos\phi$ | $\eta = \dfrac{x\,S\cos\phi}{x\,S\cos\phi + P_i + x^{2}P_{cu}}$ | $P_i$ = iron loss, $P_{cu}$ = FL copper loss |
| 12 | Voltage regulation (approximate) | $\text{VR}\% = \dfrac{I(R\cos\phi \pm X\sin\phi)}{V}\times 100$ | $+$ lagging, $-$ leading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Referral rule:** To shift series parameters from primary to secondary (or vice-versa), divide by $K^{2}$ where $K = N_1/N_2 = V_1/V_2$ (line-to-line for the same connection type).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer manufacturing industry:** Every transformer manufactured in factories undergoes OC and SC tests as a **routine quality control check** (per IS 2026 / IEC 60076 standards).
- **Power utilities:** Used for **field acceptance testing** before commissioning a transformer.
- **Design verification:** %Z obtained from SC test determines **short-circuit level** and **parallel operation compatibility**.
- **Loss capitalisation:** Iron and copper losses are **billable** in some power-purchase agreements; these tests provide the official loss figures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit Mapping

The per-phase equivalent circuit of a transformer referred to the primary is:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= I_1 R_{01} + j I_1 X_{01} + E_1 \\
E_1 &= I_0\left(\dfrac{jR_c X_m}{R_c + jX_m}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

The shunt branch (parallel combination of *R*ₒc and *jX*ₘ) is determined entirely from the **OC test**, while the series branch (*R*₀₁, *X*₀₁) is determined entirely from the **SC test**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem Statement (Module Experiment 2 — Typical KTU Numerical):**
> A 3-phase, **50 Hz, 10 kVA, 500/250 V, star-star transformer** gave the following test results:
> - **OC test on LV side:** *V*₀ = 250 V (line), *I*₀ = 2.5 A (line), *P*₀ = 250 W
> - **SC test on HV side:** *V*sc = 25 V (line), *I*sc = 11.55 A (line), *P*sc = 400 W
>
> Calculate: (i) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to the LV side. (ii) Efficiency at full load 0.8 PF lagging. (iii) Voltage regulation at full load 0.8 PF lagging.

### Step A — Sanity Check the Full-Load Currents

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{FL(HV)} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{HV}} = \frac{10\,000}{\sqrt{3}\times 500} = 11.55 \text{ A} \quad \checkmark \\
I_{FL(LV)} &= \frac{S}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{LV}} = \frac{10\,000}{\sqrt{3}\times 250} = 23.09 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

The SC test current (11.55 A) matches the HV-side full-load current, confirming the SC test was performed at full load.

### Step B — OC Test: Compute No-Load Power Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_0 &= \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3}\,V_0\,I_0} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{3}\times 250\times 2.5} \\
\cos\phi_0 &= \frac{250}{1082.53} = 0.2309
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $\sin\phi_0 = \sqrt{1-0.2309^{2}} = 0.9730$.

### Step C — OC Test: Working and Magnetising Components

$$\begin{aligned}
I_w &= I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 2.5 \times 0.2309 = 0.5773 \text{ A} \\
I_\mu &= I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 2.5 \times 0.9730 = 2.4325 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step D — OC Test: Per-Phase Shunt Parameters (Star Connection)

For star: *V*ph = *V*L/√3 ; *I*ph = *I*L ; *P*ph = *P*L/3.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{ph} &= \frac{V_0}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{250}{\sqrt{3}} = 144.34 \text{ V} \\
P_{ph} &= \frac{P_0}{3} = \frac{250}{3} = 83.33 \text{ W} \\
R_c &= \frac{V_{ph}^{2}}{P_{ph}} = \frac{(144.34)^{2}}{83.33} = \frac{20\,834}{83.33} = 250 \; \Omega \\
X_m &= \frac{V_{ph}}{I_\mu} = \frac{144.34}{2.4325} = 59.34 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

### Step E — SC Test: Compute Short-Circuit Power Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_{sc} &= \frac{P_{sc}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_{sc}\,I_{sc}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}\times 25\times 11.55} \\
\cos\phi_{sc} &= \frac{400}{500.10} = 0.7998
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $\sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{1-0.7998^{2}} = 0.6003$.

### Step F — SC Test: Per-Phase Series Parameters (HV Side, Star)

For star HV: *V*sc(ph) = *V*sc/√3 ; *I*sc(ph) = *I*sc.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{sc(ph)} &= \frac{25}{\sqrt{3}} = 14.434 \text{ V} \\
Z_{01} &= \frac{V_{sc(ph)}}{I_{sc(ph)}} = \frac{14.434}{11.55} = 1.2497 \approx 1.25 \; \Omega \\
R_{01} &= Z_{01}\cos\phi_{sc} = 1.25 \times 0.7998 = 0.9997 \approx 1.0 \; \Omega \\
X_{01} &= Z_{01}\sin\phi_{sc} = 1.25 \times 0.6003 = 0.7504 \approx 0.75 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

### Step G — Refer Series Parameters to LV Side

The turns ratio (line-to-line) is $K = 500/250 = 2$; therefore $K^{2} = 4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{02} &= \frac{R_{01}}{K^{2}} = \frac{1.0}{4} = 0.25 \; \Omega \\
X_{02} &= \frac{X_{01}}{K^{2}} = \frac{0.75}{4} = 0.1875 \; \Omega \\
Z_{02} &= \frac{Z_{01}}{K^{2}} = \frac{1.25}{4} = 0.3125 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

### Step H — Full-Load Efficiency at 0.8 PF Lagging

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &= S\cos\phi = 10\,000 \times 0.8 = 8\,000 \text{ W} \\
\text{Iron loss} \; P_i &= P_0 = 250 \text{ W} \\
\text{FL copper loss} \; P_{cu} &= P_{sc} = 400 \text{ W} \\
\text{Total loss} &= P_i + P_{cu} = 250 + 400 = 650 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Output} + \text{Losses}} = \frac{8\,000}{8\,000 + 650} = \frac{8\,000}{8\,650} \\
\eta &= 0.9249 = 92.49\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step I — Voltage Regulation at Full Load 0.8 PF Lagging (LV Side Reference)

Using the approximate (magnitude) VR formula on the LV side with $\cos\phi = 0.8$ and $\sin\phi = 0.6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VR} &= \frac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi + X_{02}\sin\phi)}{V_{2(ph)}} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{23.09 \times (0.25 \times 0.8 + 0.1875 \times 0.6)}{144.34} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{23.09 \times (0.200 + 0.1125)}{144.34} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{23.09 \times 0.3125}{144.34} \times 100 = \frac{7.216}{144.34} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= 5.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step J — Cross-Verification Using HV Side

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VR} &= \frac{I_1(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_{1(ph)}} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{11.55 \times (1.0 \times 0.8 + 0.75 \times 0.6)}{288.68} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{11.55 \times 1.25}{288.68} \times 100 = \frac{14.44}{288.68} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= 5.0\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Star vs. Delta Reference

> [!NOTE]
> Same numerical *line* readings, **different per-phase** parameters. The choice must match the physical winding connection.

| Quantity | Star Connection | Delta Connection |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| $V_{ph}$ | $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ | $V_L$ |
| $I_{ph}$ | $I_L$ | $I_L/\sqrt{3}$ |
| $R_c$ (per phase) | $V_0^{2}/P_0$ | $3V_0^{2}/P_0$ |
| $X_m$ (per phase) | $V_0/(\sqrt{3}\,I_\mu)$ | $V_0/I_\mu$ |
| $Z_{01}$ (per phase) | $V_{sc}/(\sqrt{3}\,I_{sc})$ | $V_{sc}\sqrt{3}/I_{sc}$ |
| Power formula (universal) | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Open-Circuit Test

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUP[3 Phase AC Supply LV Rated Frequency] --> VAR[Variac Autotransformer]
    VAR --> AM[Ammeter A Line Current]
    AM --> W1C[W1 Current Coil In Phase R]
    W1C --> W2C[W2 Current Coil In Phase B]
    W2C --> LV[LV Side Winding Terminals]
    VM[Voltmeter V Line Voltage] -. Parallel Across LV .-> LV
    W1P[W1 Pressure Coil] -. Measures VRY .-> W1C
    W2P[W2 Pressure Coil] -. Measures VBY .-> W2C
    LV -. HV Side Open Circuited .-> HVOP[HV Side Open No Load]
    style SUP fill:#D6E9F5
    style HVOP fill:#FFD6D6
    style AM fill:#FFF2CC
    style VM fill:#FFF2CC
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Short-Circuit Test

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUP[3 Phase Variac Reduced Voltage 5 To 10 Percent] --> AM[Ammeter A Line Current]
    AM --> W1C[W1 Current Coil In Phase R]
    W1C --> W2C[W2 Current Coil In Phase B]
    W2C --> HV[HV Side Winding Terminals]
    VM[Voltmeter V Short Circuit Voltage] -. Parallel Across HV .-> HV
    W1P[W1 Pressure Coil] -. Measures VRY .-> W1C
    W2P[W2 Pressure Coil] -. Measures VBY .-> W2C
    HV -- Thick Copper Shorting Link --> LVSC[LV Side Winding Short Circuited]
    style SUP fill:#D6E9F5
    style LVSC fill:#FFD6D6
    style AM fill:#FFF2CC
    style VM fill:#FFF2CC
```

## 4.3 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit With Identified Parameters

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1[V1 Applied Primary] --> RO1[R01 Equivalent Winding Resistance]
    RO1 --> XO1[X01 Equivalent Leakage Reactance]
    XO1 --> E1[E1 Induced EMF]
    E1 --> V2[V2 Secondary Terminal]
    RC[Rc Core Loss Resistance] -. Shunt Branch .-> E1
    XM[Xm Magnetising Reactance] -. Shunt Branch .-> E1
    style RO1 fill:#FCE4D6
    style XO1 fill:#FCE4D6
    style RC fill:#E2EFDA
    style XM fill:#E2EFDA
```

## 4.4 Phasor Diagram — No-Load Current Components

```mermaid
graph LR
    V0[V0 Reference Phasor Horizontal] --> IWTXT[Iw In Phase With V0 Core Loss Component]
    V0 --> IMTXT[Imu Lags V0 By 90 Deg Magnetising Component]
    IWTXT --> IO[I0 Resultant No Load Current]
    IMTXT --> IO
    style V0 fill:#D6E9F5
    style IWTXT fill:#FFF2CC
    style IMTXT fill:#FFE4E1
    style IO fill:#E2EFDA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**List the instruments used in the OC and SC tests on a 3-phase transformer. Justify the use of the two-wattmeter method in both tests.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Instruments used** *(1 mark)*: One voltmeter, one ammeter, and **two wattmeters** connected using the **two-wattmeter method**.
- **Justification for two-wattmeters** *(2 marks)*: A 3-phase, 3-wire system has no neutral; hence power in individual phases cannot be measured directly. The two-wattmeter method uses the line currents and line-to-line voltages to determine the total 3-phase active power as **P = W₁ + W₂**, irrespective of the connection (Y or Δ) or load balance. This method is universally applicable and economical (only two wattmeters instead of three).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**The no-load power factor of a transformer is usually very low (around 0.1 to 0.3). Justify. Also, state the role of the magnetising branch in the equivalent circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Justification for low no-load PF** *(2 marks)*: The no-load current consists predominantly of the magnetising component *I*μ (90° lagging the voltage), with a small in-phase component *I*w representing core loss. Since *I*μ ≫ *I*w, the no-load current lags the voltage by a large angle (close to 90°), giving a very low power factor (typically 0.1–0.3).
- **Role of magnetising branch** *(1 mark)*: The magnetising branch (parallel combination of *R*ₒc and *X*ₘ) models the **core loss** and the **magnetising MMF** required to establish the working flux in the core, and is responsible for drawing the no-load current from the supply.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 marks]
**With neat circuit diagrams, explain the procedure to perform the open circuit and short circuit tests on a 3-phase transformer. Mention the side on which each test is conducted, the quantities measured, and the parameters determined.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Open Circuit Test:*
1. **Connection** *(1 mark)*: The test is performed on the **LV side**, with the **HV side kept open**. Rated voltage at rated frequency is applied to the LV terminals through a variac to enable smooth voltage ramping.
2. **Instruments** *(1 mark)*: One voltmeter (across LV terminals), one ammeter (in series with one line), and two wattmeters (in two-wattmeter configuration).
3. **Procedure** *(1 mark)*: Increase the variac voltage gradually to rated LV voltage. Record *V*₀, *I*₀, *W*₁, *W*₂. Total no-load power: *P*₀ = *W*₁ + *W*₂.
4. **Parameters determined** *(1 mark)*: Iron loss *P*₀, no-load PF cos φ₀, working component *I*w, magnetising component *I*μ, and per-phase *R*ₒc and *X*ₘ.

*Short Circuit Test:*
5. **Connection** *(1 mark)*: The test is performed on the **HV side**, with the **LV side short-circuited** by a thick copper link of negligible resistance. A reduced voltage is applied through a variac.
6. **Instruments** *(1 mark)*: Same as OC test (V, A, W₁, W₂ in two-wattmeter configuration).
7. **Procedure and parameters** *(1 mark)*: Increase variac voltage gradually until the ammeter reads **full-load current**. Record *V*sc, *I*sc, *W*₁, *W*₂. From *P*sc = *W*₁ + *W*₂, determine per-phase *Z*₀₁, *R*₀₁, *X*₀₁, and the equivalent impedance referred to the test side.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 10 kVA, 500/250 V, star-star transformer gave the following test results:**
- **OC test (LV side):** 250 V, 2.5 A, 250 W
- **SC test (HV side):** 25 V, 11.55 A, 400 W

**Calculate (i) the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the LV side, (ii) the efficiency at full load 0.8 PF lagging, and (iii) the voltage regulation at full load 0.8 PF lagging.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Refer to the exhaustive worked example in Section 3 above.*

**(i) Equivalent circuit parameters referred to LV side** *(3 marks)*:
- *R*ₒc = **250 Ω**, *X*ₘ = **59.34 Ω** (from OC test)
- *R*₀₂ = **0.25 Ω**, *X*₀₂ = **0.1875 Ω** (SC test values referred from HV using K² = 4)

**(ii) Full-load efficiency at 0.8 PF lagging** *(2 marks)*:
- Output = 8 kW ; Total losses = 0.65 kW
- **η = 92.49 %**

**(iii) Voltage regulation at full load 0.8 PF lagging** *(2 marks)*:
- VR = (*I*₂*R*₀₂ cos φ + *I*₂*X*₀₂ sin φ) / *V*₂(ph)
- **VR = 5.0 %**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Explain with the help of phasor diagrams how the working component *I*w and the magnetising component *I*μ of the no-load current are obtained from the OC test readings. Why is the no-load current typically only 2 – 5 % of the full-load current in a well-designed transformer?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

*Phasor diagram and components* *(4 marks)*:
1. Take the applied voltage *V*₀ as the reference phasor along the positive x-axis.
2. The total no-load current *I*₀ lags *V*₀ by an angle φ₀, where cos φ₀ = *P*₀ / (√3 *V*₀ *I*₀).
3. Resolve *I*₀ into two perpendicular components:
   - **Working component** *I*w = *I*₀ cos φ₀ (along *V*₀) → represents the **core loss** component.
   - **Magnetising component** *I*μ = *I*₀ sin φ₀ (lagging *V*₀ by 90°) → represents the **magnetising flux** component.
4. The triangle with sides *I*w, *I*μ, and *I*₀ is the **no-load power triangle**, from which cos φ₀ is obtained.

*Why I₀ is only 2 – 5 % of FL current* *(3 marks)*:
1. The core is made of high-grade **silicon steel laminations** with high permeability, requiring only a small magnetising MMF to establish rated flux.
2. The lamination thickness is small (0.3 – 0.5 mm) with insulation between layers, keeping **eddy-current losses low**, hence *I*w (core-loss component) is also small.
3. The flux density is kept **below the saturation knee** (≈ 1.5 – 1.7 T), so even moderate MMF can produce rated flux with low magnetising current.
4. Hence the resultant *I*₀ = √(*I*w² + *I*μ²) is small, typically 2 – 5 % of the rated current.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 50 kVA, 3300/330 V, star-star transformer is tested as follows:**
- **OC test (LV side):** 330 V, 4.5 A, 500 W
- **SC test (HV side):** 100 V, 8.75 A, 1200 W

**Calculate (i) the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side, (ii) full-load efficiency at UPF, and (iii) voltage regulation at 0.707 PF lagging.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) OC test parameters (per phase, star LV)** *(2 marks)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_0 &= \frac{500}{\sqrt{3}\times 330\times 4.5} = \frac{500}{2571.5} = 0.1944 \\
I_\mu &= 4.5 \times \sin\phi_0 = 4.5 \times 0.9809 = 4.414 \text{ A} \\
R_c &= \frac{V_0^{2}}{P_0} = \frac{330^{2}}{500} = 217.8 \; \Omega \; \text{(star)} \\
X_m &= \frac{V_0}{\sqrt{3}\,I_\mu} = \frac{330}{\sqrt{3}\times 4.414} = 43.16 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

Referred to HV side with $K = 3300/330 = 10$ and $K^{2} = 100$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_c' &= 217.8 \times 100 = 21\,780 \; \Omega \\
X_m' &= 43.16 \times 100 = 4\,316 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**SC test parameters (per phase, star HV)** *(2 marks)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_{sc} &= \frac{1200}{\sqrt{3}\times 100\times 8.75} = 0.7918 \\
Z_{01} &= \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}\times 8.75} = 6.6 \; \Omega \\
R_{01} &= 6.6 \times 0.7918 = 5.226 \; \Omega \\
X_{01} &= 6.6 \times 0.6108 = 4.031 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Efficiency at full load, UPF** *(1.5 marks)*:
- Output = 50 kW ; Iron loss = 0.5 kW ; Cu loss = 1.2 kW
- **η = 50 / (50 + 0.5 + 1.2) = 96.71 %**

**(iii) Voltage regulation at 0.707 PF lagging** *(1.5 marks)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VR} &= \frac{8.75(5.226 \times 0.707 + 4.031 \times 0.707)}{1905.3} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &= \frac{8.75 \times 6.540 \times 0.707}{1905.3} \times 100 = \frac{40.46}{1905.3} \times 100 \\
\text{VR} &\approx 2.12\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Using line quantities directly as per-phase values** — KTU evaluators strictly deduct 1–2 marks if star/delta conversion (*V*ph vs. *V*L) is missed. Always state the connection explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to refer SC-test parameters** — Parameters from the SC test are referred to the side on which the test was performed. To use them in efficiency/regulation calculations on the other side, dividing by *K*² is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Sign convention in VR formula** — Use **'+' for lagging** PF and **'−' for leading** PF. The approximate formula gives sufficiently accurate results (within 0.5 % of the exact phasor method) and is what KTU expects.
> 4. **Confusing *P*₀ with total iron loss** — *P*₀ is the total 3-phase iron loss, but to compute *R*ₒc per phase, divide it by 3 (or use *V*ph²/*P*ph).
> 5. **Using three-wattmeter method for 3-wire system** — Not applicable; always use the **two-wattmeter method**.
> 6. **Units and rounding** — KTU expects answers to **3 significant figures** minimum; mismatched units (kVA vs W, V_L vs V_ph) is a common 0.5-mark deduction.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OC test** → determines **iron loss** and **shunt branch** (*R*ₒc, *X*ₘ). Conducted on **LV side**, HV open.
- **SC test** → determines **full-load copper loss** and **series branch** (*R*₀₁, *X*₀₁). Conducted on **HV side**, LV shorted.
- **Two-wattmeter method** is the **universal** method for 3-phase power measurement (Y or Δ, balanced or unbalanced).
- **Per-phase vs. line quantities** depend on the **winding connection**:
  - Star → *V*ph = *V*L/√3 ; *I*ph = *I*L
  - Delta → *V*ph = *V*L ; *I*ph = *I*L/√3
- **Referral of parameters** between primary and secondary → divide by $K^{2}$ where *K* is the turns ratio (line-to-line for same connection).
- **Power factor** in both tests is always computed using the **2-wattmeter formula**: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$.
- **Efficiency** at any load fraction *x* and power factor cos φ:
  $$\eta = \frac{xS\cos\phi}{xS\cos\phi + P_i + x^{2}P_{cu}}$$
- **Voltage regulation** approximate formula (good for KTU):
  $$\text{VR}\% = \frac{I(R\cos\phi \pm X\sin\phi)}{V}\times 100$$
- **%Z = Vsc% at full-load current** is the standard short-circuit voltage rating printed on the transformer nameplate.
- **No-load current** is only 2–5 % of FL current due to high-permeability silicon-steel core, thin laminations, and operation below saturation.
- The **SC test voltage** required to circulate FL current is typically **5 % – 12 %** of rated voltage.
- **Star-Star transformer** SC test on HV gives per-phase *R*₀₁, *X*₀₁ directly using line values divided by √3 for *V*sc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scott Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL407 - DC MACHINES & TRANSFORMERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Scott Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scott Connection (Scott-T Connection):** A specialized method of interconnecting **two single-phase transformers** to either convert a **three-phase (3-φ) supply into a two-phase (2-φ) supply** or vice versa. The connection employs a **Main Transformer** (primary wound for the full line voltage of the 3-φ side) and a **Teaser Transformer** (primary tapped at **50% of the main transformer's primary turns** and connected to the **neutral point** created at the center tap of the main transformer).

The Scott Connection was patented by Charles F. Scott in the 1890s and remains the textbook standard for polyphase phase conversion in legacy traction systems, furnace supplies, and symmetrical component analysis laboratories.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it as a "Phase Translator" or "Electrical Translator Device"**

Imagine you have a **three-lane highway** (3-φ supply) merging into a **two-lane road** (2-φ load) through a **toll plaza** (transformer bank). You cannot simply re-stripe the road — you need a clever routing system. The **Main Transformer** handles one full lane of traffic directly, while the **Teaser Transformer** acts as an **auxiliary ramp** that takes the remaining phase combined with a "neutral split" to synthesize the second phase. Together, they ensure the traffic flows smoothly and the loads remain balanced without any lane being overcrowded.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Turns Ratio (Main)** | $N_2/N_1 = a$ | Step-down/step-up ratio for main transformer |
| **Teaser Tap Position** | **0.5 : 1** (i.e., 50% tap) | Tapped at the midpoint of main primary |
| **Tapping Point (Neutral)** | **Center of Main Primary** | Creates artificial neutral `N` |
| **Frequency** | **50 Hz (India Standard)** | Constant for design |
| **Phase Displacement (Output)** | **90° (Quadrature)** | Two phases are electrically in quadrature |

## 1.4 Why Scott Connection is Used in KTU Labs

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme **PCEEL407 lab** includes the Scott Connection experiment to teach students:
> - Conversion of 3-φ to 2-φ supply using **two single-phase transformers**.
> - Verification of the **turns ratio** of the teaser transformer ($1 : \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} : 1$).
> - Loading characteristics of the two-phase side with balanced and unbalanced loads.
> - Understanding of **symmetrical components** and **quadrature phase synthesis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and the "How"

The Scott Connection operates on the principle of **vector decomposition** of a 3-phase system into two orthogonal (90° apart) 2-phase components. Let us break down the operational logic step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Main Transformer (M):** Its primary is connected across two line terminals of the 3-phase supply (say R and Y). The **center tap** of this primary winding is brought out as a common terminal `N` (the artificial neutral).
- **Step 2 — Teaser Transformer (T):** Its primary is connected between the third line terminal (B) of the 3-phase supply and the artificial neutral `N` (center tap of main primary). The teaser primary has a **turn ratio of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$** compared to the main.
- **Step 3 — Phase Synthesis:** The secondaries of both transformers deliver two voltages that are **displaced by 90°** (in electrical quadrature), producing a balanced **2-phase, 4-wire** or **2-phase, 3-wire** system.
- **Step 4 — Loading:** The 2-phase loads are connected across the two secondary windings, with a common return wire to the inter-connected secondaries.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derivable** and align with standard electrical machinery texts used in the KTU 2024 B.Tech EEE curriculum.

| Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Use Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Teaser Primary Turns Ratio | $N_T = \frac{N_M}{2}$ | $N_T$ = teaser turns, $N_M$ = main turns | Tapped at 50% of main primary |
| Teaser Voltage (Primary) | $V_T = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$ | $V_L$ = 3-φ line voltage | Voltage across teaser primary |
| Main Voltage (Primary) | $V_M = V_L$ | $V_L$ = line-to-line voltage | Voltage across main primary |
| Phase Displacement (Output) | $\theta = 90°$ | Output 2-φ voltage quadrature | True balanced 2-phase |
| Turns Ratio (Teaser Tapping) | $N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = 1 : \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} : 1$ | Three-winding equivalent | Used in phasor construction |
| Power Transferred (Balanced) | $P = 2 \cdot V_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi$ | $V_2, I_2$ = 2-φ secondary quantities | Balanced 2-φ loading |
| Kapp Voltage Regulation | $V.R. = \frac{I \cdot R \cos\phi \pm I \cdot X \sin\phi}{V}$ | Approximate regulation | Standard transformer V.R. |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Output Power} + \text{Losses}}$ | Percentage efficiency | At unity / lagging P.F. |

## 2.3 Critical Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications of Scott Connection:**
> 1. **Electric Traction Systems** — Legacy 25 kV AC traction in Indian Railways historically used Scott-connected transformers at substations to feed 2-phase overhead contact systems.
> 2. **Blast Furnace & Arc Furnaces** — Symmetrical 2-phase heating with Scott connections ensures uniform heat distribution.
> 3. **Symmetrical Component Studies** — Lab demonstration of how a 2-phase system can be derived from 3-phase supplies.
> 4. **Phase Converters** — Industrial sites that require 2-phase motors from a 3-phase grid.
> 5. **Wind Energy Conversion** — Some legacy 2-phase generators were coupled to the grid via Scott connections.

## 2.4 The Phasor Geometry (Conceptual Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a balanced 3-phase system, the three line voltages are mutually displaced by **120°**. The Scott connection **decomposes** this 3-phase set into a **2-phase orthogonal set** by exploiting the **midpoint of one line voltage** and the **perpendicular bisector** concept of phasor geometry.

The teaser transformer primary is connected such that its induced EMF is **$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$** times the line voltage and lags/ leads the main secondary voltage by **exactly 90°** — the foundation of all derivations that follow in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Turns Ratio Between Main and Teaser Transformers

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the teaser transformer primary turns must be **$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$** times the main transformer primary turns for balanced 2-phase operation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the 3-phase line-to-line voltages be:

$$V_{RY}, \ V_{YB}, \ V_{BR}$$

Each of magnitude $V_L$ and mutually displaced by **120°**.

**Step 1: Identify the Main Transformer Primary Voltage**

The main transformer primary is connected across R-Y. Therefore:

$$V_{Main} = V_{RY} = V_L \angle 0°$$

This voltage is taken as the **reference phasor**.

**Step 2: Locate the Center Tap (Artificial Neutral)**

The center tap `N` divides $V_{RY}$ into two equal halves:

$$V_{RN} = V_{NY} = \frac{V_L}{2}$$

These two half-voltages are **in phase** with $V_{RY}$ and are displaced from each other by **0°** (collinear).

**Step 3: Identify the Teaser Transformer Primary Voltage**

The teaser primary is connected between the third line (B) and the neutral `N`. Its voltage is the **phasor sum**:

$$V_{Teaser} = V_{BN} = V_{BR} + V_{RN}$$

Expressing phasors in rectangular form:
- $V_{BR} = V_L \angle 120° = V_L \left( -\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$
- $V_{RN} = \frac{V_L}{2} \angle 0° = \frac{V_L}{2} (1 + j0) = \frac{V_L}{2}$

**Step 4: Compute the Phasor Sum**

$$V_{BN} = V_L \left( -\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) + \frac{V_L}{2}$$

$$V_{BN} = V_L \left( -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$

$$V_{BN} = j \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$$

$$V_{BN} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L \angle 90°$$

**Step 5: Compare Magnitudes and Phase**

The magnitude of the teaser primary voltage is:

$$\vert V_{Teaser} \vert = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$$

The phase angle is **+90°** with respect to the main primary voltage. Since the main is at $0°$, the teaser is at **$90°$** — exactly the **quadrature** displacement required for 2-phase operation.

**Step 6: Establish the Turns Ratio**

Since the transformers operate on the same frequency, the flux in both cores is the same when they are properly designed. The induced EMF is proportional to turns:

$$\frac{N_{Teaser}}{N_{Main}} = \frac{V_{Teaser}}{V_{Main}} = \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L}{V_L}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{N_{Teaser}}{N_{Main}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866}$$

This is the **fundamental turns ratio** of the Scott connection, and it is the most heavily tested formula in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Balance Between 3-φ Input and 2-φ Output

**Problem Statement:** Show that a Scott-connected transformer bank transfers **balanced 3-φ power** to a **balanced 2-φ load** without any neutral current flow on the primary side.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Assume a Balanced 2-φ Load**

Let the secondary load currents be $I_2$ (main) and $I_2$ (teaser), both at the same magnitude and **displaced by 90°**:

$$I_{2,Main} = I_2 \angle -\phi$$
$$I_{2,Teaser} = I_2 \angle (90° - \phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the load power factor angle.

**Step 2: Reflect the Load Currents to the Primary**

Using the turns ratio $a = N_2/N_1$ for the main and $a' = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a$ for the teaser:

$$I_{1,Main} = \frac{I_{2,Main}}{a}, \quad I_{1,Teaser} = \frac{I_{2,Teaser}}{a'}$$

**Step 3: Compute the 3-φ Input Line Currents**

The three line currents at the 3-φ input are:

$$I_R = I_{1,Main} \angle 0° + I_{1,Teaser} \angle -60°$$

$$I_Y = I_{1,Main} \angle 180° + I_{1,Teaser} \angle -150°$$

$$I_B = I_{1,Teaser} \angle 90°$$

**Step 4: Verify Magnitude Equality**

Substituting the reflected currents and simplifying, one obtains:

$$\vert I_R \vert = \vert I_Y \vert = \vert I_B \vert = \frac{I_2}{a} = I_{line}$$

All three line currents are **equal in magnitude** and mutually displaced by **120°**, confirming that the 3-φ supply remains balanced.

**Step 5: Power Balance Check**

Input power (3-φ side):

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_{line} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Output power (2-φ side):

$$P_{out} = 2 \cdot V_{2\phi} \cdot I_{2\phi} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Since the transformer bank is ideal (no losses), $P_{in} = P_{out}$, and the power transfer is **complete and balanced**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Phasor Diagram Construction (Analytical Matrix)

The complete phasor diagram can be tabulated for clarity:

| Phasor | Magnitude | Angle (with respect to $V_{RY}$) | Location |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{RY}$ | $V_L$ | $0°$ | Main Primary |
| $V_{YB}$ | $V_L$ | $-120°$ | Between Y and B |
| $V_{BR}$ | $V_L$ | $+120°$ | Between B and R |
| $V_{RN}$ | $V_L/2$ | $0°$ | Half of Main Primary |
| $V_{NY}$ | $V_L/2$ | $0°$ | Half of Main Primary |
| $V_{BN}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$ | $+90°$ | Teaser Primary |
| $V_{Main,sec}$ | $a V_L$ | $0°$ | Main Secondary |
| $V_{Teaser,sec}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a V_L$ | $+90°$ | Teaser Secondary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 90° displacement is the **defining characteristic** of the Scott connection. All other derivations (efficiency, regulation, Kapp's test) are **secondary** to this primary geometric truth.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Scott-connected bank of two single-phase transformers converts 6.6 kV, 3-φ, 50 Hz to 440 V, 2-φ. The secondary load is **100 kVA** at **0.8 power factor lagging**. Calculate:
1. The line currents on the 3-φ side.
2. The turns ratio of the teaser transformer.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Output Power and Per-Phase Current**

Total 2-φ output power:

$$P_{out} = 100 \text{ kVA}, \quad V_{2\phi} = 440 \text{ V}$$

Per-phase current on 2-φ side:

$$I_{2\phi} = \frac{100{,}000}{2 \times 440} = 113.64 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Turns Ratio of the Main Transformer**

$$a = \frac{V_{1\phi}}{V_{2\phi}} = \frac{6600}{440} = 15$$

**Step 3: Turns Ratio of the Teaser Transformer**

$$a_{Teaser} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times a = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 15$$

$$a_{Teaser} = 0.866 \times 15 = 12.99 \approx 13$$

**Step 4: 3-φ Side Line Current**

$$I_{line} = \frac{I_{2\phi}}{a} = \frac{113.64}{15} = 7.576 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Verification by Power Balance**

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 6600 \times 7.576 \times 0.8 = 69{,}294 \text{ W} \approx 69.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{out} = 100{,}000 \times 0.8 = 80{,}000 \text{ W} = 80 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The difference (10.7 kW) is due to **transformer losses** (iron + copper), which are neglected in the ideal analysis. In a lab experiment, you would measure both values to compute **efficiency**.

---

## 3.5 KTU Examiner's Pattern: Common Numerical Traps

| Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
| :--- | :--- |
| Using $V_L / 2$ for teaser voltage | Use $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$ |
| Forgetting to tap main at center | Always identify the **midpoint `N`** explicitly |
| Confusing primary and secondary angles | Angles are preserved (or inverted by 180°) on reflection |
| Adding powers arithmetically | Use **$P = 2 V_2 I_2 \cos\phi$** for 2-φ, **$P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$** for 3-φ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are purely **alphanumeric** (e.g., `n1`, `n2`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone nodes. Labels contain only **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.2 Scott Connection — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[THREE PHASE 50 HZ SUPPLY R Y B]:::source
    n2[MAIN TRANSFORMER PRIMARY V LINE TO LINE]:::main
    n3[CENTER TAP NEUTRAL N AT 50 PERCENT]:::tap
    n4[TEASER TRANSFORMER PRIMARY V SQUARE ROOT 3 BY 2]:::teaser
    n5[MAIN SECONDARY V 2 PHASE]:::mainsec
    n6[TEASER SECONDARY V 2 PHASE QUADRATURE]:::teasersec
    n7[TWO PHASE LOAD Z 1]:::load
    n8[TWO PHASE LOAD Z 2]:::load
    n9[OUTPUT 2 PHASE 90 DEG QUADRATURE]:::output

    n1 -->|R Y TERMINALS| n2
    n2 -->|CENTER TAP OUTPUT| n3
    n3 -->|NEUTRAL CONNECTION| n4
    n1 -->|B PHASE TO NEUTRAL| n4
    n2 -->|INDUCTION COUPLING| n5
    n4 -->|INDUCTION COUPLING| n6
    n5 -->|PHASE 1 VOLTAGE| n7
    n6 -->|PHASE 2 VOLTAGE| n8
    n7 --> n9
    n8 --> n9

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tap fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef teaser fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mainsec fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef teasersec fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#00CED1,stroke:#008080,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Phasor Geometry Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1[V RY REFERENCE 0 DEG]:::phasor
    n2[V YB MINUS 120 DEG]:::phasor
    n3[V BR PLUS 120 DEG]:::phasor
    n4[V RN HALF 0 DEG]:::phasor
    n5[V NY HALF 0 DEG]:::phasor
    n6[V TEASER 90 DEG QUADRATURE]:::phasor
    n7[V MAIN SECONDARY 0 DEG]:::phasor
    n8[V TEASER SECONDARY 90 DEG]:::phasor

    n1 --> n4
    n1 --> n5
    n3 --> n6
    n4 --> n6
    n2 --> n3
    n6 --> n8
    n1 --> n7

    classDef phasor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 KTU Lab Wiring Topology (Block View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[3 PHASE MCB PANEL]:::panel
    n2[VOLTMETER V1 0 TO 750 V AC]:::meter
    n3[AMMETER A1 0 TO 10 A AC]:::meter
    n4[MAIN TRANSFORMER 1 KVA]:::mainxmer
    n5[TEASER TRANSFORMER 1 KVA]:::teaserxmer
    n6[RHEOSTAT LOAD PHASE 1]:::load
    n7[RHEOSTAT LOAD PHASE 2]:::load
    n8[MEASUREMENT PANEL 2 PHASE]:::panel
    n9[DATA ACQUISITION TABLE]:::data

    n1 --> n2
    n1 --> n3
    n2 --> n4
    n3 --> n4
    n4 -->|CENTER TAP| n5
    n1 --> n5
    n4 --> n6
    n5 --> n7
    n6 --> n8
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9

    classDef panel fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mainxmer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef teaserxmer fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram**: The 3-phase supply enters the panel, feeds the main transformer (R-Y) and teaser transformer (B-N). Outputs feed two rheostat loads. All measurements (voltage, current, power) are routed to the data table for analysis.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define Scott Connection. State its primary application.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Scott Connection** is a method of interconnecting two single-phase transformers to convert **3-phase supply to 2-phase supply** (or vice versa). It uses a **Main Transformer** connected across two lines of the 3-φ supply and a **Teaser Transformer** connected between the third line and the **center tap** of the main transformer's primary winding.
>
> **Primary Application:** Electric traction systems (e.g., legacy Indian Railways 25 kV AC traction substations) and laboratory demonstration of phase conversion for symmetrical component studies.
>
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - Definition — 1 Mark
> - Main + Teaser identification — 1 Mark
> - Application — 1 Mark

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

### Question 2: Why is the Teaser Transformer Primary Tapped at 50% of the Main Primary?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The teaser transformer primary is connected between the third phase line and the **artificial neutral** created at the center of the main primary. For the output 2-φ voltages to be **equal in magnitude** and **displaced by 90°**, the teaser primary voltage must equal $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$ where $V_L$ is the line voltage. This voltage is obtained by **tapping the main primary at 50%** (i.e., at the midpoint), which creates the neutral `N` such that $V_{BN} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L \angle 90°$.
>
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Phasor sum explanation — 2 Marks
> - 50% tap justification — 1 Mark

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Full Numerical on Scott Connection

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat phasor diagram, derive the expression for the **turns ratio of the teaser transformer** in a Scott connection. Prove that the teaser primary voltage is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ times the line voltage.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Scott-connected bank of two single-phase transformers is used to convert **11 kV, 3-φ, 50 Hz** to **440 V, 2-φ**. The 2-φ load is **150 kVA** at **0.85 power factor lagging**. Calculate:
   1. The line currents on the 3-φ side.
   2. The turns ratio of the teaser transformer.
   3. The total power input.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Draw the 3-φ phasor diagram with $V_{RY}$, $V_{YB}$, $V_{BR}$ at 0°, -120°, +120°.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Mark the center tap `N` on $V_{RY}$. Note that $V_{RN} = V_{NY} = V_L / 2$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Express $V_{BN}$ as the phasor sum:
$$V_{BN} = V_{BR} + V_{RN}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Substitute and simplify in rectangular form to obtain:
$$V_{BN} = j \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L \implies \vert V_{BN} \vert = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L, \quad \angle V_{BN} = 90°$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** State the conclusion: the turns ratio is:
$$\frac{N_{Teaser}}{N_{Main}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{L,3\phi} = 11000$ V, $V_{2\phi} = 440$ V, Load = 150 kVA, P.F. = 0.85 lagging.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute 2-φ per-phase current:
$$I_{2\phi} = \frac{150{,}000}{2 \times 440} = 170.45 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute main transformer turns ratio:
$$a = \frac{V_{1\phi}}{V_{2\phi}} = \frac{11{,}000}{440} = 25$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute teaser transformer turns ratio:
$$a_{Teaser} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 25 = 21.65 \approx 21.65$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute 3-φ line current (assuming ideal transformer):
$$I_{line} = \frac{I_{2\phi}}{a} = \frac{170.45}{25} = 6.818 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute total real power input:
$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 11{,}000 \times 6.818 \times 0.85 = 110{,}528 \text{ W} \approx 110.53 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Verification by 2-φ output:
$$P_{out} = 150{,}000 \times 0.85 = 127{,}500 \text{ W} = 127.5 \text{ kW}$$
The difference is the **transformer loss** (iron + copper) — to be measured in the lab experiment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Conceptual & Phasor Focus

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the constructional features of a Scott-connected transformer bank. Differentiate between the **Main Transformer** and the **Teaser Transformer** in terms of:
   1. Winding connections
   2. Voltage ratings
   3. Turns ratio
   4. Phasor position

**(b)** [7 Marks] Draw the complete phasor diagram of a Scott connection showing all primary and secondary voltages. Label the **90° phase displacement** and identify the **artificial neutral**. Also, state the role of the **teaser transformer** in achieving phase conversion.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Main Transformer (3.5 Marks):**
- Connected across **two line terminals** of the 3-φ supply (R-Y).
- Primary voltage = $V_L$ (full line voltage).
- Has a **center tap** brought out as the artificial neutral `N`.
- Turns ratio is **standard** ($N_1 : N_2$).
- Carries the **main phase** of the 2-φ output.

**Teaser Transformer (3.5 Marks):**
- Connected between the **third line** (B) and the **center tap `N`** of the main primary.
- Primary voltage = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$ — only 86.6% of the line voltage.
- Has **fewer turns** than the main ($N_T = 0.866 N_M$).
- Generates the **teaser phase** of the 2-φ output, displaced by **90°** from the main phase.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Phasor Diagram Elements (4 Marks):**
- Draw $V_{RY}$, $V_{YB}$, $V_{BR}$ at 0°, -120°, +120° (3-φ primary phasors).
- Mark the center tap `N` on $V_{RY}$, splitting it into $V_{RN}$ and $V_{NY}$ (each $V_L/2$).
- Draw $V_{BN}$ as the resultant of $V_{BR}$ and $V_{RN}$, pointing **upward at 90°**.
- Draw the secondary phasors $V_{M2}$ (along 0°) and $V_{T2}$ (along 90°), both of equal magnitude $a V_L$ for balanced operation.

**Identification (2 Marks):**
- The **artificial neutral `N`** is at the midpoint of $V_{RY}$.
- The **90° phase displacement** is the angle between $V_{M2}$ and $V_{T2}$ (the two 2-φ outputs).

**Role of Teaser Transformer (1 Mark):**
- It **synthesizes the second orthogonal phase** by combining the third line voltage with the artificial neutral potential, enabling conversion of 3-φ to 2-φ without any rotating machine.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Scott Connection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 50% center tap on the main primary** — The most common mistake. Students often tap the main at 1/3 or 2/3, which is for **Open-Delta (V-V) connection**, not Scott. Always tap at the **center (1/2)**.
> 2. **Using $V_L$ for teaser voltage** — The teaser voltage is **$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$**, NOT $V_L$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Confusing phasor addition rules** — Students must convert phasors to **rectangular form** before adding, then convert back to polar. Skipping this loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up 3-φ and 2-φ power formulas** — Use $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ and $P_{2\phi} = 2 V_{2\phi} I_{2\phi} \cos\phi$. Mixing them is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring the 90° phase shift in the phasor diagram** — A phasor diagram without the **90° angle** explicitly marked is considered **incomplete** and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not stating the practical application** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for mentioning **traction, furnace, or phase converter** as the application. Don't skip it!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scott Connection (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** Scott connection = 3-φ to 2-φ conversion using 2 single-phase transformers.
- **Main Transformer:** Connected across R-Y, has **center tap** for neutral `N`, primary voltage = $V_L$.
- **Teaser Transformer:** Connected between B and `N`, primary voltage = $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_L$, turns = $0.866 \times$ main turns.
- **Critical Ratio:** $\frac{N_{Teaser}}{N_{Main}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$ — **THE** most important formula.
- **Phase Displacement:** 90° (quadrature) between main and teaser secondaries.
- **Power Formulas:**
  - 3-φ side: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
  - 2-φ side: $P = 2 V_{2\phi} I_{2\phi} \cos\phi$
- **Artificial Neutral:** Created at the **midpoint of the main primary** — never at any other point.
- **No Neutral Current on Primary:** When 2-φ load is **balanced**, no current flows through the artificial neutral.
- **Applications:** Electric traction, arc furnaces, phase converters, symmetrical component labs.
- **KTU Lab Activity:** Measure primary/secondary voltages, verify the 90° phase shift using a **dual-trace oscilloscope** or **phase sequence indicator**, and plot **efficiency vs. load** curves.
- **Examiner's Favourite Questions:**
  - Derivation of $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ ratio (7 marks).
  - Phasor diagram with all labels (3 marks).
  - Numerical on line current and power (7 marks).
- **Common Pitfalls:** Wrong tap position, wrong teaser voltage, mixing power formulas, missing 90° angle.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for the Lab:** *"Main has the half-tap, Teaser takes the $\sqrt{3}/2$ gap!"* — remember this in the exam hall to instantly recall the core geometry.
